question: The rapid changes of $\mathrm{pH}$ near the stoichiometric point of an acid base titration is the basis of indicator detection. $\mathrm{pH}$ of the solution is related to ratio of the concentrations of the conjugate acid ${\text{(HIn)}}$ and base ( $\mathrm{In}^{-}$) forms of the indicator given by the expression
explanation:
Acid indicators are generally weak acids. The dissociation of indicator ${\text{ HIn}}$ takes place as follows:
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{HIn} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{In} \\& \therefore \quad \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{In}}=\dfrac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{In}^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{HIn}]} \\& \text { or }\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{In}} \dfrac{[\mathrm{HIn}]}{\left[\mathrm{In}^{-}\right]} \\& \because \quad \mathrm{pH}=-\log \left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right] \\& \therefore \quad \mathrm{pH}=-\log \left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{In}} \cdot \dfrac{[\mathrm{HIn}]}{\left[\mathrm{In}^{-}\right]}\right) \\& =-\log \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{In}}+\log \dfrac{\left[\mathrm{In}^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{HIn}]} \\& =\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{In}}+\log \dfrac{\left[\mathrm{In}^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{HIn}]}\end{aligned}$
or $\log \dfrac{\left[\mathrm{In}^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{HIn}]}=\mathrm{pH}-\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{In}}$

question:
Assertion: The $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}$ of a weak acid becomes equal to $\mathrm{pH}$ of the solution at the midpoint of its titration.


Reason: The molar concentrations of proton acceptor and proton donor become equal at the midpoint of titration of a weak acid.
explanation:
According to Henderson-Hasselbalch equation,
$\mathrm{pH}=\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}+\log \dfrac{\text { [proton acceptor }]}{\text { [proton donor] }}$
At midpoint of titration of weak acid,
$[\mathrm{HA}]=\left[\mathrm{A}^{-}\right]$and
hence, $\mathrm{pH}=\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}+\log 1.0=\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}$ So, option (1)
is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

The precipitate particles turn reddish pink due to complex formation between $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$and fluorescein anion.

question: ${{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}$ values for acids $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{3}, \mathrm{HNO}_{2}, \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$ and HCN are respectively $1.3 \times 10^{-2}, 4 \times 10^{-4}$, $1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ and $4 \times 10^{-10}$, which of the above acids produces stronger conjugate base in aqueous solution?
explanation:
Acidic strength $\propto \mathrm{Ka}$
The conjugate base of a weakest acid is strongest
$\therefore \mathrm{HCN}\left(\mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{a}}=4 \times 10^{-10}\right) \text { (least value) }$

question:
Observe the following species
(i) $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$
(ii) $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$
(iii) $\mathrm{SnCl}_{4}$
(iv) $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$
(v) $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$
(vi) $\mathrm{HSO}_{4}^{-}$
How many of the above species act as Lewis acids?
explanation:
Lewis acids are electron pair acceptors. Thus they are electron deficient. Out of given,
(ii) $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$, (iii) $\mathrm{SnCl}_{4}$, (iv) $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$, and (v) $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$are electron deficient species i.e., Lewis acids.
Thus, 4 species act as Lewis acid.

question: The correct decreasing order of the basic strength is
explanation:
Decreasing order of basicity is as follows $\mathrm{CH}_{3}^{-}>\mathrm{NH}_{2}^{-}>\mathrm{CN}^{-}>\mathrm{HS}^{-}$
To determine the basic strength of these anions we need to find out the acidic strength of the conjugate acids.
Conjugate acids of $\mathrm{CH}_{3}^{-}, \mathrm{NH}_{2}^{-}, \mathrm{CN}^{-}, \mathrm{HS}^{-}$are respectively $\mathrm{CH}_{4}, \mathrm{NH}_{3}, \mathrm{HCN}, \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}$.
As we know that, Acidity $\propto$ Electronegativity On going from left to right in the period, electronegativity increases.
So, acidity order is $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}>\mathrm{HCN}>\mathrm{NH}_{3}>\mathrm{CH}_{4}$. Hence, the basicity order of the given anions will be just reverse of the acidity order of the conjugate acids.

question:
Phosphoric acid ionizes in three steps with their ionization constant values $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{1}}, \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{a}_{2}}$ and $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{3}}$, respectively, while K is the overall ionization constant. Which of the following statements are true?

B. $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}$ is a stronger acid than $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{PO}_{4}^{-}$ and $\mathrm{HPO}_{4}^{2-}$
C. $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{1}}>\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{2}}>\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{3}}$
D. $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{1}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{3}}+\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{2}}}{2}$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
explanation:
(A) $\log \mathrm{K}=\log \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{1}}+\log \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{2}}+\log \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{3}}$
(B) $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}$ is a stronger acid than $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{PO}_{4}^{-}$ and $\mathrm{HPO}_{4}^{2-}$
(C) $\mathrm{HH}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{PO}_{4}^{-}(\mathrm{aq}): \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{1}}$
$=7.5 \times 10^{-3}$
${{\text{H}}_2}{\text{PO}}_4^ - ({\text{aq}}) \rightleftharpoons {{\text{H}}^ + }({\text{aq}}) + {\text{HPO}}_4^{2 - }({\text{aq}}):$ ${{\text{K}}_{{{\text{a}}_2}}} = 6.2 \times {10^{ - 8}}$
${\text{HPO}}_4^{2 - }({\text{aq}}) \rightleftharpoons {{\text{H}}^ + }({\text{aq}}) + {\text{PO}}_4^{3 - }({\text{aq}}):$ ${{\text{K}}_{{{\text{a}}_3}}} = 1.7 \times {10^{ - 12}}$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{1}}>\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{2}}>\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{3}}$
The correct option is 1 .

so, $\mathrm{C}=\dfrac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{1}}}{\mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{a}_{2}}} \times \dfrac{\alpha_{2}^{2}}{\alpha_{1}^{2}}$

explanation: https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764953347/preptracker/questions/q_18517_explanation_1764953346682.png  Both fumaric and maleic acids have two ionisable $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ions i.e., protons. The maleate monoanion shows intramolecular $\mathrm{H}$-bonding, whereas fumarate monoanion shows intermolecular $\mathrm{H}$-bonding. Thus, fumarate monoanion requires more energy to give fumarate dianion. Hence, second dissociation constant of fumaric acid is more than that of maleic acid. So option (2) is correct.

question:
Assertion: $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ are known as polybasic acids.

Reason: They have two or more than two protons per molecule of the acid.
explanation:
(i) $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}$
(ii) $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}^{-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{PO}_{4}^{-}$
$\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{PO}_{4} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{PO}_{4}^{-}$
$\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{PO}_{4}^{2-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{PO}_{4}^{-3}$
The acids which can produce more than one proton are called polybasic acids. So option (1) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

explanation: The common ion effect is defined as the suppression of the dissociation of a weak electrolyte by the addition of a strong electrolyte having some common ion. It is observed in weak electrolytes. So option (1) is correct.

question: The $\mathrm{pH}$ of monoacidic weak base is 10.9 . Calculate the percent dissociation in $0.02 \mathrm{M}$ solution.
explanation:
$\mathrm{pOH}=14-\mathrm{pH}=14-10.9=3.1$
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{pOH}=-\log _{10}\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right] \\& \begin{aligned}\therefore \log _{10}\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right] & =-\mathrm{pOH} \\& =-3.1 \\& =-3-0.1-1+1 \\& =\overline{4}+0.9=\overline{4} .9\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$
$\therefore\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=\operatorname{antilog}(\overline{4} .9)=7.943 \times 10^{-4}$
For monoacidic base,
$\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=\propto \mathrm{c}$
$\therefore \propto=\dfrac{\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]}{\mathrm{c}}$
$=\dfrac{7.943 \times 10^{-4}}{0.02}=3.97 \times 10^{-2}$
Percent dissociation $=\propto \times 100$
$=3.97 \times 10^{-2} \times 100$
$=3.97$
subject: Chemistry

question: At $298 \mathrm{~K}, 0.1 \mathrm{M}$ solutin of acetic acid is $1.34 \%$ ionized. What is the dissociation constant of acetic acid?

$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}=\propto^{2} \mathrm{c} \\& =\left(1.34 \times 10^{-2}\right)^{2} \times 0.1 \\& =1.8 \times 10^{-5}\end{aligned}$

question: A weak monobasic acid is $3.0 \%$ dissociated in it's $0.04 \mathrm{M}$ solution. What is the dissociaiton constant of acid?

$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}=\propto^{2} \mathrm{c}=\left(3 \times 10^{-2}\right)^{2} \times 0.04=3.6 \times 10^{-5}$

question: A weak monobasic acid is $10 \%$ dissociated in $0.05 \mathrm{M}$ solution. What is its percentage dissociation in $0.10 \mathrm{M}$ solution?
explanation:
$\propto_{1}=10 \%=\dfrac{10}{100}=0.1, \mathrm{c}_{1}=0.05 \mathrm{M}$
$\mathrm{c}_{2}=0.10 \mathrm{M}, \propto_{2}=?$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}=\mathrm{C} \alpha^{2}$ So, $\mathrm{C}_{1} \alpha_{1}^{2}=\mathrm{C}_{2} \alpha_{2}^{2}$
$\therefore(0.1)^{2} \times 0.05=\propto_{2}^{2} \times 0.10$
$\therefore \propto_{2}^{2}=\dfrac{10^{-2} \times 5 \times 10^{-2}}{10^{-1}}=50 \times 10^{-4}$
$\therefore \propto_{2}=\sqrt{50 \times 10^{-4}}=7.17 \times 10^{-2}=7.17 \%$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Ionization Constant of Weak Acid, \[\rm{K_a}\]

question: The dissociation constant of weak monobasic acid is $2.7 \times 10^{-5}$. If degree of dissociation of acid is $3 \times 10^{-2}$, what is the concentration of acid?
explanation:
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}=2.7 \times 10^{-5}, \propto c=3 \times 10^{-2}, \mathrm{c}=$ ?
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}=\mathrm{c} \propto^{2}$
$\therefore \mathrm{c}=\dfrac{\mathrm{K}}{\propto^{2}}=\dfrac{2.7 \times 10^{-5}}{\left(3 \times 10^{-2}\right)^{2}}=\dfrac{2.7 \times 10^{-5}}{9 \times 10^{-4}}=0.03 \mathrm{M}$

question:
Dissociation constant of propionic acid is $1.32 \times 10^{-5}$. Calculate the degree of dissociation of acid in $0.05 \mathrm{M}$ solution.
[2021]
explanation:

$\propto=\sqrt{\dfrac{\mathrm{Ka}}{\mathrm{c}}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{1.32 \times 10^{-5}}{0.05}}=\sqrt{2.64} \times 10^{-2}$
$\therefore \propto=1.6 \times 10^{-2}$

(i) $\mathrm{pH}=-\log _{10}\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]$
$\therefore\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=4.571 \mathrm{M}$
(ii) $\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=\propto \mathrm{c}$
$\therefore \propto=\dfrac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]}{\mathrm{c}}=\dfrac{4.571 \times 10^{-3}}{0.1}=4.571 \times 10^{-2}$

question:

It is more difficult to remove a positively charged proton from a negative ion due to Coulombic forces.
explanation: Higher order ionisation constants $\mathrm {\left(\mathrm{K} a_{2}\right.}$, $\mathrm {\mathrm{K} a_{3}}$ ) are smaller than the lower order ionisation constant $\mathrm {\left(\mathrm{K} a_{1}\right)}$ of a polyprotic acid. The reason for this is that, it is more difficult to remove a positively charged proton from a negative ion due to electrostatic forces. This can be seen in the case of removing a proton from the uncharged $\mathrm {\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}}$ as compared from a negatively charged $\mathrm {\mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-1}}$. So, the option (3) is correct.

question: Which one of the following arrangements represents the correct order of the proton affinity of the given species?
explanation:
The species with the greatest proton affinity will be the strongest base, and its conjugate acid will be the weakest acid. The weakest acid will have the smallest value of$\rm {{{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}}$ Since HI is a stronger acid than HF which is a stronger acid than $\mathrm {\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}}$, a partial order of proton affinity is
$\rm {{{\text{I}}^{\text{ - }}}{\text{ < }}{{\text{F}}^{\text{ - }}}{\text{ < H}}{{\text{S}}^{\text{ - }}}}$
Since $\mathrm {\mathrm{NH}_{3}}$ is a very weak acid, $\mathrm {\mathrm{NH}_{2}^{-}}$must be a very strong base. Therefore the correct order of proton affinity is
$\rm {{{\text{I}}^{\text{ - }}}{\text{ < }}{{\text{F}}^{\text{ - }}}{\text{ < HS < NH}}_{\text{2}}^{\text{ - }}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: The Bronsted – Lowry Theory of Acids and Bases

explanation:
$\mathrm {2 \mathrm{NH}_{3(l)} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}+\mathrm{NH}_{2}^{-}}$
$\rm {K_{N H_{3}}=x^{2}=10^{-30}}$
Thus, ${\text{x = 1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 15}}}}{\text{ M = }}\left[ {{\text{NH}}_{\text{2}}^{\text{ - }}} \right]$
$\mathrm {\left[N H_{2}^{-}\right]=10^{-15} \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{L}=\dfrac{10^{-15}}{10^{6}} \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{mm}^{3}}$
$\mathrm {\left[\mathrm{NH}_{2}^{-}\right]=10^{-21} \times 6 \times 10^{23}}$ ions $\mathrm {/ \mathrm{mm}^{3}=600}$ ions $\mathrm {/ \mathrm{mm}^{3}}$

question: The ionisation constant of dimethylamine is $5.4 \times 10^{-4}$. Calculate percentage ionisation of ${\text{0}}{\text{.02}}\,\,{\text{M}}$ solution in ${\text{0}}{\text{.1}}\,\,{\text{M NaOH}}$?
explanation:
In the presence of ${\text{0}}.{\text{1 M NaOH}}$, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764953236/preptracker/questions/q_17155_explanation_1764953235819.png [dimethylamine is a weak base and $\mathrm {\mathrm{NaOH}}$ is a strong base so we can assume that $\mathrm {(\mathrm{C} \alpha+0.1) \approx 0.1]}$
$\mathrm {\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}=\dfrac{\left[\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \stackrel{+}{\mathrm{N}} \mathrm{H}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]}{\left[\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{NH}\right]}}$
or $\mathrm {5.4 \times 10^{-4}=\dfrac{\mathrm{C} \alpha \times 0.1}{0.02}}$
$\mathrm {\mathrm{C} \alpha=\dfrac{5.4 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.02}{0.1}=108 \times 10^{-6}}$
$\mathrm {\alpha=\dfrac{108 \times 10^{-6}}{0.02}=54 \times 10^{-4}=5.4 \times 10^{-3}}$.
$\mathrm {\alpha=0.54 \%}$.
In the presence of strong base, dissociation of weak base i.e., dimethylamine decreases due to common ion effect.

subtopic: Ionization Constant of Weak Base, \[\rm{K_b}\]

${{\text{K}}_{\text{1}}}{\text{ = 4}}{\text{.2}} \times {\text{1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 7}}}}{\text{ and }}{{\text{K}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ = 4}}{\text{.8}} \times {\text{1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 11}}}}$

question:
At ${\rm{T}}({\rm{K}}),{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{c}}}$ for the reaction
${\text{A}}{{\text{O}}_2}(\,{\text{g}}) + {\text{B}}{{\text{O}}_2}(\,{\text{g}}) \rightleftharpoons {\text{A}}{{\text{O}}_3}(\,{\text{g}}) + {\text{BO}}({\text{g}})$ is 16 . One mole each of reactants and products are taken in a 1 L flask and heated to $\mathrm{T}(\mathrm{K})$, and equilibrium is established. What is the equilibrium concentration of BO (in $\mathrm{mol} \mathrm{L}^{-1}$ )?
explanation:
${{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}} = \frac{{\left[ {{\text{A}}{{\text{O}}_3}} \right][{\text{BO}}]}}{{\left[ {{\text{A}}{{\text{O}}_2}} \right]\left[ {{\text{B}}{{\text{O}}_2}} \right]}} = 16$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951854/preptracker/questions/q_105072_explanation_1764951854138.png
$\dfrac{(1+\alpha) \times(1+\alpha)}{(1-\alpha)(1-\alpha)}=16 \Rightarrow \dfrac{1+\alpha}{1-\alpha}=4$
$\therefore 1+\alpha=4-4 \alpha \Rightarrow 5 \alpha=3 \Rightarrow \alpha=0.6$.
$\therefore$ Equilibrium concentration of $\mathrm{BO}=1+\alpha$
$=1+0.6=1.6$.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Calculation of Equilibrium Concentration

question: Match the following columns based on the relationship between equilibrium constant K , reaction quotient Q , and Gibbs free energy $\Delta \mathrm{G}$, and choose the correct options from the codes given below.
explanation:
(A - Q) $\Delta {\text{G}} = 0$ describes the condition of equilibrium where there is no net change in the concentrations of reactants and products.
(B - R) ${\text{Q}} = {\text{K}}$ indicates that the reaction quotient has reached the equilibrium constant's value, signifying that the system is at equilibrium.
(C - S) $\Delta {\text{G}} < 0$ indicates that the reaction is spontaneous and will proceed in the forward direction to form more products.
(D - P) $\Delta {\text{G}} > 0$ shows that the reaction is non-spontaneous, favoring the formation or maintenance of reactants over products. Correct option is (2).

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
A reaction quotient Q greater than the equilibrium constant K indicates that a reaction will proceed in the reverse direction to reach equilibrium.
$\mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{K}$ at equilibrium indicates that the concentrations of reactants and products no longer change.
explanation: Both statements are true. Statement A correctly describes how a reaction will shift to restore equilibrium when Q is greater than K (Le Chatelier's Principle), indicating an excess of products relative to reactants. Statement B correctly states that at equilibrium $(\mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{K})$, the concentrations of reactants and products will remain constant, reflecting a dynamic balance between the forward and reverse reactions. Correct option is (1).

Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

Statement B is also true as ${{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}}$ quantifies the relationship between reactants and products at equilibrium. Correct option is (1).

question: The $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}$ value at equilibrium of $\mathrm{SO}_{3}$ formation reaction from $\mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ and $\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ is $5 \mathrm{~atm}^{-1}$. What is the equilibrium partial pressure of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ if the equilibrium pressure of $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{SO}_{3}$ are equal?
explanation:
The given reaction is
$\underset{(\mathrm{g})}{2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}}+\underset{(\mathrm{g})}{\mathrm{O}_{2}} \rightleftharpoons \underset{(\mathrm{~g})}{2 \mathrm{SO}_{3}}$
$\therefore \quad {{\text{K}}_{\text{P}}} = \frac{{{{\left[ {{{\text{p}}_{{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_3}}}} \right]}^2}}}{{{{\left[ {{{\text{p}}_{{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_2}}}} \right]}^2}\left[ {{{\text{p}}_{{{\text{O}}_2}}}} \right]}}$
$\mathrm{As},\left[\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{SO}_{3}}\right]=\left[\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{SO}_{2}}\right]$
So, $\quad 5=\dfrac{1}{\left[\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}\right]} \Rightarrow\left[\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}\right]=\dfrac{1}{5}=0.2 \mathrm{~atm}$

question: For a reaction, $2 \mathrm{~A} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{~B}+\mathrm{C}, \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}$ is $2 \times 10^{-3}$. At a given time, the reaction mixture has$[\mathrm{A}]=[\mathrm{B}]=[\mathrm{C}]=3 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{M}$. Which of the following options is correct?
explanation:
For the reaction, the reaction quotient ${{\text{Q}}_{\text{C}}}$ is given by
$\mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{[\mathrm{B}][\mathrm{C}]}{[\mathrm{A}]^{2}}$
As, $[\mathrm{A}]=[\mathrm{B}]=[\mathrm{C}]=3 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{M}$
$\mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{\left(3 \times 10^{-4}\right)\left(3 \times 10^{-4}\right)}{\left(3 \times 10^{-4}\right)^{2}}=1$
As, $\mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{C}}>\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}$,
The reaction will proceed in the reverse direction.
So, correct option is (2).

The reaction proceeds to the right

question: For a given reaction, $2 \mathrm{~A} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{~B}+\mathrm{C}$, the equilibrium constant is $2 \times 10^{-3}$. If at any given time the composition of the reaction mixture is $[\mathrm{A}]=[\mathrm{B}]=[\mathrm{C}]=6 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{M}$; predict in which direction the reaction will proceed and the correct value for reaction quotient.
explanation:
$2 \mathrm{~A} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{~B}+\mathrm{C}$
Reaction quotient,
$\mathrm{Q}=\dfrac{[\mathrm{B}][\mathrm{C}]}{[\mathrm{A}]^{2}}=\dfrac{\left(6 \times 10^{-5}\right) \times\left(6 \times 10^{-5}\right)}{\left(6 \times 10^{-5}\right)^{2}}=1$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{Q}=1>\mathrm{K}_{\text {eq }}=2 \times 10^{-3}$
So, the reaction will proceed in backward direction.

question:
$\mathrm{PCl}_{5} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{PCl}_{3}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$
If the equilibrium constant $\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}\right)$ for the above reaction at 500 K is 1.79 and the equilibrium concentration of $\mathrm{PCl}_{5}$ and $\mathrm{PCl}_{3}$ are 1.41 M and 1.59 M , respectively, then the concentration of $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ is approximately.
explanation:
Equilibrium constant $\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}\right)=\dfrac{\left[\mathrm{PCl}_{3}\right]\left[\mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{PCl}_{5}\right]}$
$1.79=\dfrac{1.59 \times\left[\mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right]}{1.41}$
$\left[\mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right]=\dfrac{1.79 \times 1.41}{1.59}=1.587 \simeq 1.59 \mathrm{~m}$

question: The equilibrium constant at 298 K for a reaction ${\mathrm{\mathrm{A}_{(\mathrm{g})}+\mathrm{B}_{(\mathrm{g})} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{C}_{(\mathrm{g})}+\mathrm{D}_{(\mathrm{g})}}}$ is 100 . If the initial concentration of all the four species were 1 M each, then equilibrium concentration of ${\mathrm{D}}$ (in ${\mathrm{\mathrm{mol} \mathrm{L}^{-1}}}$ ) will be ____ .
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764959160/preptracker/questions/q_19070_explanation_1764959159729.png ${\mathrm{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}} \quad=\dfrac{[\mathrm{C}][\mathrm{D}]}{[\mathrm{A}][\mathrm{B}]}}}$
${\mathrm{100=\dfrac{[1+x][1+x]}{[1-x][1-x]}}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad 100=\dfrac{[1+x]^{2}}{[1-x]^{2}}}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad 10=\dfrac{[1+x]}{[1-x]}}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad[1+x]=10-10 x}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad x+10 x=10-1=9}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad x=\dfrac{9}{11}=0.82}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore}}$ Equilibrium concentration of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{D}=1+x}}$
${\mathrm{=1+0.82=1.82 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}}}$

Concentration of
$\left[ {{\rm{N}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}} \right] = \frac{{{\rm{number}}\,{\rm{of}}\,{\rm{moles}}}}{{{\rm{volume}}\,({\rm{in}}\,{\rm{L}})}} = \frac{6}{{10}} = 0.6{\rm{2}}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}} \rightleftharpoons {{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ + 3}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}$
${\rm{Initial}}\,{\rm{mode}}\quad {\rm{0}}.{\rm{6}}\quad {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{0}}\quad {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,\,\,{\rm{0}}$
${\rm{At}}\,{\rm{equil}}{\rm{.}}\quad {\rm{0}}.{\rm{6}} - {\rm{2x}}\quad{\rm{x}}\quad {\rm{3x}}$
Then, concentration of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ at equilibrium will be
$0.6-2 \mathrm{x}$

question: Formaldehyde polymerises to form glucose according to the reaction, $6 \mathrm{HCHO} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}$. The theoretically computed equilibrium constant for this reaction is found to be $6 \times 10^{22}$. If $1 \mathrm{M}$ solution of glucose dissociates according to the above equilibrium, the concentration of formaldehyde in the solution will be
explanation:
$6 \mathrm{HCHO} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}$
A very high value of $\mathrm{K}$ for the given equilibrium shows that dissociation of glucose to form HCHO is very-very small. Hence, at equilibrium,
$\left[\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}\right]=1 \mathrm{M}$
${\rm{K}} = \frac{{\left[ {{{\rm{C}}_6}{{\rm{H}}_{12}}{{\rm{O}}_6}} \right]}}{{{{[{\rm{HCHO}}]}^6}}}{\rm{ }}$
${\rm{i}}{\rm{.e}}{\rm{. }}6 \times {10^{22}} = \frac{1}{{{{[{\rm{HCHO}}]}^6}}}$
${\rm{or }}\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} [{\rm{HCHO}}] = {\left( {\frac{1}{{6 \times {{10}^{22}}}}} \right)^{1/6}}$
$ = 1.6 \times {10^{ - 4}}{\rm{M}}$

question: 28g  of $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ and $6 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ were mixed. At equilibrium $17 \mathrm{~g}$ $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ was produced. The weights of $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ at equilibrium are respectively
explanation:
$\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {}&{{{\text{N}}_2}}& + &{3{{\text{H}}_2}}& \rightleftharpoons &{2{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_3}} \\ {{\text{Initially}}{\mkern 1mu} }&{{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} 28\,{\text{g}}}&{}&{6\,{\text{g}}}&{}&{0{\mkern 1mu} } \\ {}&{{\mkern 1mu} {\text{1 mole}}}&{}&{{\mkern 1mu} {\text{3 mole}}{\mkern 1mu} }&{}&{{\mkern 1mu} {\text{2 mole}}} \\ {{\text{At}}{\mkern 1mu} {\text{equil}}}&{(1 - 0.5)}&{}&{{\mkern 1mu} (3 - 1.5)}&{}&{17{\mkern 1mu} {\text{g}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}} \end{array}$
Concentration of 1 mole of $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ combines with 3 moles of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ to produce 2 moles of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ but, since at equilibrium 1 mole of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ is produced. So, 0.5 moles, i.e. $14 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ are left at equilibrium, Similarly, 3 moles of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \equiv 2$ moles of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$.
For 1 mole of $\mathrm{NH}_{3} \equiv 1.5$ mole of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$
Hence, 1.5 mole i.e. $3 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ are left at equilibrium.
So, $14 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ and 3g of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ are left at equilibrium.

question: The value of  ${{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}}$ for the reaction, ${\text{2A}} \rightleftharpoons {\rm{B}} + {\rm{C}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{is}}\,{\mkern 1mu} 2 \times {10^{ - 3}}$. At a given time, the composition of reaction mixture is ${\rm{[A] = [B] = [C]}}$=$3 \times {10^{ - 4}}{\rm{M}}$. In which direction, the reaction will proceed?
explanation:
For the reaction, the reaction quotient $\mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{C}}$ is given by
$\mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{C}}=[\mathrm{B}][\mathrm{C}] /[\mathrm{A}]^{2}$
Given that,
${\text{[A]}} = {\text{[B]}} = [{\text{C]}} = 3 \times {10^{ - 4}}{\text{M}}$
Then, $\mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{\left(3 \times 10^{-4}\right)\left(3 \times 10^{-4}\right)}{\left(3 \times 10^{-4}\right)^{2}}=1
$
As, $\mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{C}}>\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}$, so the reaction will proceed in the reverse direction.

question: For the equilibrium, $2 \operatorname{NOBr}(\mathrm{g}) \rightleftharpoons$ $2 \mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{Br}_{2}$, calculate the ratio $\frac{{{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{p}}}}}{{\rm{P}}},$ where P is the total pressure given that ${{\rm{P}}_{{\rm{B}}{{\rm{r}}_{\rm{2}}}}} = \frac{{\rm{P}}}{9}$ at a certain temperature
explanation:
$\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {}&{{\text{2NOBr}}}& \rightleftharpoons &{{\text{2NO}}}& + &{{\text{B}}{{\text{r}}_{\text{2}}}} \\ {{\text{Initial:}}}&2&{}&0&{}&0 \\ \begin{gathered} {\text{equm}} \hfill \\ {\text{moles}} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} &{2 - 2{\text{x}}}&{}&{2{\text{x}}}&{}&{\text{x}} \\ \begin{gathered} {\text{equm}} \hfill \\ {\text{Pressure}} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} &{\frac{{2 - 2{\text{x}}}}{{2 + {\text{x}}}}.{\text{P}}}&{}&{\frac{{2{\text{x}}}}{{2 + {\text{x}}}}.{\text{P}}}&{}&{\frac{{\text{x}}}{{2 + {\text{x}}}}.{\text{P}}} \end{array}$
Initial moles $=2+2 \mathrm{x}$
$\dfrac{\mathrm{x}}{2+\mathrm{x}} \cdot \mathrm{P}=\dfrac{\mathrm{P}}{9}$ (Given)
$\mathrm{x}=1 / 4$. On solving $\dfrac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{P}}=\dfrac{1}{81}$

$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=1.086 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~atm}^{2}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The efflorescent nature of $\mathrm{CuSO}_{4} \cdot 5 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ can be noticed when the vapour pressure of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ in atmosphere is
explanation:

question: $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}$ for $\mathrm{PCl}_{5}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{PCl}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ is 0.04 at $250^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. How many moles of $\mathrm{PCl}_{5}$ must be added to 3L flask to obtain a $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ concentration of 0.15 M .
explanation:
$\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {}&{{\text{PC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{5}}}{\text{(g)}}}& \rightleftharpoons &{{\text{PC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{(g)}}}& + &{{\text{C}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}}} \\ {{\text{Initial:}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} }&{\text{a}}&{}& - &{}& - \\ {{\text{eq}}{{\text{u}}^{\text{n}}}{\text{:}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} }&{\frac{{{\text{a}} - {\text{x}}}}{3}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} }&{}&{\frac{{\text{x}}}{3}}&{}&{\frac{{\text{x}}}{3}} \end{array}$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}=\dfrac{\left(\dfrac{\mathrm{x}}{3}\right)^{2}}{\left(\dfrac{\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{x}}{3}\right)}=\dfrac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{3(\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{x})}=0.04$
Given, $\left[\mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right]=\dfrac{\mathrm{x}}{3}=0.15$
x = 0.45
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{(0.45)^{2}}{3(\mathrm{a}-0.45)}=0.04$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{0.45 \times 0.45}{\mathrm{a}-0.45}=0.12$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=2.13$

question: In a system,  $\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{s}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{~B}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{g})$, if the concentration of C at equilibrium is increased by a factor of 2 , it will cause the equilibrium concentration of B to change to
explanation:
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}=[\mathrm{B}]^{2}[\mathrm{C}]$
even $[\mathrm{C}]$ is doubled, $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}$ will remain same, as $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}$ depends only on temp.
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}=[\mathrm{B}]^{2}[\mathrm{C}]=\left[\mathrm{B}^{\prime}\right]^{2}[2 \mathrm{C}]$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{[\mathrm{B}]^{2}}{2}[\mathrm{C}]=\left[\mathrm{B}^{\prime}\right]^{2}[\mathrm{C}]$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{[\mathrm{B}]^{2}}{2}=\left[\mathrm{B}^{\prime}\right]^{2}$
$\Rightarrow\left[\mathrm{B}^{\prime}\right]=\dfrac{[\mathrm{B}]}{\sqrt{2}}$

options.text: One half of its original value

question:
For the reaction ${\mathrm{2 \mathrm{~A} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{B}+\mathrm{C}, \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}=4 \times 10^{-3}}}$. At a given time, the composition of reaction mixture is : ${\mathrm{[\mathrm{A}]=[\mathrm{B}]=[\mathrm{C}]=2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M}}}$.

explanation:
${\mathrm{2 \mathrm{~A} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{B}+\mathrm{C}, \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=4 \times 10^{-3}}}$ and reaction quotient, ${\mathrm{Q_{c}}}$
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{[\mathrm{B}][\mathrm{C}]}{[\mathrm{A}]^{2}}=\dfrac{\left(2 \times 10^{-3}\right)\left(2 \times 10^{-3}\right)}{\left(2 \times 10^{-3}\right)^{2}} \\& \mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{C}}=1\end{aligned}$
As ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{C}}>\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}}}$, so reaction has a tendency to move backward.

Reaction has a tendency to go in backward direction.
Reaction has gone to completion in forward direction.

question:
Assertion: If $\mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{c}}$ (reaction quotient) $ < \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}$ (equilibrium constant), reaction moves in direction of products.


Reason: Reaction quotient is defined in the same way as equilibrium constant at any stage of the reaction.
explanation:
$\mathrm{aA}+\mathrm{bB} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{cC}+\mathrm{dD}, \mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{c}}=\dfrac{[\mathrm{C}]^{\mathrm{c}}[\mathrm{D}]^{\mathrm{d}}}{[\mathrm{A}]^{\mathrm{a}}[\mathrm{B}]^{\mathrm{b}}}$
If ${{\text{Q}}_{\text{c}}}{\text{ > }}{{\text{K}}_c}$, reaction will proceed in the direction of reactants.
If ${{\text{Q}}_{\text{c}}}{\text{ < }}{{\text{K}}_{\text{c}}}$, reaction will move in direction of products.
If $\mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{c}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}$, the reaction mixture is already at equilibrium.
So option (2) is correct.

question:
In a 2 litre flask, the reaction takes place as:
${\text{COC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{CO(g) + C}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}}$
The equilibrium conc. of $\mathrm{COCl}_{2}$ was found to be 0.4. If excess of $\mathrm{COCl}_{2}$ is added to the system, the equilibrium re-establish and ${\kern 1pt} \left[ {{\text{COC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}} \right]$ becomes 1.6. What is the equilibrium conc. of $[\mathrm{CO}]$ ?
explanation:
$\rm {{\text{COC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{CO(g) + C}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}}}$
$\rm {\therefore \dfrac{[\mathrm{CO}]\left[\mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{COCl}_{2}\right]}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}}$
Let $\mathrm {[\mathrm{CO}]=\mathrm{x}}$, then $\mathrm {\left[\mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right]=\mathrm{x}}$
$\mathrm {\therefore \dfrac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{COCl}_{2}\right]}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}}$ or $\mathrm {\dfrac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{0.4}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}}$
Again $\mathrm {\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{0.4}=\dfrac{[\mathrm{CO}]\left[\mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{COCl}_{2}\right]}}$
$\mathrm {\dfrac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{0.4}=\dfrac{[\mathrm{CO}]^{2}}{1.6}[}$ as $\mathrm {[\mathrm{CO}]=[\mathrm{Cl}]]}$
$\mathrm {\therefore \mathrm{x}^{2} \times 4=[\mathrm{CO}]^{2}}$
$\mathrm {2 \mathrm{x}=[\mathrm{CO}]}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Calculation of Equilibrium Concentration

question: ${\text{x}}$ moles and ${\text{y}}$ moles per litre of $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$, respectivley, were allowed to react. At the equilibrium ${{\text{A}}_{\left( {\text{g}} \right)}}{\text{ + 2}}{{\text{B}}_{\left( {\text{g}} \right)}} \rightleftharpoons \frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{2}}}{{\text{C}}_{\left( {\text{g}} \right)}}$. The concentration of A, B and C were found to be  4,2 and $2 \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{L}$, respectively. values of ${\text{x}}$ and ${\text{y}}$ are
explanation:
$\mathrm {\mathrm{A}+2 \mathrm{~B} \rightleftharpoons \dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{C}}$
At equilibrium if ' $\mathrm {x}$ ' moles of $\mathrm {A}$ is reacted
$\mathrm {\left(x-x^{\prime}\right)\left(y-2 x^{\prime}\right)=\dfrac{1}{2} x^{\prime}}$
$\mathrm {\left(x-x^{\prime}\right)=4}$
$\mathrm {\left(y-2 x^{\prime}\right)=2}$
$\mathrm {\dfrac{1}{2} x^{\prime}=2 ; x^{\prime}=4}$
$\mathrm {[\mathrm{A}]=\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{x}^{\prime}=\mathrm{x}-4=4}$
$\mathrm {\mathrm{x}=8}$
$\mathrm {\mathrm{y}-8=2 ; \mathrm{y}=10}$

When concentration of $\mathrm {\mathrm{OH}^{-}}$ions is decresed

question:
For the equilibrium
${\text{CaC}}{{\text{O}}_{{\text{3}}\left( {\text{s}} \right)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{Ca}}{{\text{O}}_{\left( {\text{s}} \right)}}{\text{ + C}}{{\text{O}}_{{\text{2}}\left( {\text{g}} \right)}}{\text{;}}$
${{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}}{\text{ = }}$ ${\text{1}}{\text{.64}}$ $\,{\text{atm}}\,\,$ ${\text{at}}$ ${\text{1000K.}}$ ${\mkern 1mu} {\text{50}}\,$ ${\text{g}}$ ${\text{of}}$ ${\text{CaC}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}$ in a

explanation:
For the reaction
$\mathrm {\mathrm{CaCO}_{3(\mathrm{~s})} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CaO}_{(\mathrm{s})}+\mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}}$
$\mathrm {\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}=\left[\mathrm{CO}_{2}\right]}$ or $\mathrm {\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}}$
No. of moles of
$\mathrm {\mathrm{CaCO}_{3(\mathrm{~s})}=\dfrac{\text { Mass }}{\text { Mol.mass }}=\dfrac{50}{100}=0.5}$
Applying ideal gas equation, $\mathrm {\mathrm{PV}=\mathrm{nRT}}$
$\mathrm {1.64 \times 10=\mathrm{n} \times 0.082 \times 1000}$
No. of moles of $\mathrm {\mathrm{CO}_{2}, \mathrm{n}=0.2}$
No. of moles of unreacted
$\mathrm {\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}=0.5-0.2=0.3}$
$\mathrm {\%}$ of unreacted $\mathrm {\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}=\dfrac{0.3}{0.5} \times 100=60 \%}$

question:
${\text{3}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{2}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{(g)}}$
for the above reaction at $298 \mathrm{~K}, \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{C}}$ is found to be $3.0 \times 10^{-59}$. If the concentration of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ at equilibrium is $0.040\,\,{\text{M}}$ then concentration of $\mathrm{O}_{3}$ in $\mathrm{M}$ is
explanation:
$\rm {{{\text{3O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{2}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{(g)}}}$
$\mathrm {\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}=\dfrac{\left[\mathrm{O}_{3}\right]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]^{3}}}$
$\mathrm {3 \times 10^{-59}=\dfrac{\left[\mathrm{O}_{3}\right]^{2}}{\left(4 \times 10^{-2}\right)^{3}}}$
$\mathrm {\left[\mathrm{O}_{3}\right]^{2}=3 \times 10^{-59} \times 64 \times 10^{-6}=192 \times 10^{-65}}$
$\mathrm {=19.2 \times 10^{-64}}$
$\mathrm {\left[\mathrm{O}_{3}\right]=4.38 \times 10^{-32} \mathrm{M}}$

question: At $700 \mathrm{~K}$, equilibrium constant for the reaction:${{\text{H}}_{{\text{2(g)}}}}{\text{ + }}{{\text{I}}_{{\text{2(g)}}}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{2H}}{{\text{I}}_{{\text{(g)}}}}$ is ${\text{54}}{\text{.8}}$ . If $0.5 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$ of $\mathrm{HI}(\mathrm{g})$ is present at equilibrium at $700 \mathrm{~K}$, what are the concentration of ${{\text{H}}_{{\text{2(g)}}}}{\text{ }}$ and ${\text{ }}{{\text{I}}_{{\text{2(g)}}}}$, respectively assuming that we initially started with $\mathrm{HI}(\mathrm{g})$ and allowed it to reach equilibrium at $700 \mathrm{~K}$ ?
explanation:
Given, $\mathrm {H_{2}+I_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 H I, K_{c}=54.8}$
For, $\mathrm {2 \mathrm{HI} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{I}_{2}, K_{c}^{1}=\dfrac{1}{54.8}}$
$\rm {[H I]=0.5 M}$
Let, $\mathrm {\left[H_{2}\right]=\left[I_{2}\right]=x M}$
$\therefore {\text{K}}_{\text{C}}^{\text{1}}{\text{ = }}\frac{{\left[ {{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}} \right]\left[ {{{\text{I}}_{\text{2}}}} \right]}}{{{{\left[ {{\text{HI}}} \right]}^{\text{2}}}}}$
$\frac{{\text{1}}}{{{\text{54}}{\text{.8}}}}{\text{ = }}\frac{{{\text{(x)(x)}}}}{{{{{\text{(0}}{\text{.5)}}}^{\text{2}}}}}$
on solving, $\mathrm {x=0.0675 M}$
$\rm {\therefore \quad\left[H_{2}\right]=\left[I_{2}\right]=0.0675 M}$

explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; however, the reason given does not fully explain the assertion because the pH of a buffer also depends on the intrinsic properties of the weak acid and base, such as their $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}$, not solely on their concentrations. explanation: The assertion is true as it correctly describes the primary function of a buffer solution. question: A solution is prepared by mixing 10 mL of 1.0 M acetic acid and 20 mL of 0.5 M sodium acetate and diluted to 100 mL . If the $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}$ of acetic acid is 4.76, then the pH of the solution is
explanation:
The solution obtained by mixing the given data is an acidic buffer.
The expression used for finding the pH of acidic buffer is given by
$\mathrm{pH}=\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}+\log \dfrac{[\text { Salt }]}{[\text { Acid }]}$
$\mathrm{pK}_{a}=4.76$ (given)
$[$ Salt $]=\dfrac{20 \times 0.5}{100}=0.1 \mathrm{M}$
$[$ Acid $]=\dfrac{10 \times 1}{100}=0.1 \mathrm{M}$
Substituting the values in above equation, $\mathrm{pH}=4.76+\log \dfrac{0.1}{0.1}=4.76$
Thus, option (1) is correct.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Preparation of Acidic Buffer and pH Calculation: Henderson – Hasselbalch Equation

question: Which of the following does not form a buffer solution?
explanation:
Buffer is a solution of weak acid and its conjugate base or weak base and its conjugate acid in which pH cannot be changed much after, adding a certain amount of acid and base.
Hence, $1: 1$ mole ratio of $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}+\mathrm{NaOH}\right)$ is not a buffer solution, since both acid and base has same ratio.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Definition and Types of Buffers

question: If the molar concentrations of base and its conjugate acid are same, then pOH of the buffer solution is
explanation:
$\mathrm{pOH}=\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{b}}+\log \dfrac{[\text { salt }]}{[\text { base }]}$
When, [salt] = [base], then $\mathrm{pOH}=\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{b}}$.
(Here salt is conjugate acid of base)
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Preparation of Basic Buffer and pH and pOH Calculation: Henderson – Hasselbalch Equation

question: What is the pH of 10 L of a buffer solution containing $0.01 \mathrm{MNH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}$ and $0.1 \mathrm{MNH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}$ having $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{b}}$ of 5 ?
explanation:
It is a basic buffer
$\therefore \mathrm{pOH}=\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{b}}+\log \dfrac{[\text { salt }]}{[\text { base }]}=5+\log \dfrac{0.01}{0.1}=4$
$\therefore \mathrm{pH}=14-\mathrm{pOH}=14-4=10$.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Preparation of Basic Buffer and pH and pOH Calculation: Henderson – Hasselbalch Equation

question: A solution of 0.1 mole of $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{NH}_{2}\left(\mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{b}}=5 \times\right.$ $10^{-4}$ ) and 0.08 mole of HCl is diluted to one litre, then the pOH of the solution is $(\log 1.25=0.1)$
explanation:
Given, $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}$ for $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{NH}_{2}=5 \times 10^{-4}$;
$\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{b}}=-\log 5 \times 10^{-4}=4-\log 5=3.301$
Moles of $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{NH}_{2}=0.1 \mathrm{~mol}$
Moles of $\mathrm{HCl}=0.08 \mathrm{~mol}$
$\because \quad {\text{pOH}} = {\text{p}}{{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}} + \log \frac{{[{\text{ base }}]}}{{[{\text{ acid }}]}}$ $ = 3.301 + \log \frac{{0.1}}{{0.08}}$
$\because \quad {\text{pOH}} = 3.301 + \log 1.25 = 3.41$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Preparation of Basic Buffer and pH and pOH Calculation: Henderson – Hasselbalch Equation

: A buffer solution is the mixture of a salt and an acid or a base mixed in any particular quantities.

explanation: A buffer solution is the mixture of weak acid or base with its conjugate ion. So, the correct option is (2).

question: pH of an aqueous solution of ${\mathrm{0.6\, \mathrm{M}\, \mathrm{NH}_{3}}}$ and ${\mathrm{0.4 \, \mathrm{M} \, \mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}}}$ is 9.4 ${\rm{(p}}{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{b}}}{\rm{ = }}\,{\rm{4}}.{\rm{74}})$. The new pH when ${\mathrm{0.1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}}}$ solution is added to it is
explanation:
Since ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}}}$ is completely ionised. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764959186/preptracker/questions/q_19379_explanation_1764959186310.png BBB rule: On adding base,to hte basic buffer,cocentration of base increases and salt decreases.
$\therefore $ New concentartaion of base and salt are:
${\mathrm{[}}$ Base ${\mathrm{]=\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]=0.6+0.2=0.8 \mathrm{M}}}$
${\mathrm{[}}$ Salt ${\mathrm{]=\left[\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}\right]=0.4-0.2=0.2 \mathrm{M}}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore \mathrm{pOH}=\mathrm{p} K_{b}+\log \left[\dfrac{\text { Salt }}{\text { Base }}\right]}}$
$\begin{array}{l} {\rm{pOH}} = 4.74 + \log \left( {\frac{{0.2}}{{0.8}}} \right)\\ \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, = 4.74 - 2\log 2 = 4.74 - 2 \times 0.30\\ \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, = 4.14\\ {\rm{pH}} = 14 - 4.14 = 9.86 \end{array}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Preparation of Acidic Buffer and pH Calculation: Henderson – Hasselbalch Equation

question: The $\mathrm{pH}$ of a buffer containing equal molar concentrations of a weak base and its chloride ( $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}$ for weak base $=2 \times$ $10^{-5}, \log 2=0.3$ ) is ____.
explanation:
$\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{b}}=-\log \left(2 \times 10^{-5}\right)$
$=5-0.301=4.699=4.7$
$\mathrm{pOH}=\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{b}}+\log \dfrac{[\text { Salt }]}{[\text { Base }]}$
$=4.7+\log (1)=4.7$
$\mathrm{pH}=14-4.7=9.3$

subtopic: Preparation of Basic Buffer and pH and pOH Calculation: Henderson – Hasselbalch Equation

question: When $3 \mathrm{~mol}$ of $\mathrm{HCl}$ is added to $1 \mathrm{~L}$ of an acidic buffer, its $\mathrm{pH}$ changes from 3.9 to 2.5 . The buffer capacity of the buffer solution is ____.
explanation:
Buffer capacity
$=\dfrac{\text { Number of moles } / \text { litre of acid or base added }}{\text { changes in } \mathrm{pH} \text { or } \mathrm{pOH}}$
$=\dfrac{3}{3.9-2.5}=\dfrac{3}{0.6}=5$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Buffer Capacity

question: The concentration of sodium acetate which should be added to 0.1 M solution of acetic acid ${\mathrm{\left[\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}=4.7\right]}}$ to give a solution of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{pH}=5.7}}$ is ____ M.
explanation:
${\mathrm{\mathrm{pH}=\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}+\log _{10} \dfrac{\text { [Salt }]}{[\text { Acid }]}}}$
${\mathrm{5.7=4.7+\log _{10} \dfrac{\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right]}}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad \log _{10} \dfrac{\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right]}=1}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad \log _{10} \dfrac{\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}\right]}{0.1}=1}}$
${\rm{lo}}{{\rm{g}}_{{\rm{10}}}}\left[ {{\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{COONa}}} \right]{\rm{ - lo}}{{\rm{g}}_{{\rm{10}}}}\left( {{\rm{0}}.{\rm{1}}} \right){\rm{ = 1}}$
${\mathrm{\log _{10}\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}\right]=1+\log _{10}(0.1)}}$
$=0$
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}\right]=1 \mathrm{M} \quad\left[\because \log _{10}(1)=0\right]}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Preparation of Basic Buffer and pH and pOH Calculation: Henderson – Hasselbalch Equation

question: A buffer solution contains 0.1 mole of sodium acetate in $1000 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}$ of ${\text{0}}{\text{.1}}\,\,{\text{M}}$ acetic acid. To the above buffer solution, 0.1 mole of sodium acetate is further added and dissolved. The $\mathrm{pH}$ of the resulting buffer is equal to
explanation:

$\mathrm {[}$ Salt $\mathrm {]=\dfrac{0.1+0.1}{1000 \mathrm{~mL}}=\dfrac{0.2}{1}=0.2}$
$\mathrm {[}$ Acid $\mathrm {]=\dfrac{0.1}{1000 \mathrm{~mL}}=\dfrac{0.1}{1}=0.1}$
$\mathrm {\mathrm{pH}=\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}+\log 2}$

question: Calculate $\mathrm{pH}$ of the buffer solution containing 0.15 moles of $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}$ and 0.25 moles of $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}, \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}$ for $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}$ is $1.8 \times 10^{-5}$
explanation:
Applying the equation,
$\rm { \mathrm{pOH}=\log \dfrac{[\text { Salt }]}{[\text { Base }]}-\log \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}} \\ =\log \dfrac{0.25}{0.15}-\log 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \\ =\log 5-\log 3-\log 1.8 \times 10^{-5}=4.96 \\ \mathrm{pH}=(14-4.96)=9.04}$

question: The $\mathrm{pH}$ of the solution containing $50 \mathrm{~mL}$ each of $0.10\,\,\,{\text{M}}$  sodium acetate and $0.01\,\,\,{\text{M}}$ acetic acid is [Given, $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}$ of ${\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}{\text{COOH}} = 4.57]$
explanation:
Given mixture is an example of acidic buffer as it contains weak acid,
$\mathrm {\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}}$ and salt of its conjugate base, $\mathrm {\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}}$.
$\mathrm {\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}\right]=0.10 \mathrm{M}}$;
$\mathrm {\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right]=0.01 \mathrm{M}}$;
$\mathrm {\mathrm{pH}}$ of acidic buffer solution is given by
$\mathrm {\mathrm{pH}=\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}+\log \dfrac{[\text { Salt }]}{[\text { Acid }]}}$
$\mathrm {=4.57+\log \left(\dfrac{0.1}{0.01}\right)}$
$\mathrm {=5.57}$

question: $20 \mathrm{~mL}$ of ${\text{0}}{\text{.1 M  }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}$  solution is added to 30 $\mathrm{mL}$ of ${\text{0}}{\text{.2  M N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{4}}}{\text{OH}}$ solution. The $\mathrm{pH}$ of the resultant mixture is $\left[\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{b}}\right.$ of $\left.\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}=4.7\right]$
explanation:
${\rm{0}}{\rm{.1M}}$ ${{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{S}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}$ means $\mathrm {0.2 \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}}$
milli equivalents of $\mathrm {\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}=20 \times 0.2=4}$
milli equivalents of $\mathrm {\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}=30 \times 0.2=6}$
After neutralisation, milli equivalents of $\mathrm {\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}}$ are 2 and salt $\mathrm {\left[\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}\right]}$ milli equivalents are 4.
$\rm { p O H=p K_{b}+\log \dfrac{[\text { salt }]}{[\text { base }]} \\ 4.7+\log \dfrac{4}{2} \\ p O H=5 \\\therefore \quad p H=14-p O H=14-5=9}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Preparation of Basic Buffer and pH and pOH Calculation: Henderson – Hasselbalch Equation

question: $50 \mathrm{~mL}$ of ${\text{0}}{\text{.2}}\,\,{\text{M}}$  ammonia solution is treated with $25 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $0.2{\rm{M}}\,\,{\rm{HCl}}$. If  ${\rm{p}}{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{b}}}$ of ammonia solution is ${\text{4}}{\text{.75,}}$  the $\mathrm{pH}$ of the mixture will be
explanation:
$\mathrm {\mathrm{NH}_{3}+\mathrm{HCl} \longrightarrow \mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}}$
moles of
$\rm {\mathrm{HCl}=0.2 \mathrm{M} \times 25 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~L}=0.005 \text { moles } \mathrm{HCl}}$
(total consumed)
moles of $\mathrm {\mathrm{NH}_{3}=0.2 \mathrm{M} \times 50 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~L}=0.01 \mathrm{moles}}$
$\mathrm {\mathrm{HCl}}$ excess $\mathrm {\mathrm{NH}_{3}=0.01-0.005=0.005}$ moles
1 mole ammonia $\mathrm {=1}$ mole $\mathrm {\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}}$
${\rm{0}}{\rm{.005}}\,$ ${\rm{N}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}$ ${\rm{ = }}\,\,{\rm{0}}{\rm{.005}}\,\,$ ${\rm{N}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{4}}}{\rm{Cl}}$
Total volume $\mathrm {=V_{H C l}+V_{N H_{3}}=25+50=75 \mathrm{~mL}}$
$\rm {\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]=\left[\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}\right]=\dfrac{0.005 \mathrm{~mole}}{75 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~L}}=0.066 \mathrm{M}}$
$\rm {p O H=p K_{b}+\log \dfrac{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]}}$

$\rm { p O H=4.75+\log \dfrac{[0.066]}{[0.066]} \\ p O H=4.75 \\ p H=14-p O H \Rightarrow p H=9.25}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Preparation of Basic Buffer and pH and pOH Calculation: Henderson – Hasselbalch Equation

question: Addition of sodium hydroxide solution to a weak acid (HA) results in a buffer of $\mathrm{pH}$ ${\text{6}}$. If ionisation constant of HA is $10^{-5}$, the ratio of salt to acid concentration in the buffer solution will be
explanation:
$\rm {{\text{HA}} \rightleftharpoons {{\text{H}}^{\text{ + }}}{\text{ + }}{{\text{A}}^{\text{ - }}}}$
(Unionised, weak acid and common ion effect)
$\rm {\begin{gathered}\mathrm{HA}+\mathrm{NaOH} \longrightarrow \mathrm{NaA}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \\\mathrm{NaA} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Na}^{+}+\mathrm{A}^{-} \quad \text { (ionised) } \\\mathrm{Ka}=\dfrac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{A}^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{HA}]}\end{gathered}}$
Given, $\mathrm {p H=6,\left[H^{+}\right]=1 \times 10^{-6}}$
$\rm { {\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=\dfrac{K_{a}[\text { Acid }]}{[\text { Salt }]}} \\ \dfrac{[\text { Salt }]}{[\text { Acid }]}=\dfrac{K_{a}}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]}=\dfrac{10^{-5}}{10^{-6}}=10: 1}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Preparation of Acidic Buffer and pH Calculation: Henderson – Hasselbalch Equation

question: In some solutions, the concentration of $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}$remains constant even when small amounts of strong acid or strong base are added to them. These solutions are known as:
explanation: Solutions which resist the change in the value of $\mathrm {\mathrm{pH}}$ when small amount of acid or base is added to them are known as buffers.

question:
What would be the $\mathrm{pH}$ of a solution obtained by mixing $5 \mathrm{~g}$ of acetic acid and $7.5 \mathrm{~g}$ of sodium acetate and making the volume equal to $500 \mathrm{~mL}$ ?
$\left( {{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{a}}}{\rm{ = 1}}{\rm{.75 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{ - 5}}}}{\rm{,p}}{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{a}}}{\rm{ = 4}}{\rm{.76}}} \right)$
explanation:
$\rm {{\text{pH = p}}{{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}}$$\rm {{\rm{ + log}}\dfrac{{{\rm{[ salt ]}}}}{{{\rm{[ acid ]}}}}}$
$\rm {=4.76+\log \dfrac{\dfrac{7.5}{500}}{\dfrac{5}{500}}=4.7+\log 1.5=4.87}$
Hence correct answer is $\mathrm {4.76 < p H < 5.0}$

question: The $\mathrm{pH}$ of an acid buffer can be raised by 2 units by
explanation:
$\rm {{\text{p}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{1}}}{\text{ = p}}{{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}{\text{ + log}}\left( {\dfrac{{{\text{ Salt }}}}{{{\text{ Acid }}}}} \right)}$
$\rm {{\text{p}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{1}}}{\text{ = p}}{{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}{\text{ + log}}\left( {\dfrac{{{{\text{x}}_{\text{1}}}}}{{{{\text{y}}_{\text{1}}}}}} \right)}$, $\rm {{\text{p}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ = p}}{{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}{\text{ + log}}\left( {\dfrac{{{{\text{x}}_{\text{2}}}}}{{{{\text{y}}_{\text{2}}}}}} \right)}$

The number of moles of $\mathrm {NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}$ that should be added to one litre of ${\rm{1M}}\,\,{\rm{N}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{4}}}{\rm{OH}}$ to prepare buffer solution with $\mathrm{pH}=9$ is
$\left( {{{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}{\text{ = 2 }} \times {\text{1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 5}}}}} \right.{\text{,take }}\left. {{\text{log 2 = 0}}{\text{.3}}} \right)$
explanation:
Given is an example of basic buffer
$\rm {{\text{pOH = p}}{{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}{\text{ + log}}\dfrac{{{\text{[salt]}}}}{{{\text{[Base]}}}}}$
$\rm {{\text{pH = 9, So pOH = 14 - pH = 14 - 9 = 5}}}$
$\rm {\therefore \quad 5=4.7+\log \dfrac{x}{1} \\ 0.3=\log x \\ \log 2=\log x \\ x=2}$

question: How much of 0.3 ${\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{4}}}{\text{OH}}$ Should be mixed with $30 \mathrm{~mL}$ of ${\text{0}}{\text{.2}}\,\,{\text{M}}$  solution of $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}$ to give buffer solution of ${\rm{pH}}$  8.65? $\left( {{\rm{p}}{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{b}}}{\rm{ = 4}}{\rm{.75}}} \right)$
explanation:
It is a basic buffer
$\rm {p H=8.65, p O H=14-8.65=5.35}$
${\text{ pOH = p}}{{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}{\text{ + }}\log \frac{{\left[ {{\text{NH}}_{\text{4}}^{\text{ + }}} \right]}}{{\left[ {{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{4}}}{\text{OH}}} \right]}}$ $5.35 = 4.75 + \log \frac{{\left[ {{\text{NH}}_4^ + } \right]}}{{\left[ {{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_4}{\text{OH}}} \right]}}$
$\frac{{\left[ {{\text{NH}}_4^ + } \right]}}{{\left[ {{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_4}{\text{OH}}} \right]}} = \operatorname{antilog} (5.35 - 4.75)$
$ = {\text{ antilog }}(0.6) \approx 4.0$
$\therefore \frac{{\left[ {{\text{NH}}_4^ + } \right]}}{{\left[ {{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_4}{\text{OH}}} \right]}} = \frac{{30 \times 0.2}}{{0.3 \times {\text{V}}}} \approx 4.0$
$\therefore {\text{V}} = 5{\text{mL}}$

question: The $\mathrm{pH}$ of blood is 7.4. Assuming that the buffer in blood is carbon dioxide, hydrogen carbonate ion, what is the ratio of conjugate base to acid necessary to maintain blood at its proper $\mathrm{pH} ?\left(\mathrm{Ka}=4.5 \times 10^{-7}\right)$

$\rm {\mathrm{Ka}_{1}=\dfrac{\left[\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}\right]}=4.5 \times 10^{-7}}$
Given that $\mathrm {\mathrm{pH}=7.4}$ then $\mathrm {\left[\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}\right]}$or $\mathrm {\left[H^{+}\right]=4 \times 10^{-8}}$
$\rm {\dfrac{\left[\mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{CO}_{2}\right]}=\dfrac{4.5 \times 10^{-7}}{4 \times 10^{-8}}=11}$

question: The vapour density of $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ in $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}$ is 40 . The degree of dissociation is
explanation:
Given, vapour density (d) $=40$
Vapour density of $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ (D) $=46$
$\therefore \alpha($ Degree of dissociation $)=\dfrac{\mathrm{D}-\mathrm{d}}{(\mathrm{n}-1) \mathrm{d}}$
( $\mathrm{n}=$ number of $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ at equilibrium)
$\alpha=\dfrac{46-40}{(2-1) 40}=\dfrac{6}{40}=0.15$

question:
Phosphoric acid ionizes in three steps with their ionization constant values $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{1}}, \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{a}_{2}}$ and $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{3}}$, respectively, while K is the overall ionization constant. Which of the following statements are true?
A. $\log \mathrm{K}=\log \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{1}}+\log \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{2}}+\log \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{3}}$
B. $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}$ is a stronger acid than $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{PO}_{4}^{-}$ and $\mathrm{HPO}_{4}^{2-}$
C. $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{1}}>\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{2}}>\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{3}}$
D. $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{1}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{3}}+\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{2}}}{2}$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
explanation:
(A) $\log \mathrm{K}=\log \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{1}}+\log \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{2}}+\log \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{3}}$
(B) $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}$ is a stronger acid than $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{PO}_{4}^{-}$ and $\mathrm{HPO}_{4}^{2-}$
(C) $\mathrm{HH}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{PO}_{4}^{-}(\mathrm{aq}): \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{1}}$
$=7.5 \times 10^{-3}$
${{\text{H}}_2}{\text{PO}}_4^ - ({\text{aq}}) \rightleftharpoons {{\text{H}}^ + }({\text{aq}}) + {\text{HPO}}_4^{2 - }({\text{aq}}):$ ${{\text{K}}_{{{\text{a}}_2}}} = 6.2 \times {10^{ - 8}}$
${\text{HPO}}_4^{2 - }({\text{aq}}) \rightleftharpoons {{\text{H}}^ + }({\text{aq}}) + {\text{PO}}_4^{3 - }({\text{aq}}):$ ${{\text{K}}_{{{\text{a}}_3}}} = 1.7 \times {10^{ - 12}}$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{1}}>\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{2}}>\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{3}}$
The correct option is 1 .

question:
Assertion: The second dissociation constant of fumaric acid is greater than maleic acid.


Reason: Higher the dissociation constant of acid, more is the acidic character.
explanation: https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764953347/preptracker/questions/q_18517_explanation_1764953346682.png  Both fumaric and maleic acids have two ionisable $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ions i.e., protons. The maleate monoanion shows intramolecular $\mathrm{H}$-bonding, whereas fumarate monoanion shows intermolecular $\mathrm{H}$-bonding. Thus, fumarate monoanion requires more energy to give fumarate dianion. Hence, second dissociation constant of fumaric acid is more than that of maleic acid. So option (2) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ are known as polybasic acids.

Reason: They have two or more than two protons per molecule of the acid.
explanation:
(i) $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}$
(ii) $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}^{-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{PO}_{4}^{-}$
$\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{PO}_{4} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{PO}_{4}^{-}$
$\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{PO}_{4}^{2-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{PO}_{4}^{-3}$
The acids which can produce more than one proton are called polybasic acids. So option (1) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question:
Higher order ionisation constant ${\rm{K}}{{\rm{a}}_{\rm{2}}}\,{\rm{and}}\,{\rm{K}}{{\rm{a}}_{\rm{3}}}$ are smaller than the lower order ionisation constant ${\rm{K}}{{\rm{a}}_{\rm{1}}}$of a polyprotic acid
It is more difficult to remove a positively charged proton from a negative ion due to Coulombic forces.
explanation: Higher order ionisation constants $\mathrm {\left(\mathrm{K} a_{2}\right.}$, $\mathrm {\mathrm{K} a_{3}}$ ) are smaller than the lower order ionisation constant $\mathrm {\left(\mathrm{K} a_{1}\right)}$ of a polyprotic acid. The reason for this is that, it is more difficult to remove a positively charged proton from a negative ion due to electrostatic forces. This can be seen in the case of removing a proton from the uncharged $\mathrm {\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}}$ as compared from a negatively charged $\mathrm {\mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-1}}$. So, the option (3) is correct.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

question:
For a reaction, $\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{s}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{B}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{g})$ the set of all correct statements are
(A) K is independent of [A].
(B) K is dependent on partial pressure of C at a given temperature.
(C) $\Delta \mathrm{H}$ will be independent of temperature.
(D) $\Delta \mathrm{H}$ is independent of the catalyst addition.
explanation:
For the reaction at equilibrium,
${\text{A}}({\text{s}}) \rightleftharpoons {\text{B}}({\text{s}}) + {\text{C}}({\text{g}});{{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}} = [{\text{C}}]$
The concentrations of pure solids, pure liquids, and solvents are omitted from equilibrium constant expressions because they do not change significantly during reactions when enough amount is present to reach equilibrium.
$\Delta \mathrm{H}$ is dependent on temperature, if the reaction is exothermic, and the temperature is increased, the reaction enthalpy becomes more negative (more heat energy is released). If the reaction is endothermic, and the temperature is increased, the reaction enthalpy becomes more positive (more heat energy is absorbed).
$\Delta \mathrm{H}$ is independent of catalyst addition. The catalyst only decreases the activation energy of a reaction, thereby increases the reaction rate but does not affect the enthalpy of reaction.
So, statements (A), (B) and (D) are correct.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Miscellaneous questions on equilibrium constant

question: 1 mole of HI is heated in a closed container of capacity of 2 L . At equilibrium half a mole of HI is dissociated. The equilibrium constant of the reaction is
explanation:

$0.5 \quad 0.25 \quad 0.25$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{I}_{2}\right]}{[\mathrm{HI}]^{2}}$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{\dfrac{0.25 \times 0.25}{2}}{\dfrac{0.5 \times 0.5}{2}}$
$=\dfrac{1}{4}=0.25$

question:
At ${\rm{T}}({\rm{K}}),{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{c}}}$ for the reaction
${\text{A}}{{\text{O}}_2}(\,{\text{g}}) + {\text{B}}{{\text{O}}_2}(\,{\text{g}}) \rightleftharpoons {\text{A}}{{\text{O}}_3}(\,{\text{g}}) + {\text{BO}}({\text{g}})$ is 16 . One mole each of reactants and products are taken in a 1 L flask and heated to $\mathrm{T}(\mathrm{K})$, and equilibrium is established. What is the equilibrium concentration of BO (in $\mathrm{mol} \mathrm{L}^{-1}$ )?
explanation:
${{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}} = \frac{{\left[ {{\text{A}}{{\text{O}}_3}} \right][{\text{BO}}]}}{{\left[ {{\text{A}}{{\text{O}}_2}} \right]\left[ {{\text{B}}{{\text{O}}_2}} \right]}} = 16$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951854/preptracker/questions/q_105072_explanation_1764951854138.png
$\dfrac{(1+\alpha) \times(1+\alpha)}{(1-\alpha)(1-\alpha)}=16 \Rightarrow \dfrac{1+\alpha}{1-\alpha}=4$
$\therefore 1+\alpha=4-4 \alpha \Rightarrow 5 \alpha=3 \Rightarrow \alpha=0.6$.
$\therefore$ Equilibrium concentration of $\mathrm{BO}=1+\alpha$
$=1+0.6=1.6$.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Calculation of Equilibrium Concentration

question: At $\mathrm{T}(\mathrm{K}), \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}$ for the dissociation of $\mathrm{PCl}_{5}$ is $2 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$. The number of moles of $\mathrm{PCl}_{5}$ that must be taken in 1.0 L flask at the same temperature to get 0.2 mol of chlorine at equilibrium is
explanation:
Given, ${{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}} = 2 \times {10^{ - 2}}{\text{mol}}{{\text{L}}^{ - 1}}$
$\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {}&{{\text{BO}}}&{ = 1}& + & \propto \\ {{\text{moles}}\,\,{\text{at}}\,\,{\text{initial}}}&{\text{x}}&0&{}&0 \\ {{\text{moles}}\,\,{\text{at}}\,\,{\text{equilibrium}}}&{{\text{x}} - 0.2}&{0.2}&{}&{0.2} \end{array}$
Equilibrium constant, $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}=\dfrac{\left[\mathrm{PCl}_{3}\right]\left[\mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{PCl}_{5}\right]}$
$2 \times 10^{-2}=\dfrac{[0.2] \times[0.2]}{[\mathrm{x}-0.2]}$
$\mathrm{x}=2.2$ moles
$\therefore$ Number of moles $\mathrm{PCl}_{5}$ taken $=2.2$ moles

question:
At $\mathrm{T}(\mathrm{K})$ the equilibrium constants for the following two reactions are given below$2 \mathrm{~A}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{B}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{g}) ; \mathrm{K}_{1}=16$$2 \mathrm{~B}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{D}(\mathrm{g}) ; \mathrm{K}_{2}=25$
What is the value of equilibrium constant (K) for the reaction given below at $\mathrm{T}(\mathrm{K})$ ?$\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{g})+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{~B}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{D}(\mathrm{g})$
explanation:
$2 \mathrm{~A}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{B}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{g}) ; \mathrm{K}_{1}=16$
${{\text{K}}_1} = \frac{{[{\text{B}}][{\text{C}}]}}{{{{[{\text{A}}]}^2}}} = 16\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
$2 \mathrm{~B}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{D}(\mathrm{g}) ; \mathrm{K}_{2}=25$
${{\text{K}}_2} = \frac{{{{[{\text{D}}]}^2}}}{{[{\text{C}}]{{[{\text{B}}]}^2}}} = 25{\text{ }}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad {\text{(2)}}$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{A}+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{~B}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{D} ; \mathrm{K}_{3}=$ ?
$\mathrm{K}_{3}=\dfrac{[\mathrm{D}]}{[\mathrm{A}][\mathrm{B}]^{1 / 2}}$
$\mathrm{K}_{3}=\sqrt{\mathrm{K}_{2} \times \mathrm{K}_{1}}$
$=\sqrt{\dfrac{[\mathrm{D}]^{2}}{[\mathrm{C}][\mathrm{B}]^{2}} \times \dfrac{[\mathrm{B}][\mathrm{C}]}{[\mathrm{A}]^{2}}}=\sqrt{25 \times 16}$
$\mathrm{K}_{3}=\dfrac{[\mathrm{D}]}{[\mathrm{B}]^{1 / 2}[\mathrm{~A}]}=4 \times 5=20$.

subtopic: Stoichiometric Representation of Equilibrium Constant

question: Match the following columns based on the relationship between equilibrium constant K , reaction quotient Q , and Gibbs free energy $\Delta \mathrm{G}$, and choose the correct options from the codes given below.
explanation:
(A - Q) $\Delta {\text{G}} = 0$ describes the condition of equilibrium where there is no net change in the concentrations of reactants and products.
(B - R) ${\text{Q}} = {\text{K}}$ indicates that the reaction quotient has reached the equilibrium constant's value, signifying that the system is at equilibrium.
(C - S) $\Delta {\text{G}} < 0$ indicates that the reaction is spontaneous and will proceed in the forward direction to form more products.
(D - P) $\Delta {\text{G}} > 0$ shows that the reaction is non-spontaneous, favoring the formation or maintenance of reactants over products. Correct option is (2).

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
A reaction quotient Q greater than the equilibrium constant K indicates that a reaction will proceed in the reverse direction to reach equilibrium.
$\mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{K}$ at equilibrium indicates that the concentrations of reactants and products no longer change.
explanation: Both statements are true. Statement A correctly describes how a reaction will shift to restore equilibrium when Q is greater than K (Le Chatelier's Principle), indicating an excess of products relative to reactants. Statement B correctly states that at equilibrium $(\mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{K})$, the concentrations of reactants and products will remain constant, reflecting a dynamic balance between the forward and reverse reactions. Correct option is (1).

question: Find the value of the equilibrium constant (K) of a reaction at 300 K, when standard Gibbs free energy change is $-25 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ ?  (Consider $\mathrm{R}=8.33 \mathrm{Jmol}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$ )
explanation:
$\Delta {\text{G}}^\circ = - {\text{RT}}\ln {\text{K}} - 25\,{\text{kJ}}\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}$ $ = \left( { - 8.314\,{\text{J}}\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}\,{{\text{K}}^{ - 1}} \times 300\,{\text{K}}} \right)\ln {\text{K}}$
$\ln \mathrm{K}=\left(-\dfrac{25 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}{8.314 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \times 300 \mathrm{~K}}\right)$
$=\dfrac{25 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}{8.314 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \times 300 \mathrm{~K}}$
$\ln \mathrm{K}=-10.02, \mathrm{~K}=\mathrm{e}^{10.02}$
Hence, option (3) is correct.
subject: Chemistry
