question: At 780 K and 10 atmosphere pressure the equilibrium constant for the reaction  $2 \mathrm{~A}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{B}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{g})$ is 3.52 . At the same temperature and 7.04 atmosphere pressure, the equilibrium constant for the same reaction is
explanation: ${{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}}$  depends only on temperature, not on pressure. So $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}$ remains same i.e., $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=3.52$.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between Equilibrium Constant and Total Pressure of Reactions

question: At  ${\rm{T(K)}}$  when one mole of  ${\rm{X}}$  and one mole of  ${\rm{Y}}$  are heated in a 1 L flask, 0.5 moles of Z is formed at the equilibrium. The  ${{\rm{K}}_{\rm{C}}}$  value of the reaction is  $\mathrm{X}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{Y}(\mathrm{g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Z}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{g})$
explanation:
$\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {}&{{\text{X}}({\text{g}}) + {\text{Y}}({\text{g}}) \rightleftharpoons }&{{\text{Z}}({\text{g}}) + {\text{A}}({\text{g}})} \\ {{\text{Initially}}}&{1\,{\text{mole}}\quad \,\,1\,{\text{mole}}}&{0\,\quad \quad \quad 0} \\ \begin{gathered} {\text{At}}\,{\text{equi}} - \hfill \\ {\text{librium}} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} &{(1 - 0.5)\,\,\,\,(1 - 0.5)}&{0.5\,\quad \quad 0.5} \end{array}$
$\therefore$ Equilibrium constant,
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{[\mathrm{Z}][\mathrm{A}]}{[\mathrm{X}][\mathrm{Y}]}=\dfrac{0.5 \times 0.5}{(1-0.5)(1-0.5)}=1$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Calculation of Equilibrium Constant for Homogeneous Equilibrium

question:
$\operatorname{At} \mathrm{T}(\mathrm{K}), \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}$ value for the reaction,
$\dfrac{1}{3} \mathrm{~N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons \dfrac{2}{3} \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})$ is 50. The $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}$ value for the reaction,
$2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ at the same temperature is
explanation:
The ${{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}}$ for the reaction,
$\frac{1}{3}\,{{\text{N}}_2}({\text{g}}) + {{\text{H}}_2}({\text{g}}) \rightleftharpoons \frac{2}{3}{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_3}({\text{g}})$ is 50 (at ${\text{T}}({\text{K}})).$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]^{2 / 3}}{\left[\mathrm{~N}_{2}\right]^{1 / 3}\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]}=50$
Thus, for reaction, $2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
$\left.\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}_{1}}=\dfrac{\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]^{3}\left[\mathrm{~N}_{2}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]^{2}}\right]$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}_{1}}=\left[\dfrac{1}{\mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{C}}}\right]^{3}=\left(\dfrac{1}{50}\right)^{3}=8 \times 10^{-6}$.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Stoichiometric Representation of Equilibrium Constant

question: For the formation of ammonia gas from its constituent elements, the $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}} / \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}$ is
explanation:
$\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g}) ; \Delta \mathrm{n}=-2$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}(\mathrm{RT})^{\Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}}}$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}(\mathrm{RT})^{-2} \Rightarrow \dfrac{\mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{p}}}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}}=\dfrac{1}{(\mathrm{RT})^{2}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between Equilibrium Constants, \[\rm{K_p}\]  and \[\rm{K_c}\]

question: The $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}$ value at equilibrium of $\mathrm{SO}_{3}$ formation reaction from $\mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ and $\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ is $5 \mathrm{~atm}^{-1}$. What is the equilibrium partial pressure of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ if the equilibrium pressure of $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{SO}_{3}$ are equal?
explanation:
The given reaction is
$\underset{(\mathrm{g})}{2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}}+\underset{(\mathrm{g})}{\mathrm{O}_{2}} \rightleftharpoons \underset{(\mathrm{~g})}{2 \mathrm{SO}_{3}}$
$\therefore \quad {{\text{K}}_{\text{P}}} = \frac{{{{\left[ {{{\text{p}}_{{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_3}}}} \right]}^2}}}{{{{\left[ {{{\text{p}}_{{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_2}}}} \right]}^2}\left[ {{{\text{p}}_{{{\text{O}}_2}}}} \right]}}$
$\mathrm{As},\left[\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{SO}_{3}}\right]=\left[\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{SO}_{2}}\right]$
So, $\quad 5=\dfrac{1}{\left[\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}\right]} \Rightarrow\left[\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}\right]=\dfrac{1}{5}=0.2 \mathrm{~atm}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Calculation of Equilibrium Concentration

question: For the formation of ammonia from its constituent elements (1 mole of $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ and 3 moles of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$) in a closed vessel of volume $\mathrm{V}(\mathrm{L})$, the value of $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}$ is [units of $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\mathrm{mol}^{-2} \mathrm{~L}^{2}$ ]
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Calculation of Equilibrium Constant for Homogeneous Equilibrium

question: Calculate the value of the equilibrium constant  $({{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}})$  for the reaction of oxygen gas oxidising ammonia gas to nitric oxide and water vapour. The pressure of each gas at equilibrium is 0.5 atm.
explanation:
$4 \mathrm{NH}_{3}+5 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 4 \mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g})+6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g})$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=\dfrac{\left(\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{NO}^{2}}\right)^{4}\left(\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}\right)^{6}}{\left(\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}\right)^{5}\left(\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}\right)^{4}}=\dfrac{(0.5)^{4}(0.5)^{6}}{(0.5)^{5}(0.5)^{4}}=0.5 \mathrm{~atm}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Calculation of Equilibrium Constant for Homogeneous Equilibrium

question: For the reaction $\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{B}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{g}), \mathrm{A}$ is $33 \%$ dissociated at a total pressure p.   The correct relation between p and  ${{\text{K}}_{\text{P}}}$  is
explanation:
$\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {}&{{\text{A}} \rightleftharpoons }&{\text{B}}& + &{\text{C}} \\ {{\text{Initial}}\,\,{\text{moles}}}&1& - &{}& - \\ \begin{gathered} {\text{Equilibrium}} \hfill \\ {\text{moles}} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} &{\left( {1 - 0.33} \right)}&{0.33}&{}&{0.33} \end{array}$
Total moles $=(1-0.33)+0.33+0.33=1.33$
$\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}=\mathrm{P}_{\text {Total }} \times \dfrac{0.67}{1.33}$
$\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}=\mathrm{P}_{\text {Total }} \times \dfrac{0.33}{1.33}$
$\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{C}}=\mathrm{P}_{\text {Total }} \times \dfrac{0.33}{1.33}$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{P}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{C}}}{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{P}_{\text {Total }} \times \dfrac{0.33}{1.33} \times \mathrm{P}_{\text {Total }} \times \dfrac{0.33}{1.33}}{\mathrm{P}_{\text {Total }} \times \dfrac{0.67}{1.33}}$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{P}}=\mathrm{P}_{\text {Total }} \times 0.125$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{P}_{\text {Total }}=\mathrm{p}=8 \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{p}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between Equilibrium Constant and Total Pressure of Reactions

question: The equilibrium constant  $\left( {{{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}}} \right)$  for the formation of ammonia from its constituent elements at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $1.2 \times 10^{-4}$ and at $127^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $0.60 \times 10^{-4}$. Calculate the mean heat of formation of ammonia per mole in this temperature range.
explanation:
$\log \dfrac{\mathrm{K}_{2}}{\mathrm{~K}_{1}}=\dfrac{\Delta \mathrm{H}}{2.303 \mathrm{R}}\left[\dfrac{\mathrm{T}_{2}-\mathrm{T}_{1}}{\mathrm{~T}_{1} \mathrm{~T}_{2}}\right]$
$\log \dfrac{0.6 \times 10^{-4}}{1.2 \times 10^{-4}}=\dfrac{\Delta \mathrm{H}}{2.303 \times 2}\left[\dfrac{400-300}{400 \times 300}\right]$
$\log 0.5=\dfrac{\Delta \mathrm{H} \times 100}{2.303 \times 2 \times 400 \times 300}$
$\Delta \mathrm{H}=\dfrac{-0.3010 \times 2.303 \times 2 \times 400 \times 300}{100}$
( $\log 0.5=-0.3010$ )
$\Delta {\text{H}} = - 826.4\,\,{\text{cal}}.$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Effect of Temperature on the Equilibrium Constant

question: For a reaction, $2 \mathrm{~A} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{~B}+\mathrm{C}, \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}$ is $2 \times 10^{-3}$. At a given time, the reaction mixture has$[\mathrm{A}]=[\mathrm{B}]=[\mathrm{C}]=3 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{M}$. Which of the following options is correct?
explanation:
For the reaction, the reaction quotient ${{\text{Q}}_{\text{C}}}$ is given by
$\mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{[\mathrm{B}][\mathrm{C}]}{[\mathrm{A}]^{2}}$
As, $[\mathrm{A}]=[\mathrm{B}]=[\mathrm{C}]=3 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{M}$
$\mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{\left(3 \times 10^{-4}\right)\left(3 \times 10^{-4}\right)}{\left(3 \times 10^{-4}\right)^{2}}=1$
As, $\mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{C}}>\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}$,
The reaction will proceed in the reverse direction.
So, correct option is (2).

options.text: The reaction proceeds to the left
The reaction proceeds to the right

subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Predicting the Direction of the Reaction: Reaction Quotient (Q)

question: The equilibrium constant for the reaction is $\mathrm{P}_{4}(\mathrm{~s})+5 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{P}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{10}(\mathrm{~s})$
explanation:
Given, $\mathrm{P}_{4}(\mathrm{~s})+5 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{P}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{10}(\mathrm{~s})$
Concentration of solid compound is 1 .
$\therefore \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{1}{\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]^{5}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Types, Expressions, and Units of Equilibrium Constant

question: For the reaction, $\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{I}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{HI}(\mathrm{g})$
explanation:
$\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{I}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{HI}(\mathrm{g}) ; \Delta \mathrm{n}=0$
Relationship between ${{\text{K}}_{\text{P}}}$ and ${{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}}$
${{\text{K}}_{\text{P}}} = {{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}}{({\text{RT}})^{\Delta {\text{n}}}}$
$\therefore \quad {{\text{K}}_{\text{P}}} = {{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}},$ but not equal to zero
$\therefore \quad {{\text{K}}_{\text{P}}} = {{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}} \ne 0$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between Equilibrium Constants, \[\rm{K_p}\]  and \[\rm{K_c}\]

question: For the given equilibrium reaction, $2 \mathrm{~A}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{~B}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{g})$ the equilibrium constant   $({{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}})$  at 800 K is $4 \times 10^{-4}$. Calculate  ${{\text{K}}_{\text{P}}}$  for the reaction at 800 K temperature
explanation:
$2 \mathrm{~A}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{~B}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{g}) ; \Delta \mathrm{n}=3-2=1$
${{\text{K}}_{\text{P}}} = {{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}} \cdot {({\text{RT}})^{\Delta {{\text{n}}_{\text{g}}}}}$
${{\text{K}}_{\text{P}}} = 4 \times {10^{ - 4}}{(0.0821 \times 800)^1} = 0.026$
Hence, correct option is (2).
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between Equilibrium Constants, \[\rm{K_p}\]  and \[\rm{K_c}\]

question: If the equilibrium constant for the reaction, $2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{SO}_{3}$ is 64 at 500 K, then the equilibrium constant for the reaction  $\mathrm{SO}_{3} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{SO}_{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ at the same temperature is
explanation:
When the reaction is reversed, equilibrium constant $=\dfrac{1}{\mathrm{~K}}$
and then if it is made half, then equation constant $=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{~K}}}$
$\therefore$ Equilibrium constant $=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{64}}=\dfrac{1}{8}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Stoichiometric Representation of Equilibrium Constant

question: What is the equilibrium constant $\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}\right)$ for the given reaction?  $\mathrm{N}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}$  Where the equilibrium concentration of $\mathrm{N}_{2}, \mathrm{O}_{2}$ and NO are found to be $4 \times 10^{-3}, 3 \times 10^{-3}$ and $3 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M}$ respectively.
explanation:
$\mathrm{N}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{[\mathrm{NO}]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{~N}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]}=\dfrac{\left(3 \times 10^{-3}\right)^{2}}{\left(4 \times 10^{-3}\right)\left(3 \times 10^{-3}\right)}=0.75$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Calculation of Equilibrium Constant for Homogeneous Equilibrium

question: For the given equilibrium reaction $\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{I}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{HI}(\mathrm{g})$ Choose the correct equation to calculate  ${{\text{K}}_{\text{P}}}.$
explanation:
${{\text{K}}_{\text{P}}} = {{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}}{({\text{RT}})^{\Delta {{\text{n}}_{\text{g}}}}}$
For, $\mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{I}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{HI} ; \Delta \mathrm{n}=0$
$\therefore \quad {{\text{K}}_{\text{P}}} = {{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between Equilibrium Constants, \[\rm{K_p}\]  and \[\rm{K_c}\]

question: For a given reaction, $2 \mathrm{~A} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{~B}+\mathrm{C}$, the equilibrium constant is $2 \times 10^{-3}$. If at any given time the composition of the reaction mixture is $[\mathrm{A}]=[\mathrm{B}]=[\mathrm{C}]=6 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{M}$; predict in which direction the reaction will proceed and the correct value for reaction quotient.
explanation:
$2 \mathrm{~A} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{~B}+\mathrm{C}$
Reaction quotient,
$\mathrm{Q}=\dfrac{[\mathrm{B}][\mathrm{C}]}{[\mathrm{A}]^{2}}=\dfrac{\left(6 \times 10^{-5}\right) \times\left(6 \times 10^{-5}\right)}{\left(6 \times 10^{-5}\right)^{2}}=1$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{Q}=1>\mathrm{K}_{\text {eq }}=2 \times 10^{-3}$
So, the reaction will proceed in backward direction.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Predicting the Direction of the Reaction: Reaction Quotient (Q)

question: For a reversible reaction $\mathrm{A} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{B}$,  pre - exponential factor is same for both the forward and backward reactions and has value of $20 \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. If the enthalpy change along the forward reaction is $-41.5 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$, the value of equilibrium constant at 500 K is
explanation:
For the forward reaction of $\mathrm{A} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{B}$,
$\ln \mathrm{K}=-\dfrac{\Delta \mathrm{H}}{\mathrm{RT}} \quad$ [van't Hoff isotherm equation]
$\Delta \mathrm{H}=$ Enthalpy change of the forward reaction
$=-41.5 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
$\mathrm{R}=8.314 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}, \mathrm{~T}=500 \mathrm{~K}$
$\mathrm{K}=$ Equilibrium constant
$\mathrm{K}=\mathrm{e}^{-\Delta \mathrm{H} / \mathrm{RT}}=\mathrm{e}^{\frac{-(-41.5)}{8.314 \times 10^{-3} \times 500}}=\mathrm{e}^{9.983} \approx \mathrm{e}^{10}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Effect of Temperature on the Equilibrium Constant

question: For the reaction, $0.5 \mathrm{C}(\mathrm{s})+0.5 \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g})$ the equilibrium pressure is 12 atm . If   $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ conversion is $50 \%$, the value of   ${{\text{K}}_{\text{P}}}{\text{,}}$   in atm is
explanation:
$\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\frac{1}{2}\,\,{\text{C}} + }&{\frac{1}{2}\,\,{\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_2}}&{ \to {\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_2}} \\ {{\text{Initial}}}&{0.5}& - \\ {{\text{At}}\,{\text{equilibrium}}}&\begin{gathered} 0.5 - 0.25 \hfill \\ = 0.25 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} &\begin{gathered} 0.5\,\,({\text{As}}\,50\,\% \hfill \\ {\text{only}}\,\,{\text{converted}}) \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \end{array}$
Total moles $=0.75$
$\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}=\dfrac{0.25}{0.75} \times 12=4 \mathrm{~atm}$
$\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{CO}}=\dfrac{0.5}{0.75} \times 12=8 \mathrm{~atm}$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{CO}}}{\left(\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}\right)^{1 / 2}}=\dfrac{8}{(4)^{1 / 2}}=\dfrac{8}{2}=4$.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between Equilibrium Constant and Total Pressure of Reactions

question: For a chemical reaction, the standard Gibbs energy change, $\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}$ is $-7.64 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. What is the value of equilibrium constant $(\mathrm{K})$ ?
explanation: When $\Delta {\text{G}}$ is $-\mathrm{ve}, \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}$ is greater than 1 .
subject: Chemistry
topic: Relationship between Equilibrium Constant, Reaction Quotient and Gibbs Free Energy
subtopic: Relationship between Equilibrium Constant, Reaction Quotient and Gibbs Free Energy

question: Consider the equilibrium, $\mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{I}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{HI}$. Calculate the equilibrium constant of the reverse reaction when the equilibrium concentration of $\mathrm{H}_{2}, \mathrm{I}_{2}$ and HI are $1.14 \times 10^{-2}, 0.12 \times 10^{-2}$ and $2.52 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{molL}^{-1}$, respectively
explanation:
$\mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{I}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{HI}$
Reverse of above reaction is
$2 \mathrm{HI} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{I}_{2}$
Equilibrium constant $(\mathrm{K})=\dfrac{\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{I}_{2}\right]}{[\mathrm{HI}]^{2}}$
$\mathrm{K}=\dfrac{\left[1.14 \times 10^{-2}\right]\left[0.12 \times 10^{-2}\right]}{\left[2.52 \times 10^{-2}\right]^{2}}=0.021$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Stoichiometric Representation of Equilibrium Constant

question: In which of the following plots, an endothermic reaction is correctly represented?
explanation:
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}} \propto \mathrm{T}$ (for endothermic reactions)
Thus, the plot between $\dfrac{1}{\mathrm{~T}}$ and $\ln {{\text{K}}_{\text{P}}}$ shows a downward straight line slope for $\ln {{\text{K}}_{\text{P}}}$.
Hence, option (4) is correct.
subject: Chemistry

question: At 298 K , the equilibrium constant of the process  $1.5{{\text{O}}_2}({\text{g}}) \rightleftharpoons {{\text{O}}_3}({\text{g}})$   is $3 \times 10^{-29}$. Standard free energy change (in $\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$) of the process is approximately   $({\text{R}} = 8.314\,{\text{J}}\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}\,{{\text{K}}^{ - 1}}\,\,;\log 3 = 0.47)$
explanation:
$\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=-2.303 \mathrm{RT} \log \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}$
$\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=-2.303 \times 8.314 \times 10^{-3} \times 298 \log 3 \times 10^{-29}$
$=163 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
subject: Chemistry

question: For a reaction $2 \mathrm{~A}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{~B}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{g})$, $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=3.75 \times 10^{-6}$ at 1069 K. The approximate value of   ${{\text{K}}_{\text{P}}}$    for this reaction at the same temperature is ($\mathrm{R}=0.082 \mathrm{~L}$ bar mol$^{ - 1}\,{{\text{K}}^{ - 1}})$
explanation:
${{\text{K}}_{\text{P}}}$ is related to ${{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}}$ by the formula,
${{\text{K}}_{\text{P}}} = {{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}}{({\text{RT}})^{\Delta {\text{n}}}}$
For the equation, $2{\text{A}}({\text{g}}) \rightleftharpoons 2{\text{B}}({\text{g}}) + {\text{C(g}})\,\,;$
$\Delta \mathrm{n}=(2+1)-2=1$
${{\text{K}}_{\text{P}}} = \left( {3.75 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}} \right){(0.082 \times 1069)^1} = 3.3 \times {10^{ - 4}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between Equilibrium Constants, \[\rm{K_p}\]  and \[\rm{K_c}\]

question: For the formation of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ from $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ at 500 K , the concentration of $\mathrm{N}_{2}, \mathrm{H}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ at equilibrium are $1.5 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{M}, 3.0 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{M}$ and $1.2 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{M}$, respectively. The equilibrium constant for the reverse reaction is
explanation:
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{\left[\mathrm{N}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]^{3}}{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]^{2}}$ (for reverse reaction of formation of ammonia)
$\therefore \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{-2} \times\left(3 \times 10^{-2}\right)^{3}}{\left(1.2 \times 10^{-2}\right)^{2}}=2.81 \times 10^{-3}$.
subject: Chemistry

question:
$\mathrm{PCl}_{5} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{PCl}_{3}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$
If the equilibrium constant $\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}\right)$ for the above reaction at 500 K is 1.79 and the equilibrium concentration of $\mathrm{PCl}_{5}$ and $\mathrm{PCl}_{3}$ are 1.41 M and 1.59 M , respectively, then the concentration of $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ is approximately.
explanation:
Equilibrium constant $\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}\right)=\dfrac{\left[\mathrm{PCl}_{3}\right]\left[\mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{PCl}_{5}\right]}$
$1.79=\dfrac{1.59 \times\left[\mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right]}{1.41}$
$\left[\mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right]=\dfrac{1.79 \times 1.41}{1.59}=1.587 \simeq 1.59 \mathrm{~m}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Calculation of Equilibrium Concentration

question:
For the reaction $\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{~B}(\mathrm{~g})$, the backward reaction rate constant is higher than the forward reaction rate constant by a factor of 2500, at 1000 K.
[Given: $\mathrm{R}=0.0831 \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{~atm} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$ ]
${{\text{K}}_{\text{P}}}$ for the reaction at 1000 K is
explanation:
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{f}}}{\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{b}}}=\dfrac{1}{2500}$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{P}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}(\mathrm{RT})^{\Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}}} \quad\left(\Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}}=2-1=1\right)$
$=\dfrac{1}{2500} \times 0.0831 \times 1000$
$=0.033$
subject: Chemistry

question:
Given:
$\mathrm{NH}_{2} \mathrm{COONH}_{4(\mathrm{~s})} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$
If the partial pressure of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ gas at equilibrium is 0.4 atm and the total pressure is 1 atm , then the value of ${{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}}$ at the same temperature is
explanation:
$\mathrm{NH}_{2} \mathrm{COONH}_{4(\mathrm{~s})} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$
Total pressure at equilibrium $=1 \mathrm{~atm}$
Partial pressure of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ at equilibrium $=0.4 \mathrm{~atm}$
$\therefore$ Partial pressure of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ at equilibrium $=0.6$ atm
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{P}}=\left(\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}\right)^{2}\left(\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{co}_{2}}\right)=(0.6)^{2}(0.4)$
\begin{equation*}=0.144 \mathrm{~atm}^{3}\end{equation*}

question:
For the given reaction, choose the correct expression of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}}}}$ from the following
${\mathrm{\mathrm{\mathrm{Fe}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{3+}+\mathrm{SCN}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{-} \rightleftharpoons\left[(\mathrm{FeSCN})_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{2+}\right]}}}$
explanation:
${\mathrm{\mathrm{\mathrm{Fe}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{3+}+\mathrm{SCN}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{-} \rightleftharpoons\left[(\mathrm{FeSCN})_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{2+}\right]}}}$
${\mathrm{\mathrm{\therefore \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{\left[(\mathrm{FeSCN})^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}\right]\left[\mathrm{SCN}^{-}\right]}}}}$
So, the correct option is (3).

question:
For the given hypothetical reactions, the equilibrium constants are as follows:
${\text{X}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{Y;}}{{\text{K}}_1} = 1.0$
${\text{Y}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{Z}};{{\text{K}}_2} = 2.0$
${\text{Z}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{W}};{{\text{K}}_3} = 4.0$
The equilibrium constant for the reaction, ${\mathrm{\mathrm{\mathrm{X} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{W}}}}$ is
explanation:
By adding given three equations, desired equation is obtained. When equations are added, rate constants are multiplied to get resultant rate constant.
${\mathrm{\mathrm{\mathrm{X} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Y} ; \mathrm{K}_{1}=1.0}}}$
${\mathrm{\mathrm{\mathrm{Y} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Z} ; \mathrm{K}_{2}=2.0}}}$
${\mathrm{\mathrm{\mathrm{Z} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{W} ; \quad \mathrm{K}_{3}=4.0}}}$
${\mathrm{\mathrm{\mathrm{X} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{W} ; \quad \mathrm{K}=\mathrm{K}_{1} \times \mathrm{K}_{2} \times \mathrm{K}_{3}}}}$
${\mathrm{\mathrm{K=1 \times 2 \times 4=8}}}$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Stoichiometric Representation of Equilibrium Constant

question:
The ratio ${\mathrm{\mathrm{\dfrac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}}}}}$ for the reaction,
${\mathrm{\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}_{(\mathrm{g})}+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}}}}$ is
explanation:
${\mathrm{\mathrm{K_{p}=K_{c}(R T)^{\Delta n(g)}}}}$
Here, ${\mathrm{\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}}=1-\left(1+\dfrac{1}{2}\right)=-\dfrac{1}{2}}}}$
${\mathrm{\mathrm{\therefore \dfrac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}}=(\mathrm{RT})^{-\dfrac{1}{2}}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{RT}}}}}}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between Equilibrium Constants, \[\rm{K_p}\]  and \[\rm{K_c}\]

question:
${\mathrm{\mathrm{\mathrm{At}-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}}}$ and 1 atm pressure, a cylinder is filled with equal number of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2}, \mathrm{I}_{2}}}}$ and HI molecules for the reaction,
${\mathrm{\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{I}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{HI}_{(\mathrm{g})}}}}$, the ${\mathrm{\mathrm{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}}}}$ for the process is ${\mathrm{\mathrm{\mathrm{x} \times 10^{-1} . \mathrm{x}=}}}$ ____
[Given : ${\mathrm{\mathrm{\mathrm{R}=0.082 \mathrm{L\,atm} \,\mathrm{K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}}}$ ]
explanation:
${\mathrm{\mathrm{K_{p}=K_{c} \cdot(R T)^{\Delta n}}}}$
$\begin{gathered}\text { as } \Delta \mathrm{n}_{(\mathrm{g})}=0 ; \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}} \\\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{P}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}=\dfrac{[\mathrm{HI}]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{I}_{2}\right]}=1 \text { (or) } 10 \times 10^{-1}\end{gathered}$
$\left[[\mathrm{HI}]=\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]=\left[\mathrm{I}_{2}\right]\right]$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Calculation of Equilibrium Constant for Homogeneous Equilibrium

question:
The equilibrium constant for the reaction,
${\mathrm{\mathrm{\mathrm{SO}_{3(\mathrm{~g})} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{SO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}}}}$ is ${\mathrm{\mathrm{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}=4.9 \times 10^{-2}}}}$.
The value of ${{\rm{K}}_{\rm{c}}}$ for the reaction given below is
${\mathrm{\mathrm{2 \mathrm{SO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{SO}_{3(\mathrm{~g})}}}}$ is
explanation:
${\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_{{\text{3}}(\,{\text{g}})}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_{{\text{2}}(\,{\text{g}})}}{\text{ + }}\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{{\text{2}}(\,{\text{g}})}};{{\text{K}}_{\text{c}}}{\text{ = 4}}.{\text{9}} \times {\text{1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 2}}}}\,(1)$
Reversing and multiplying equation (1) by 2, the desired equation will be obtained
${\mathrm{\mathrm{2 \mathrm{SO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{SO}_{3(\mathrm{~g})} ; \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}^{\prime}=}}}$ ?
$\therefore {{\rm{K}}_c}^\prime {\rm{ = }}\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{{{\left( {{\rm{\,}}{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{c}}}} \right)}^{\rm{2}}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{{{\left( {{\rm{4}}.{\rm{9}} \times {\rm{1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{ - 2}}}}} \right)}^{\rm{2}}}}}$
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{1}}{{\rm{0}}^{\rm{4}}}}}{{{\rm{4}}.{\rm{9}} \times {\rm{4}}.{\rm{9}}}}{\rm{ = 416}}.{\rm{49}}$
So, the correct option is (1).

question:
$\mathrm{SO}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ are introduced into a $3 \mathrm{~L}$ vessel. Partial pressure of $\mathrm{SO}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ at equilibrium are $1 \mathrm{~atm}$ and $2 \mathrm{~atm}$ respectively. The value of ${{\text{K}}_{\text{P}}}$ for the following reaction
$\mathrm{SO}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \text { is } 10$
The total pressure in atm at equilibrium would be ____.
explanation:
$\mathop {{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{C}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}}}\limits_{1{\text{ atm }}} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}}}\limits_{{\text{x}}\,{\text{atm}}} {\mkern 1mu} + \mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}}}\limits_{2\,{\text{atm}}} $

question: For the reaction $\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{g})+3 \mathrm{~B}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 4 \mathrm{C}(\mathrm{g})$, initial concentration of $\mathrm{A}$ is equal to that of B. If the equilibrium concentration of $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{C}$ are equal then $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}$ of the reaction is ____.
explanation:
$\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {}&{{\text{A}}({\text{g}}){\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{ }} + {\text{ }}}&{3{\text{B}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} ({\text{g}}) \rightleftharpoons }&{4{\text{C}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} ({\text{g}})} \\ {{\text{t}} = 0}&{\text{a}}&{\text{a}}&0 \\ {{{\text{t}}_{{\text{eq }}}}}&{{\text{a}} - {\text{x}}}&{{\text{a}} - 3{\text{x}}}&{4{\text{x}}} \end{array}$
${\text{given}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{a}} - {\text{x}} = 4{\text{x}}$
${\text{a}} = 5{\text{x}}$
${{\text{K}}_{\text{c}}} = \frac{{{{[{\text{C}}]}^4}}}{{[{\text{A}}]\,\,{{[{\text{B}}]}^3}}} = \frac{{{{[4{\text{x}}]}^4}}}{{[4{\text{x}}]\,\,{{[2{\text{x}}]}^3}}} = \frac{{256}}{{32}} = 8$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Calculation of Equilibrium Constant for Homogeneous Equilibrium

question:
Given
The following data at $298 \mathrm{~K}$ :
The equilibrium constants for the following reactions,
$2 \mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
and $\mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g})+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{Br}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{NOBr}(\mathrm{g})$
are $2.4 \times 10^{30}$ and 1.4 respectively.
${{\rm{K}}_{\rm{C}}}$ for the reaction is ____.
$\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{~N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{Br}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{NOBr}(\mathrm{g})$
explanation:
The net reaction is,
$\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{~N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{Br}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{NOBr}(\mathrm{g})$
${{\text{K}}_{{\text{c(net)}}}} = \frac{{{\text{[NOBr]}}}}{{{{\left[ {{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}} \right]}^{1/2}}{{\left[ {{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}} \right]}^{1/2}}{{\left[ {{\text{B}}{{\text{r}}_{\text{2}}}} \right]}^{1/2}}}}$
Considering the given equation:
$2 \mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$; equilibrium constant $=2.4 \times 10^{30}$
$\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g})$; equilibrium constant $=\dfrac{1}{2.4 \times 10^{30}}$
$\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{~N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g}) ;$
Equilibrium constant $ = {\left( {\frac{1}{{2.4 \times {{10}^{30}}}}} \right)^{1/2}}$ $ = 0.6455 \times {10^{ - 15}}$
$\dfrac{[\mathrm{NO}]}{\left[\mathrm{N}_{2}\right]^{1 / 2}\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]^{1 / 2}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}^{\prime}=0.6455 \times 10^{-15}$
$\mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g})+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{Br}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{NOBr}(\mathrm{g})$
$\frac{{[{\text{NOBr}}]}}{{[{\text{NO}}]\,\,{{[{\text{B}}{{\text{r}}_{\text{2}}}]}^{1/2}}}} = {\text{K}}_{\text{c}}^{\prime \prime } = 1.4$
Multiplying both equations
$\frac{{[{\text{NO}}]}}{{{{[{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}]}^{1/2}}{{[{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}]}^{1/2}}}} \times \frac{{[{\text{NOBr}}]}}{{[{\text{NO}}]\,\,{{[{\text{B}}{{\text{r}}_{\text{2}}}]}^{1/2}}}} = {\text{K}}_{\text{c}}^\prime \times {\text{K}}_{\text{c}}^{\prime \prime }$
$ = 0.6455 \times {10^{ - 15}} \times 1.4$
${\text{ or }}\frac{{[{\text{NOBr}}]}}{{{{[{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}]}^{1/2}}{{[{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}]}^{1/2}}{{[{\text{B}}{{\text{r}}_{\text{2}}}]}^{1/2}}}}$
$ = {{\text{K}}_{{\text{c}}\,\,{\text{(net )}}}} = 0.9037 \times {10^{ - 15}}$
$ = 9.037 \times {10^{ - 16}}$$ \approx 9 \times {10^{ -1 6}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Calculation of Equilibrium Constant for Heterogeneous Equilibrium

question: The equilibrium concentrations of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}}}$ and C for the reaction ${\mathrm{\mathrm{A}_{(\mathrm{g})} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{B}_{(\mathrm{g})}+\mathrm{C}_{(\mathrm{g})}}}$ are ${\mathrm{4.6 \mathrm{M}, 2.3 \mathrm{M}}}$ and 2.3 M respectively at 280 K . Find the value of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}}}$ at the same temperature.
explanation: ${\mathrm{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}=\dfrac{[\mathrm{B}][\mathrm{C}]}{[\mathrm{A}]}=\dfrac{2.3 \times 2.3}{4.6}=1.15}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Calculation of Equilibrium Constant for Homogeneous Equilibrium

question:
Consider the following equilibrium reaction:${\mathrm{\mathrm{AB}_{(\mathrm{s})} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{A}_{(\mathrm{g})}+\mathrm{B}_{(\mathrm{g})} ; \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=144}}$
Total pressure at equilibrium is ' ${\mathrm{x}}$ ' times the pressure of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{B}_{(\mathrm{g})}}}$. The value of ' ${\mathrm{x}}$ ' is ____ .
explanation:
${\text{A}}{{\text{B}}_{{\text{(s)}}}} \rightleftharpoons {{\text{A}}_{({\text{g}})}}{\text{ + }}{{\text{B}}_{({\text{g}})}}$
${\mathrm{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}} \times \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}=144}}$
At equilibrium, ${\mathrm{P_{A}=P_{B}}}$
${\mathrm{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}{ }^{2}=144}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}=12 \mathrm{~atm}}}$
Total pressure ${\mathrm{=\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}+\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}=12+12=24 \mathrm{~atm}}}$
${\mathrm{\dfrac{\text { Total pressure }}{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\dfrac{24}{12}=2}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore}}$ Value of ${\mathrm{x=2}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between Equilibrium Constant and Total Pressure of Reactions

question: The equilibrium concentration of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{HI}, \mathrm{H}_{2}}}$ and ${\mathrm{\mathrm{I}_{2}}}$ are ${\mathrm{0.80,0.10}}$ and ${\mathrm{0.10 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}}}$ respectively. The equilibrium constant for the reaction ${\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{I}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{HI}_{(\mathrm{g})}}}$ will be ____ .
explanation: ${\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{I}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{HI}_{(\mathrm{g})}}}$ :$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}=\dfrac{[\mathrm{HI}]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{I}_{2}\right]}=\dfrac{0.80 \times 0.80}{0.10 \times 0.10}=64$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Calculation of Equilibrium Constant for Homogeneous Equilibrium

question: For the reaction, ${\mathrm{\mathrm{C}_{(\mathrm{s})}+\mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{CO}_{(\mathrm{g})}}}$. ${\mathrm{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=63 \mathrm{~atm}}}$ at 1000 K . If at equilibrium, partial pressure of CO is ten times that of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}}$, then the total pressure of the gases at equilibrium is ____ atm.
explanation:
${\mathrm{\mathrm{C}_{(\mathrm{s})}+\mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{CO}_{(\mathrm{g})} ; \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=63 \mathrm{~atm}}}$
${\mathrm{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=\dfrac{\left(\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{CO}}\right)^{2}}{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}}=63}}$
Now,
${\mathrm{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{CO}}=10 \times \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}}}$ at equilibrium
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad 63=\dfrac{\left(10 \times \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}\right)^{2}}{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}}}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore 63=100 \times \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}=0.63 \mathrm{~atm}}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad P_{\mathrm{CO}}=10 \times 0.63=6.30 \mathrm{~atm}}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad}}$ Total pressure ${\mathrm{=\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}+\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{CO}}}}$
$\begin{aligned}& =0.63+6.30 \\& =6.93 \mathrm{~atm}\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between Equilibrium Constant and Total Pressure of Reactions

question: The equilibrium constant at 298 K for a reaction ${\mathrm{\mathrm{A}_{(\mathrm{g})}+\mathrm{B}_{(\mathrm{g})} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{C}_{(\mathrm{g})}+\mathrm{D}_{(\mathrm{g})}}}$ is 100 . If the initial concentration of all the four species were 1 M each, then equilibrium concentration of ${\mathrm{D}}$ (in ${\mathrm{\mathrm{mol} \mathrm{L}^{-1}}}$ ) will be ____ .
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764959160/preptracker/questions/q_19070_explanation_1764959159729.png ${\mathrm{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}} \quad=\dfrac{[\mathrm{C}][\mathrm{D}]}{[\mathrm{A}][\mathrm{B}]}}}$
${\mathrm{100=\dfrac{[1+x][1+x]}{[1-x][1-x]}}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad 100=\dfrac{[1+x]^{2}}{[1-x]^{2}}}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad 10=\dfrac{[1+x]}{[1-x]}}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad[1+x]=10-10 x}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad x+10 x=10-1=9}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad x=\dfrac{9}{11}=0.82}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore}}$ Equilibrium concentration of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{D}=1+x}}$
${\mathrm{=1+0.82=1.82 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Calculation of Equilibrium Concentration

question:
At constant temperature, the equilibrium constant ${\text{(}}\,{{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}})$ for decomposition reaction $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}$ is expressed by ${{\rm{K}}_{\rm{p}}} = \left( {4{{\rm{x}}^{\rm{2}}}{\rm{P}}} \right)/\left( {1 - {{\rm{x}}^2}} \right)$, where, P = pressure;

explanation:
${{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{2N}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}$
${{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}} = \frac{{4{{\text{x}}^{\text{2}}}{\text{P}}}}{{1 - {{\text{x}}^{\text{2}}}}}$
${{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}}$ of an equilibrium reaction depends only on temperature. However, the degree of dissociation will change in accordance with Le Chatelier's principle.

options.text: ${{\rm{K}}_{\rm{p}}}$ remains constant with change in P and X.

question: The standard Gibb's free energy change, $\Delta {\text{G}}^\circ $ is related to equilibrium constant, ${{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}}$ as
explanation:
Standard free energy change, $\Delta {\text{G}}^\circ $ and equilibrium constant ${{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}}$ are related as
$\Delta {\text{G}}^\circ = - {\text{RT}}\,\,{\text{ln}}\,\,{{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}}$
${\text{ln}}\,\,{{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}} = - \frac{{\Delta {\text{G}}^\circ }}{{{\text{RT}}}} \Rightarrow {{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}} = {{\text{e}}^{ - \Delta {{\text{G}}^{\text{^\circ }}}{\text{/RT}}}}$

question:
For the reaction,
$2{{\text{A}}_{({\text{g}})}} + {{\text{B}}_{2({\text{g}})}} \rightleftharpoons 2{\text{A}}{{\text{B}}_{({\text{g}})}}$
the equilibrium constant, ${{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}}$ at $300 \mathrm{~K}$ is 16.0. The value of ${{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}}$ for ${\text{A}}{{\text{B}}_{2({\text{g}})}} \rightleftharpoons {{\text{A}}_{({\text{g}})}} + 1/2{{\text{B}}_{2({\text{g}})}}$ is
explanation:
${\rm{2}}{{\rm{A}}_{{\rm{(g)}}}}{\rm{ + }}{{\rm{B}}_{{\rm{2(g)}}}}{\rm{2A}}{{\rm{B}}_{{\rm{(g)}}}}$
${{\rm{K}}_{\rm{p}}} = 16.0$
On reversing the equation,
$2{\text{A}}{{\text{B}}_{{\text{(g)}}}} \rightleftharpoons 2{{\text{A}}_{{\text{(g)}}}} + {{\text{B}}_{{\text{2(g)}}}}$
${\text{K}}_{\text{p}}^{\text{'}} = \frac{1}{{{{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}}}} = \frac{1}{{16}}$
Dividing the equation by 2
${\text{A}}{{\text{B}}_{{\text{(g)}}}} \rightleftharpoons {{\text{A}}_{{\text{(g)}}}} + \frac{1}{2}{{\text{B}}_{{\text{2(g)}}}}$
${\text{K}}_p^{\prime \prime } = \sqrt {{\text{K}}_p^\prime } = \sqrt {\frac{1}{{16}}} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Stoichiometric Representation of Equilibrium Constant

question: A vessel at $1000 \mathrm{~K}$ contains $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ with a pressure of $0.5 \mathrm{~atm}$. Some of the $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ is converted into $\mathrm{CO}$ on the addition of graphite. The value of K if the total pressure at equilibrium is 0.8 atm, is
explanation:
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_{2({\text{g}})}} + {{\text{C}}_{{\text{(s)}}}} \rightleftharpoons 2{\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_{{\text{(g)}}}}$
$\mathop {{\text{Initial}}}\limits_{{\text{pressure}}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,0.5{\text{atm}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,$
${\text{At}}\,\,{\text{equi}}.{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} (0.5 - {\text{x}}){\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} 2{\text{x}}$
$\therefore (0.5 - {\text{x}}) + 2{\text{x}} = 0.8$
${\rm{x}} = 0.3\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{atm}}$
$\therefore \,\,\,{{\text{p}}_{{\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}}} = 0.5 - 0.3 = 0.2\,{\text{atm}}$
${{\text{p}}_{{\text{CO}}}} = 2x = 2 \times 0.3 = 0.6\,{\text{atm}}$
${\text{K}} = \frac{{{{\left( {{{\text{p}}_{{\text{CO}}}}} \right)}^2}}}{{{{\text{p}}_{{\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}}}}} = \frac{{{{(0.6)}^2}}}{{0.2}} = 1.8\,{\text{atm}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between Equilibrium Constant and Total Pressure of Reactions

question:
Match Column I with Column II and choose the correct combination from the options given
Match Column I (Equations) with Column II (Types of process) and select the correct option.
explanation:
(1) When ${{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}} > {\text{Q}},$ the reaction goes in forward direction, i.e., the reaction is spontaneous.
(2)$\Delta {\text{G}} = \Delta {\text{G}}^\circ + {\text{RT}}\,\,{\text{ln}}Q,\Delta {\text{G}}^\circ < {\text{RT}}\,\,{\text{ln}}\,{\text{Q}}$, thus, $\Delta {\text{G}} = + {\text{ve}}$ and hence, the reaction is non-spontaneous.
(3) At equilibrium, ${{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}} = {\text{Q}}$
(4) ${\text{T}} > \frac{{\Delta {\text{H}}}}{{\Delta {\text{S}}}}$ or ${\text{T}}\Delta {\text{S}} > \Delta {\text{H}}$
This condition is true for spontaneous, endothermic reactions $({\text{as}}\,\,\Delta {\text{G}} = \Delta {\text{H}} - {\text{T}}\Delta {\text{S}})$.

If $\mathrm{K}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{K}_{2}$ are respective equilibrium constants for the two reactions
$\mathrm{XeF}_{6}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{XeOF}_{4}(\mathrm{~g})+2 \mathrm{HF}(\mathrm{g})$
$\mathrm{XeO}_{4}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{XeF}_{6}(\mathrm{~g})$
$\rightleftharpoons \mathrm{XeOF}_{4}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{XeO}_{3} \mathrm{~F}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$

question:
${\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_4}{\text{COON}}{{\text{H}}_2}({\text{s}}) \rightleftharpoons 2{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_3}({\text{g}}) + {\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_2}({\text{g}})$
If equilibrium pressure is $3 \mathrm{~atm}$ for the above reaction. ${\text{Kp}}$ for the reaction is
explanation:
${\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_4}{\text{COON}}{{\text{H}}_2}(\,{\text{s}}) \rightleftharpoons 2{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_3}(\,{\text{g}}) + {\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g}})$
$\therefore \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}^{2} \times \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}}{\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{COONH}_{2}}(\mathrm{~s})}$
But, $\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{COONH}_{2}}=1$
(since, $\mathrm{p}$ of solids is taken as one)
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}^{2} \times \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}$
where, $\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}=\dfrac{2}{3} \times 3$
and $\quad \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}=\dfrac{1}{3} \times 3=1$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=(2)^{2} \times 1=4$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Calculation of Equilibrium Constant for Heterogeneous Equilibrium

question: For the reaction $1 \mathrm{~g}$ moles of $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$ is enclosed in $5 \mathrm{~L}$ container${\rm{CaC}}{{\rm{O}}_3}({\rm{s}}) \to {\rm{CaO}}({\rm{s}}) + {\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_2}({\rm{g}})\,\,,\,{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{p}}} = 1.16$ at $1073 \mathrm{~K}$, then percentage dissociation of $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$ is
explanation:
$\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s}) \rightarrow \mathrm{CaO}$ (s) $+\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$,
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}$
( $\therefore$ only gaseous molecule count)
${{\rm{K}}_{\rm{p}}} = {{\rm{K}}_{\rm{C}}}{{\rm{(RT)}}^{{\rm{\Delta n}}}}$
Then, moles of $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$, dissociated is
$\mathrm{x} =$ percentage dissociation
$1.16=\dfrac{x}{5}(0.0821 \times 1073)^{1}$
$\begin{aligned}x & =\dfrac{1.16 \times 5}{0.0821 \times 1073}=\dfrac{5.8}{88.1}=0.0658 \\& =0.0658 \times 100=6.58 \%=6.5 \%\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry

Concentration of
$\left[ {{\rm{N}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}} \right] = \frac{{{\rm{number}}\,{\rm{of}}\,{\rm{moles}}}}{{{\rm{volume}}\,({\rm{in}}\,{\rm{L}})}} = \frac{6}{{10}} = 0.6{\rm{2}}$

${\rm{Initial}}\,{\rm{mode}}\quad {\rm{0}}.{\rm{6}}\quad {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{0}}\quad {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,\,\,{\rm{0}}$
${\rm{At}}\,{\rm{equil}}{\rm{.}}\quad {\rm{0}}.{\rm{6}} - {\rm{2x}}\quad{\rm{x}}\quad {\rm{3x}}$
Then, concentration of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ at equilibrium will be
$0.6-2 \mathrm{x}$

question: Formaldehyde polymerises to form glucose according to the reaction, $6 \mathrm{HCHO} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}$. The theoretically computed equilibrium constant for this reaction is found to be $6 \times 10^{22}$. If $1 \mathrm{M}$ solution of glucose dissociates according to the above equilibrium, the concentration of formaldehyde in the solution will be
explanation:
$6 \mathrm{HCHO} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}$
A very high value of $\mathrm{K}$ for the given equilibrium shows that dissociation of glucose to form HCHO is very-very small. Hence, at equilibrium,
$\left[\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}\right]=1 \mathrm{M}$
${\rm{K}} = \frac{{\left[ {{{\rm{C}}_6}{{\rm{H}}_{12}}{{\rm{O}}_6}} \right]}}{{{{[{\rm{HCHO}}]}^6}}}{\rm{ }}$
${\rm{i}}{\rm{.e}}{\rm{. }}6 \times {10^{22}} = \frac{1}{{{{[{\rm{HCHO}}]}^6}}}$
${\rm{or }}\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} [{\rm{HCHO}}] = {\left( {\frac{1}{{6 \times {{10}^{22}}}}} \right)^{1/6}}$
$ = 1.6 \times {10^{ - 4}}{\rm{M}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Calculation of Equilibrium Concentration

question:
${{\rm{K}}_{\rm{p}}}$ and ${{\rm{K}}_{\rm{p}}}^{\rm{'}}$ are the equilibrium constants of the two reactions, given below.
${\frac{1}{2}\,{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}({\text{g}}) + \frac{3}{2}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}({\text{g}}) \rightleftharpoons {\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}({\text{g}})}$

$\because $${{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}}$ and ${{\rm{K}}_{\rm{p}}}^{\rm{'}}$ are related by
explanation:
When a balanced equation having equilibrium constant ${{\rm{K}}_{\rm{p}}}$ is multiplied by a certain value n, the equilibrium constant for the new equation will be equal to ${{\rm{(}}{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{p}}}{\rm{)}}^{\rm{n}}}$.
In the given equation, $\mathrm{n}=2$
So, $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}^{\prime}=\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}\right)^{2}$ or $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=\sqrt{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}^{\prime}}$

question:
What is ${{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}}$ for the following equilibrium when equilibrium concentration of each substance is $\left[\mathrm{SO}_{2}\right]=0.60 \mathrm{M},\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]=0.82 \mathrm{M}$ and $\left[\mathrm{SO}_{3}\right]=1.90 \mathrm{M}$ ?
${\text{2S}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}} + {{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}} \rightleftharpoons 2{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{(g)}}$
explanation:
For equation,
$\begin{aligned}& 2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{SO}_{3}(\mathrm{~g}) \\& \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{\left[\mathrm{SO}_{3}\right]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{SO}_{2}\right]^{2}\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]} \\& \quad=\dfrac{[1.90 \mathrm{M}]^{2}}{[0.60 \mathrm{M}]^{2}[0.82 \mathrm{M}]} \\& \quad=12.229 \mathrm{M}^{-1} \text { or } 12.229\, \mathrm{L\,mol}^{-1}\end{aligned}$

question:
For the reaction,
${\rm{AB(g}}) \to {\rm{A(g}}) + {\rm{B(g}}),$
${\rm{AB}}\,{\rm{is}}\,{\mkern 1mu} 33\% $ dissociated at a total pressure of p. Therefore, p is related to ${{\rm{K}}_{\rm{p}}}$ by one of the following options.
explanation:
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,$$\mathrm{AB}(\mathrm{g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{A}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{B}(\mathrm{g})$
Initially
$\begin{array}{lcc}1 & 0 & 0 \\1-0.33 & 0.33 & 0.33\end{array}$
Final pressure after $33 \%$ dissociation $=0.67$
Total pressure at equilibrium
$\begin{aligned}& =1-0.33+0.33+0.33 \\\Rightarrow \quad & =1+0.33=1.33 \\\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{A}} & =\dfrac{0.33}{1.33} \mathrm{p} \Rightarrow \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{B}}=\dfrac{0.33}{1.33} \mathrm{p} \\\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{AB}} & =\dfrac{0.67}{1.63} \mathrm{p}\end{aligned}$
(where, $\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{A}}, \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{B}}$ and $\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{AB}}$ are partial pressures of $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}$ and $\mathrm{AB}$ )
$\begin{aligned}\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}} & =\dfrac{\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{A}} \cdot \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{AB}}} \\& =\dfrac{\dfrac{0.33}{1.33} \mathrm{p} \cdot \dfrac{0.33}{1.33} \mathrm{p}}{\dfrac{0.67}{1.33} \mathrm{p}}=\dfrac{(0.33)^{2} \mathrm{p}}{1.33 \times 0.67} \\& =\dfrac{0.1089 \times \mathrm{p}}{0.8911} \\\mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{p}} & =0.122 \mathrm{p} \\\Rightarrow \mathrm{p} & =\dfrac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}}{0.122}=8 \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{p}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Calculation of Equilibrium Constant for Homogeneous Equilibrium

question: 28g  of $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ and $6 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ were mixed. At equilibrium $17 \mathrm{~g}$ $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ was produced. The weights of $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ at equilibrium are respectively
explanation:
$\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {}&{{{\text{N}}_2}}& + &{3{{\text{H}}_2}}& \rightleftharpoons &{2{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_3}} \\ {{\text{Initially}}{\mkern 1mu} }&{{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} 28\,{\text{g}}}&{}&{6\,{\text{g}}}&{}&{0{\mkern 1mu} } \\ {}&{{\mkern 1mu} {\text{1 mole}}}&{}&{{\mkern 1mu} {\text{3 mole}}{\mkern 1mu} }&{}&{{\mkern 1mu} {\text{2 mole}}} \\ {{\text{At}}{\mkern 1mu} {\text{equil}}}&{(1 - 0.5)}&{}&{{\mkern 1mu} (3 - 1.5)}&{}&{17{\mkern 1mu} {\text{g}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}} \end{array}$
Concentration of 1 mole of $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ combines with 3 moles of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ to produce 2 moles of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ but, since at equilibrium 1 mole of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ is produced. So, 0.5 moles, i.e. $14 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ are left at equilibrium, Similarly, 3 moles of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \equiv 2$ moles of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$.
For 1 mole of $\mathrm{NH}_{3} \equiv 1.5$ mole of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$
Hence, 1.5 mole i.e. $3 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ are left at equilibrium.
So, $14 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ and 3g of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ are left at equilibrium.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Calculation of Equilibrium Concentration

question: In the equilibrium, ${{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}{\text{(g)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{2N}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}}$, the $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ is fifty per cent dissociated at $60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. What will be the value of ${{\rm{K}}_{\rm{P}}}$ at this temperature and one atmosphere?
explanation:
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}{\text{(g}}) \rightleftharpoons {\text{2N}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}}$
${\text{At}}\,\,{\text{equilibrium}}\,{\text{(1}} - {\text{x)}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,({\text{2x)}}$
Total number of moles = ${\mkern 1mu} 1 + {\text{x}}$
value of x = 0.5 (given)
$\mathrm{P}=1 \mathrm{~atm}$
$\begin{aligned}\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=\dfrac{\left(\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{NO}_{2}}\right)^{2}}{\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}}} & =\dfrac{\left(\dfrac{2 \mathrm{x}}{1+\mathrm{x}} \times \mathrm{p}\right)^{2}}{\left(\dfrac{1-\mathrm{x}}{1+\mathrm{x}} \times \mathrm{p}\right)} \\& =\dfrac{1}{0.75} \times 1 \\\Rightarrow \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}} & =1.33 \mathrm{~atm}\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Calculation of Equilibrium Constant for Homogeneous Equilibrium

question: The value of equilibrium constant for the reaction, $\mathrm{N}_{2}+2 \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}$ is 100 . The equilibrium constant for the reaction, $\mathrm{NO}_{2} \rightleftharpoons \dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{~N}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2}$ will be
explanation:
$\mathrm{N}_{2}+2 \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}$ for the reaction, $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{~N}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]^{2}}$
For the reaction,
$\mathrm{NO}_{2} \rightleftharpoons \dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{~N}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2}$
${{\rm{K}}_{{{\rm{C}}_{\rm{1}}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{{\left[ {{{\rm{N}}_{\rm{2}}}} \right]}^{{\rm{1/2}}}}\left[ {{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}} \right]}}{{\left[ {{\rm{N}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}} \right]}}$
Hence, $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}_{1}}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}}}$$({\rm{given}},{K_c} = 100)$
So, $\quad K_{\mathrm{C}_{1}}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{100}}=0.1$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Stoichiometric Representation of Equilibrium Constant

question: The value of  ${{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}}$ for the reaction, ${\text{2A}} \rightleftharpoons {\rm{B}} + {\rm{C}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{is}}\,{\mkern 1mu} 2 \times {10^{ - 3}}$. At a given time, the composition of reaction mixture is ${\rm{[A] = [B] = [C]}}$=$3 \times {10^{ - 4}}{\rm{M}}$. In which direction, the reaction will proceed?
explanation:
For the reaction, the reaction quotient $\mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{C}}$ is given by
$\mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{C}}=[\mathrm{B}][\mathrm{C}] /[\mathrm{A}]^{2}$
Given that,
${\text{[A]}} = {\text{[B]}} = [{\text{C]}} = 3 \times {10^{ - 4}}{\text{M}}$
Then, $\mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{\left(3 \times 10^{-4}\right)\left(3 \times 10^{-4}\right)}{\left(3 \times 10^{-4}\right)^{2}}=1
$
As, $\mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{C}}>\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}$, so the reaction will proceed in the reverse direction.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Predicting the Direction of the Reaction: Reaction Quotient (Q)

question: If equilibrium constant is $26 \times 10^{8}$ at $298 \mathrm{~K}$ then find the value of  ${\rm{\Delta }}{{\rm{G}}^{\rm{^\circ }}}$
explanation:
$\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}$ is related to thermodynamic equilibrium constant as
$=2.303 \mathrm{RT} \log \mathrm{K}$
$=-2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298 \log \left(2.6 \times 10^{8}\right)$
$=-48014.56 \mathrm{~J}$
The answer should be $48.014 \mathrm{~kJ}$.

question: $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ are taken in 1:3 molar ratio in a closed vessel to attain the following equilibrium $\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})$. Find $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}$ for reaction at total pressure of 2 P if $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}$ at equilibrium is $\dfrac{\mathrm{P}}{3}$.
explanation:
$\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {}&{{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}}}& + &{{\text{3}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}}}& \rightleftharpoons &{{\text{2N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{(g)}}} \\ {\mathop {{\text{At}}}\limits_{{\text{equilibrium}}} \,}&{\frac{{\text{P}}}{3}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} }&{}&{\text{P}}&{}&{{\kern 1pt} {{\text{P}}_{{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}}}} \end{array}$
$\frac{{\text{P}}}{3} + {\text{P}} + {{\text{P}}_{{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}}} = 2{\text{P}}$
${{\text{P}}_{{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}}} = \frac{{2{\text{P}}}}{3},\,{{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}} = \frac{{{{\left[ {{{\text{P}}_{{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}}}} \right]}^2}}}{{\left[ {{{\text{P}}_{{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}}}} \right]{{\left[ {{{\text{P}}_{{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}}}} \right]}^3}}}$
$ \Rightarrow {{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}} = \frac{{\frac{{2{\text{P}}}}{3} \times \frac{{2{\text{P}}}}{3}}}{{\frac{{\text{P}}}{3} \times {{\text{P}}^3}}} = \frac{1}{{{{\text{P}}^2}}} \cdot \frac{4}{3}$
$ \Rightarrow {{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}} = \frac{4}{{{\text{3}}{{\text{P}}^{\text{2}}}}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between Equilibrium Constant and Total Pressure of Reactions

question:
The standard Gibbs energy change at 300 K for the reaction, $2 \mathrm{~A} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{B}+\mathrm{C}$ is 2494.2 J . At a given time, the composition of the reaction mixture is $[\mathrm{A}]=\dfrac{1}{2},[\mathrm{~B}]=2$ and $[\mathrm{C}]=\dfrac{1}{2}$. The reaction proceeds in the
$[\mathrm{R}=8.314 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{K} / \mathrm{mol}, \mathrm{e}=2.718]$
explanation:
${\rm{\Delta G}} = - {\rm{RT}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{ln}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{K}}_{\rm{c}}}$
$2494.2=8.314 \times 300 \ln _{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}=\mathrm{e}^{-1}=\dfrac{1}{2.718}=0.36$
$\mathrm{Q}=\dfrac{[\mathrm{B}][\mathrm{C}]}{[\mathrm{A}]^{2}}=\dfrac{2 \times \dfrac{1}{2}}{\left[\dfrac{1}{2}\right]^{2}}=4$
$\mathrm{Q}>\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}$, i.e., backward reaction is favoured.
subject: Chemistry
topic: Relationship between Equilibrium Constant, Reaction Quotient and Gibbs Free Energy
subtopic: Relationship between Equilibrium Constant, Reaction Quotient and Gibbs Free Energy

question:
The following equilibrium is established,
$\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CaO}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ one mole of
$\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$ was kept at $727^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in a closed 20 litre container. If one third of the $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$ remains undecomposed under equilibrium, what is the value of $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}$ ?
explanation:
$\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{\text{CaC}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}(\,{\text{s}})}& \rightleftharpoons &{{\text{CaO(s)}}}& + &{{\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}(\,{\text{g}})} \\ 1&{}& - &{}& - \\ {1 - \frac{2}{3} = \frac{1}{3}}&{}&{\frac{2}{3}}&{}&{\frac{2}{3}} \end{array}$
$\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}=\dfrac{2}{3}$
$\mathrm{PV}=\mathrm{nRT}$
$\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}=\dfrac{2}{3} \times \dfrac{0.082 \times 1000}{20}=2.74$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}=2.74 \mathrm{~atm}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between Equilibrium Constant and Total Pressure of Reactions

question: For the equilibrium, $2 \operatorname{NOBr}(\mathrm{g}) \rightleftharpoons$ $2 \mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{Br}_{2}$, calculate the ratio $\frac{{{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{p}}}}}{{\rm{P}}},$ where P is the total pressure given that ${{\rm{P}}_{{\rm{B}}{{\rm{r}}_{\rm{2}}}}} = \frac{{\rm{P}}}{9}$ at a certain temperature
explanation:
$\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {}&{{\text{2NOBr}}}& \rightleftharpoons &{{\text{2NO}}}& + &{{\text{B}}{{\text{r}}_{\text{2}}}} \\ {{\text{Initial:}}}&2&{}&0&{}&0 \\ \begin{gathered} {\text{equm}} \hfill \\ {\text{moles}} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} &{2 - 2{\text{x}}}&{}&{2{\text{x}}}&{}&{\text{x}} \\ \begin{gathered} {\text{equm}} \hfill \\ {\text{Pressure}} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} &{\frac{{2 - 2{\text{x}}}}{{2 + {\text{x}}}}.{\text{P}}}&{}&{\frac{{2{\text{x}}}}{{2 + {\text{x}}}}.{\text{P}}}&{}&{\frac{{\text{x}}}{{2 + {\text{x}}}}.{\text{P}}} \end{array}$
Initial moles $=2+2 \mathrm{x}$
$\dfrac{\mathrm{x}}{2+\mathrm{x}} \cdot \mathrm{P}=\dfrac{\mathrm{P}}{9}$ (Given)
$\mathrm{x}=1 / 4$. On solving $\dfrac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{P}}=\dfrac{1}{81}$

$\mathrm{CuSO}_{4} \cdot 5 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{s}) \rightleftharpoons$$\mathrm{CuSO}_{4} \cdot 3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{s})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g})$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=1.086 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~atm}^{2}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The efflorescent nature of $\mathrm{CuSO}_{4} \cdot 5 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ can be noticed when the vapour pressure of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ in atmosphere is
explanation:

question: $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}$ for $\mathrm{PCl}_{5}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{PCl}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ is 0.04 at $250^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. How many moles of $\mathrm{PCl}_{5}$ must be added to 3L flask to obtain a $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ concentration of 0.15 M .
explanation:
$\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {}&{{\text{PC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{5}}}{\text{(g)}}}& \rightleftharpoons &{{\text{PC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{(g)}}}& + &{{\text{C}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}}} \\ {{\text{Initial:}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} }&{\text{a}}&{}& - &{}& - \\ {{\text{eq}}{{\text{u}}^{\text{n}}}{\text{:}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} }&{\frac{{{\text{a}} - {\text{x}}}}{3}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} }&{}&{\frac{{\text{x}}}{3}}&{}&{\frac{{\text{x}}}{3}} \end{array}$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}=\dfrac{\left(\dfrac{\mathrm{x}}{3}\right)^{2}}{\left(\dfrac{\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{x}}{3}\right)}=\dfrac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{3(\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{x})}=0.04$
Given, $\left[\mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right]=\dfrac{\mathrm{x}}{3}=0.15$
x = 0.45
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{(0.45)^{2}}{3(\mathrm{a}-0.45)}=0.04$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{0.45 \times 0.45}{\mathrm{a}-0.45}=0.12$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=2.13$

question: In a system,  $\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{s}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{~B}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{g})$, if the concentration of C at equilibrium is increased by a factor of 2 , it will cause the equilibrium concentration of B to change to
explanation:
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}=[\mathrm{B}]^{2}[\mathrm{C}]$
even $[\mathrm{C}]$ is doubled, $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}$ will remain same, as $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}$ depends only on temp.
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}=[\mathrm{B}]^{2}[\mathrm{C}]=\left[\mathrm{B}^{\prime}\right]^{2}[2 \mathrm{C}]$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{[\mathrm{B}]^{2}}{2}[\mathrm{C}]=\left[\mathrm{B}^{\prime}\right]^{2}[\mathrm{C}]$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{[\mathrm{B}]^{2}}{2}=\left[\mathrm{B}^{\prime}\right]^{2}$
$\Rightarrow\left[\mathrm{B}^{\prime}\right]=\dfrac{[\mathrm{B}]}{\sqrt{2}}$

options.text: One half of its original value

explanation:
${\mathrm{2 \mathrm{~A} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{B}+\mathrm{C}, \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=4 \times 10^{-3}}}$ and reaction quotient, ${\mathrm{Q_{c}}}$
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{[\mathrm{B}][\mathrm{C}]}{[\mathrm{A}]^{2}}=\dfrac{\left(2 \times 10^{-3}\right)\left(2 \times 10^{-3}\right)}{\left(2 \times 10^{-3}\right)^{2}} \\& \mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{C}}=1\end{aligned}$

question:
Assertion: The equilibrium constant is fixed and a characteristic for any given chemical reaction at a specified temperature.


Reason: The composition of the final equilibrium mixture at a particular temperature depends upon the starting amount of reactants.
explanation: The equilibrium constant is always fixed and is a characteristic of a reaction at specified temperature. It defines the composition of the final equilibrium mixture of that reaction regardless of the starting amount of reactants and products. So option (3) is correct.

question:
Assertion: If $\mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{c}}$ (reaction quotient) $ < \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}$ (equilibrium constant), reaction moves in direction of products.


Reason: Reaction quotient is defined in the same way as equilibrium constant at any stage of the reaction.
explanation:
$\mathrm{aA}+\mathrm{bB} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{cC}+\mathrm{dD}, \mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{c}}=\dfrac{[\mathrm{C}]^{\mathrm{c}}[\mathrm{D}]^{\mathrm{d}}}{[\mathrm{A}]^{\mathrm{a}}[\mathrm{B}]^{\mathrm{b}}}$
If ${{\text{Q}}_{\text{c}}}{\text{ > }}{{\text{K}}_c}$, reaction will proceed in the direction of reactants.
If ${{\text{Q}}_{\text{c}}}{\text{ < }}{{\text{K}}_{\text{c}}}$, reaction will move in direction of products.
If $\mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{c}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}$, the reaction mixture is already at equilibrium.
So option (2) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Effect of temperature on $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}$ or $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}$ depends on enthalpy change.


Reason: Increase in temperature shifts the equilibrium in forward direction in exothermic reaction whereas decrease in temperature shifts the equilibrium in backward direction in endothermic reaction.
explanation: Le Chatelier's principle states that a system at equilibrium counteracts changes in conditions. Exothermic reactions favor low temperatures, while endothermic reactions favor high temperatures.Increase in temperature shifts exothermic reaction to the backward direction. So option (3) is correct.

Forward reaction,

For reverse reaction, $2 \mathrm{HI}_{(\mathrm{g})} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{I}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$

So option (1) is correct.

question:
At $527^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the reaction given below has $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}=4$
$\mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons \dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{~N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\dfrac{3}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$.
What is the $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}$ for the reaction,
$\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g}) ?$
explanation:
If, $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}=\dfrac{\left[\mathrm{N}_{2}\right]^{1 / 2}\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]^{3 / 2}}{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]^{1}}=4$
then $\dfrac{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{~N}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]^{3}}=\dfrac{1}{4^{2}}$
And $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}} \cdot(\mathrm{RT})^{\Delta \mathrm{n}}$;
$\Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}}=2-4=-2$
$\therefore \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{P}}=\dfrac{1}{4^{2}}(800 \mathrm{R})^{-2}=\left(\dfrac{1}{4 \times 800 \mathrm{R}}\right)^{2}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Stoichiometric Representation of Equilibrium Constant

question:
The following reactions occur at $500 \mathrm{~K}$. Arrange them in increasing order of tendency to proceed to completion.
a) $2 \mathrm{NOCl}(\mathrm{g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$;
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=1.7 \times 10^{-2}$
b) $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) ; \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=1.5 \times 10^{-5}$
c) $2 \mathrm{SO}_{3}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$;
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=1.3 \times 10^{-5}$
d) $2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$;
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=5.9 \times 10^{-5}$
explanation:
Greater the ${{\text{K}}_{\text{P}}}$, more is the tendency to proceed to completion.
Increasing order of ${{\text{K}}_{\text{P}}}$ value is: c < b < d < a
order of completion towards product: c < b < d < a.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Predicting the Extent of a Reaction on the Basis of Magnitude of Equilibrium Constant

question: In which of the following reactions, the equilibrium mixture contains an appreciable concentration of both reactants and products.
explanation: Compare the values of ${{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}}$. Too high or too low value(s) of $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}$ will contain mostly products or reactants respectively. Therefore the value of $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}$ as 1.8 will contain appreciable concentration of both reactants and products. $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}$ value $10^{-3}$ to $10^{3}$ indicates appreciable conc. of both reactants and products.

the equilibrium mixture contains

$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}=7.4 \times 10^{-26}=\dfrac{\left[\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{N}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]^{2}}$,

the equilibrium constant $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}$ has the units of

question:
The dissociation equilibrium of a gas $\mathrm{AB}_{2}$ can be represented as
$2 \mathrm{AB}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{AB}_{(\mathrm{g})}+\mathrm{B}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$
The degree of dissociation is "X" and is small compared to 1. The expression relating the degree of dissociation (X) with equilibrium constant ${{\text{K}}_{\text{P}}}$ and total pressure "P" is
explanation:
$2 \mathrm{AB}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{AB}_{(\mathrm{g})}+\mathrm{B}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$
$\begin{array}{llll}\mathrm{t}=0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\\text { At equilibrium } & 2(1-\mathrm{x}) & 2 \mathrm{x} & \mathrm{x}\end{array}$
Total no. of moles at equilibrium $=$
$2-2 \mathrm{x}+2 \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{x}=2+\mathrm{x}$So; $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{AB}_{2}} \dfrac{2(1-\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{P}}{2+\mathrm{x}}, \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{AB}}=\dfrac{2 \mathrm{xP}}{2+\mathrm{x}}, \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}_{2}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{xP}}{2+\mathrm{x}}$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=\dfrac{\left(\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{AB}}\right)^{2}\left(\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}_{2}}\right)}{\left(\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{AB}_{2}}\right)^{2}}=\dfrac{\left(\dfrac{2 \mathrm{xP}}{2+\mathrm{x}}\right)^{2}\left(\dfrac{\mathrm{xP}}{2+\mathrm{x}}\right)}{\left(\dfrac{2(1-\mathrm{x})}{2+\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{P}\right)^{2}}$
$=\dfrac{4 x^{3} P}{4(2+x)(1-x)^{2}}$
${\text{ = }}\frac{{{{\text{x}}^{\text{3}}}{\text{P}}}}{{{\text{(2 + x)(1 - x}}{{\text{)}}^{\text{2}}}}}$
$[\therefore \mathrm{x} < < < 1$ and 2 , So: $(1+\mathrm{x}) \approx 1,(2+\mathrm{x}) \approx 2]$
${{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}}{\text{ = }}\frac{{{{\text{x}}^{\text{3}}}{\text{P}}}}{{\text{2}}}\,\,{\text{x = }}{\left( {\frac{{{\text{2}}{{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}}}}{{\text{P}}}} \right)^{\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{3}}}}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Calculation of Equilibrium Constant for Homogeneous Equilibrium

question: In a chemical equilibrium $\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{D}$, when one mole each of the two reactants are mixed, 0.6 mole each of the prodcuts are formed. The equilibrium constant is
explanation:
$\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{D}$
$\mathrm{t}=0$
$\begin{array}{llll}1 & 1 & 0 & 0\end{array}$
at equilibrium $(1-0.6)(1-0.6) \quad 0.6 \quad 0.6$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{[\mathrm{C}][\mathrm{D}]}{[\mathrm{A}][\mathrm{B}]}=\dfrac{0.6 \times 0.6}{0.4 \times 0.4}$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=2.25$

question: The equilibrium constant for the reaction $\mathrm{A} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{B}$, is $\mathrm{K}$. The equilibrium constant for the reaction $\mathrm{mA} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{mB}$, is
explanation:
For $\mathrm{A} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{B} ; \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{[\mathrm{B}]}{[\mathrm{A}]}=\mathrm{K}$ and for
$\mathrm{mA} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{mB} ; \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{[\mathrm{B}]^{\mathrm{m}}}{[\mathrm{A}]^{\mathrm{m}}}=\left(\dfrac{[\mathrm{B}]}{[\mathrm{A}]}\right)^{\mathrm{m}}=\mathrm{K}^{\mathrm{m}}$

question:
Assertion: $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}$ can be equal to or less than or even greater than the value of $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}$.

Reason: ${{\rm{K}}_{\rm{P}}}{\rm{ = }}{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{C}}}{{\rm{(RT)}}^{\Delta {\rm{n}}}}$
Relation between $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{P}}$ and $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}$ depends on the change in the number of moles of gaseous reactants and products.
explanation:
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{P}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}(\mathrm{RT})^{\Delta \mathrm{n}}$
$\Delta \mathrm{n}=$ number of moles of gaseous products - number of moles of gaseous reactants.
When, $\Delta \mathrm{n}>1, \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{P}}>\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}$
When, $\Delta \mathrm{n} < 1, \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{P}} < \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}$
When, $\Delta \mathrm{n}=0, \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{P}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}$

options.text: If both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
If both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is  not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
If Assertion is true but Reason is false.
Both Assertion andt reason is false.

subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between Equilibrium Constants, \[\rm{K_p}\]  and \[\rm{K_c}\]

question:
At equilibrium, the concentration of
${{\text{N}}_2} = 3.0 \times {10^{ - 3}}{\text{M}},{\text{ & }}{{\text{O}}_2} = 4.2 \times {10^{ - 3}}{\text{M}}$
and $\mathrm{NO}=2.8 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M}$
in a sealed vessel at $800 \mathrm{~K}$ and $1 \mathrm{~atm}$ pressure. What will be $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}$ for the given reaction? ${{\text{N}}_2}_{(\,{\text{g}})} + {{\text{O}}_2}_{(\,{\text{g}})} \rightleftharpoons 2{\text{N}}{{\text{O}}_{({\text{g}})}}$
explanation:
Given, $\mathrm{N}_{2}=3.0 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M}$
$\mathrm{O}_{2}=4.2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M}$
$\mathrm{NO}=2.8 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M}$
For the given reaction,
$\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g})$
equilibrium constant $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}$ can be written as
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{[\mathrm{NO}]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{~N}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]}$
$=\dfrac{\left(2.8 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M}\right)^{2}}{\left(3.0 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M}\right)\left(4.2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M}\right)}=0.622$
and $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{P}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}} \cdot(\mathrm{RT})^{\Delta \mathrm{n}}$
$\Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}}=$ Number of moles of gaseous products
- number of moles of gaseous reactants.
$\Delta \mathrm{n}=2-2=0$
$\therefore \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{P}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}} \cdot(\mathrm{RT})^{\mathrm{o}}$
or $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{P}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}$
$\therefore \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=0.622 \mathrm{~atm}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Calculation of Equilibrium Constant for Homogeneous Equilibrium

question: For the reaction $\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g})$. If the initial concentration of $\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]=\left[\mathrm{CO}_{2}\right]$ and $\mathrm{x} \mathrm{mol} / \mathrm{L}$ of hydrogen is consumed at equilibrium, the correct expression of $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}$ is
explanation:
For the reaction,
$\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g})$
$\begin{array}{lllll}\text { Initial conc. } & 1 & 1 & 0 & 0\end{array}$
At equm. $1-\mathrm{x} \quad 1-\mathrm{x} \quad \mathrm{x} \quad \mathrm{x}$
Thus, $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{(1-\mathrm{x})(1-\mathrm{x})}=\dfrac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{(1-\mathrm{x})^{2}}$
Here, $\Delta \mathrm{n}_{(\mathrm{g})}=2-2=0$
So, $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{P}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}$
Hence ${{\text{K}}_{\text{P}}}{\text{ = }}\frac{{{{\text{x}}^{\text{2}}}}}{{{{{\text{(1 - x)}}}^{\text{2}}}}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Calculation of Equilibrium Constant for Homogeneous Equilibrium

question:
Two moles of each reactant A and B are taken in a reaction flask. They react in the following manner,
${{\text{A}}_{({\text{g}})}} + {{\text{B}}_{({\text{g}})}} \rightleftharpoons {{\text{C}}_{({\text{g}})}} + {{\text{D}}_{({\text{g}})}}$
At equilibrium, it was found that the concentration of $\mathrm{C}$ is triple to that of $\mathrm{B}$. The equilibrium constant for the reaction is
explanation:
${\text{A}}({\text{g}}) + {\text{B}}({\text{g}}) \rightleftharpoons {\text{C}}({\text{g}}) + {\text{D}}({\text{g}})$
Initial moles $2 \quad 2 \quad 0 \quad 0$
At equm. $\quad(2-\mathrm{x})(2-\mathrm{x}) \quad \mathrm{x} \quad \mathrm{x}$
At equilibrium, $3[\mathrm{~B}]=[\mathrm{C}]$ (given)
$3(2-x)=x \Rightarrow x=\dfrac{3}{2}$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{[\mathrm{C}][\mathrm{D}]}{[\mathrm{A}][\mathrm{B}]}$
Here no.of moles of gaseous reactants and products are same. So, no need of considering volume.
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{\left(\dfrac{3}{2}\right)^{2}}{\left(2-\dfrac{3}{2}\right)^{2}}=\dfrac{(1.5)^{2}}{(0.5)^{2}}=9 \text {. }$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Calculation of Equilibrium Constant for Homogeneous Equilibrium

question:
Assertion: For the reaction,
$\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})$
Unit of $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\mathrm{L}^{2} \mathrm{~mol}^{-2}$

Reason: For the reaction,
$\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})$
Equilibrium constant, $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{~N}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]^{3}}$
explanation:
$\mathrm{N}_{2}+3 \underset{(\mathrm{g})}{3 \mathrm{H}_{2}} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{~N}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}_{3}\right]^{3}}$
So, unit of $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{\left(\dfrac{\mathrm{mol}}{\mathrm{L}}\right)^{2}}{\left(\dfrac{\mathrm{mol}}{\mathrm{L}}\right)\left(\dfrac{\mathrm{mol}}{\mathrm{L}}\right)^{3}}=\mathrm{L}^{2} \cdot \mathrm{mol}^{-2}$

options.text: If both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
If both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is  not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
If Assertion is true but Reason is false.
Both Assertion andt reason is false.

subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Types, Expressions, and Units of Equilibrium Constant

explanation: Higher ${{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}}$ value indicates more concentration of products. Higher ${{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}}$ is satisfied by option (2).

question:
At $500 \mathrm{~K}$, for a reversible reaction
$\mathrm{A}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{B}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{AB}_{(\mathrm{g})}$ in a closed container,
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=2 \times 10^{-5}$. In the presence of catalyst, the equilibrium is attaining 10 times faster. The equilibrium constant ${{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}}$ in the presence of catalyst at the same temperature is
explanation: Catalyst does not affect the ${{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}}$. Only Temp. can alter the value of $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}$ of a given reaction. Hence, the equilibrium constant value in the presence of catalyst is $2 \times 10^{-5}$.

question: The equilibrium concentrations of the species in the reaction $\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{D}$ are $2,3,10$ and $6 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$, respectively at $300 \mathrm{~K}$. $\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}$ for the reaction is $(\mathrm{R}=2 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{K})$
explanation:
$\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{D}$
Given
$[\mathrm{A}]=2 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$
$[\mathrm{B}]=3 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$
$[\mathrm{C}]=10 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$
$[\mathrm{D}]=6 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}, \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{[\mathrm{C}][\mathrm{D}]}{[\mathrm{A}][\mathrm{B}]}=\dfrac{10 \times 6}{2 \times 3}=10$
$\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=-2.303 \mathrm{RT} \log \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{eq}}$
$=-2.303 \times 2 \times 300 \times \log 10$
$\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=-1381.80 \mathrm{cal}$
subject: Chemistry
topic: Relationship between Equilibrium Constant, Reaction Quotient and Gibbs Free Energy
subtopic: Relationship between Equilibrium Constant, Reaction Quotient and Gibbs Free Energy

question: Equilibrium constant for a reaction is 20. What is the value of $\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}$ at $300 \mathrm{~K} ?\left(\mathrm{R}=8 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~kJ}\right)$
explanation:
Given,
$\rm {\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{eq}}=20}$
Temperature $\mathrm {(\mathrm{T})=300 \mathrm{~K}}$
$\mathrm {\mathrm{R}=8 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~kJ}}$
We have, $\mathrm {\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=-2.303 \times \mathrm{R} \times \mathrm{T} \times \log \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{eq}}}$
$\mathrm {=-2.303 \times 8 \times 10^{-3} \times \log 20 \times 300}$
$\mathrm {(\log 20=1.301)}$
$\mathrm {=-7.91 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
subject: Chemistry
topic: Relationship between Equilibrium Constant, Reaction Quotient and Gibbs Free Energy
