question:
In a 2 litre flask, the reaction takes place as:
${\text{COC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{CO(g) + C}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}}$
The equilibrium conc. of $\mathrm{COCl}_{2}$ was found to be 0.4. If excess of $\mathrm{COCl}_{2}$ is added to the system, the equilibrium re-establish and ${\kern 1pt} \left[ {{\text{COC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}} \right]$ becomes 1.6. What is the equilibrium conc. of $[\mathrm{CO}]$ ?
explanation:
$\rm {{\text{COC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{CO(g) + C}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}}}$
$\rm {\therefore \dfrac{[\mathrm{CO}]\left[\mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{COCl}_{2}\right]}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}}$
Let $\mathrm {[\mathrm{CO}]=\mathrm{x}}$, then $\mathrm {\left[\mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right]=\mathrm{x}}$
$\mathrm {\therefore \dfrac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{COCl}_{2}\right]}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}}$ or $\mathrm {\dfrac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{0.4}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}}$
Again $\mathrm {\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{0.4}=\dfrac{[\mathrm{CO}]\left[\mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{COCl}_{2}\right]}}$
$\mathrm {\dfrac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{0.4}=\dfrac{[\mathrm{CO}]^{2}}{1.6}[}$ as $\mathrm {[\mathrm{CO}]=[\mathrm{Cl}]]}$
$\mathrm {\therefore \mathrm{x}^{2} \times 4=[\mathrm{CO}]^{2}}$
$\mathrm {2 \mathrm{x}=[\mathrm{CO}]}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Calculation of Equilibrium Concentration

question:
One mole of ammonium carbonate dissociates as shown below at $500 \mathrm{~K}$ :
${\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{COON}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{4}}}{\text{(s)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{2N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{(g) + C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}}$
If the pressure exerted by the released gases is $3.0 \mathrm{~atm}$, then the value of $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}$ is
explanation:
Applying the law of chemical equilibrium, we get
$\mathrm {\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=\left(\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}\right)^{2}\left(\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}\right)}$
Since total pressure is 3 atm, the partial pressures of ${\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{(g)}}\,\,{\text{and C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}}$ are
$\mathrm {\left[\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}\right]=3 \times \dfrac{2}{3}=2 \mathrm{~atm}}$
$\mathrm {\left[\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}\right]=3 \times \dfrac{1}{3}=1 \mathrm{~atm}}$
$\mathrm {\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=[2.0]^{2}[1.0]=4.0 \mathrm{~atm}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between Equilibrium Constant and Total Pressure of Reactions

question: ${\text{x}}$ moles and ${\text{y}}$ moles per litre of $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$, respectivley, were allowed to react. At the equilibrium ${{\text{A}}_{\left( {\text{g}} \right)}}{\text{ + 2}}{{\text{B}}_{\left( {\text{g}} \right)}} \rightleftharpoons \frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{2}}}{{\text{C}}_{\left( {\text{g}} \right)}}$. The concentration of A, B and C were found to be  4,2 and $2 \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{L}$, respectively. values of ${\text{x}}$ and ${\text{y}}$ are
explanation:
$\mathrm {\mathrm{A}+2 \mathrm{~B} \rightleftharpoons \dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{C}}$
At equilibrium if ' $\mathrm {x}$ ' moles of $\mathrm {A}$ is reacted
$\mathrm {\left(x-x^{\prime}\right)\left(y-2 x^{\prime}\right)=\dfrac{1}{2} x^{\prime}}$
$\mathrm {\left(x-x^{\prime}\right)=4}$
$\mathrm {\left(y-2 x^{\prime}\right)=2}$
$\mathrm {\dfrac{1}{2} x^{\prime}=2 ; x^{\prime}=4}$
$\mathrm {[\mathrm{A}]=\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{x}^{\prime}=\mathrm{x}-4=4}$
$\mathrm {\mathrm{x}=8}$
$\mathrm {\mathrm{y}-8=2 ; \mathrm{y}=10}$

question:
For the reaction equilibrium:
${{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{{\text{4}}\left( {\text{g}} \right)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{2N}}{{\text{O}}_{{\text{2}}\left( {\text{g}} \right)}}$ the concentration of $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ at equilibrium are $4.8 \times 10^{-2}$ and $1.2 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{L}$, respectively. The value of $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}$ for the reaction is
explanation: $\mathrm {\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}=\dfrac{\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{~N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}\right]}=\dfrac{\left(1.2 \times 10^{-2}\right)^{2}}{4.8 \times 10^{-2}}=3.0 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Calculation of Equilibrium Constant for Homogeneous Equilibrium

question:
In the reaction, ${{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g) + }}{{\text{I}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{2HI(g)}}$,
the concentrations of $\mathrm{H}_{2}, \mathrm{I}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{HI}$ at equilibrium are 8.0, 3.0 and 28.0 moles per litre respectively. What will be the equilibrium constant?
explanation:
$\rm{{H}_{2} (g) + {I}_{2} (g)} \rightleftharpoons 2 \rm{HI (g)}$
Applying law of mass action
$\rm {\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{[\mathrm{HI}]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{I}_{2}\right]}}$
Given
$\rm {\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]=8.0 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}}$
$\mathrm {\left[\mathrm{I}_{2}\right]=3.0 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}}$
$\mathrm {[\mathrm{HI}]=28.0 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}}$
So, $\mathrm {\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{(28.0)^{2}}{(8.0) \times(3.0)}=32.66}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Calculation of Equilibrium Constant for Homogeneous Equilibrium

question:
The following equilibrium constant were determined at $1120 \mathrm{~K}$ :
${\text{2C}}{{\text{O}}_{\left( {\text{g}} \right)}} \rightleftharpoons {{\text{C}}_{\left( {\text{s}} \right)}}{\text{ + C}}{{\text{O}}_{{\text{2}}\left( {\text{g}} \right)}}{\text{;}}{{\text{K}}_{{{\text{p}}_{\text{1}}}}}{\text{ = 1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 14}}}}{\text{at}}{{\text{m}}^{{\text{ - 1}}}}$ &
${\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_{\left( {\text{g}} \right)}}{\text{ + C}}{{\text{l}}_{{\text{2}}\left( {\text{g}} \right)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{COC}}{{\text{l}}_{{\text{2}}\left( {\text{g}} \right)}}\,\,{\text{& }}{{\text{K}}_{{{\text{p}}_{\text{2}}}}}{\text{ = 6}} \times {\text{1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 3}}}}{\text{ at}}{{\text{m}}^{{\text{ - 1}}}}$
What is the equilibrium constant $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}$ for the following reaction at $1120 \mathrm{~K}$ ?
$\mathrm{C}_{(\mathrm{s})}+\mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+2 \mathrm{Cl}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{COCl}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$
explanation:
${{\text{C}}_{\left( {\text{g}} \right)}}{\text{ + C}}{{\text{O}}_{{\text{2}}\left( {\text{g}} \right)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{2C}}{{\text{O}}_{\left( {\text{g}} \right)}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\text{(I)}}$
$\rm {\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p} 1}=10^{14} \mathrm{~atm}}$
${\text{2C}}{{\text{O}}_{\left( {\text{g}} \right)}}{\text{ + 2C}}{{\text{l}}_{{\text{2}}\left( {\text{g}} \right)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{2COC}}{{\text{l}}_{{\text{2}}\left( {\text{g}} \right)}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\text{(II)}}$
$\rm {\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p} 2}=\left(6 \times 10^{-3}\right)^{2} \mathrm{~atm}^{-2}}$
Add (1) and (2),
${\text{ }}{{\text{C}}_{\left( {\text{s}} \right)}}{\text{ + C}}{{\text{O}}_{{\text{2}}\left( {\text{g}} \right)}}{\text{ + 2C}}{{\text{l}}_{{\text{2}}\left( {\text{g}} \right)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{2COC}}{{\text{l}}_{{\text{2}}\left( {\text{g}} \right)}}$
${{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}} = {10^{14}} \times 36 \times {10^{ - 6}} = 36 \times {10^8}$
For given reaction, $\mathrm {\Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}}=-1}$
$\rm {\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}=3.31 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{M}^{-1}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Stoichiometric Representation of Equilibrium Constant

question:
The relationship between $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}$ and $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}$ is $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}(\mathrm{RT})^{\mathrm{\Delta n}}$. What would be the value of $\Delta \mathrm{n}$ for the reaction,
${\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{4}}}{\text{C}}{{\text{l}}_{\left( {\text{g}} \right)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_{{\text{3}}\left( {\text{g}} \right)}}{\text{ + HC}}{{\text{l}}_{\left( {\text{g}} \right)}}$?
explanation:
${{\rm{K}}_{\rm{p}}}{\rm{ = }}{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{c}}}{{\rm{(RT)}}^{{\rm{\Delta }}{{\rm{n}}_{\rm{g}}}}}$
$\mathrm {\Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}}=}$ sum of the stoichiometric coefficient of
gaseous products - sum of stoichiometric coefficients of gaseous reactants
$\mathrm {\Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}}=2-0=2}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between Equilibrium Constants, \[\rm{K_p}\]  and \[\rm{K_c}\]

question:
The dissociation constants for acetic acid and $\mathrm{HCN}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ are $1.5 \times 10^{-5}$ and $4.5 \times 10^{-10}$, respectively. The equilibrium constant for the equilibrium
$\mathrm{CN}^{-}+\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{HCN}+\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}$would be
explanation:
Given, $\mathrm {\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}+\mathrm{H}^{+}}$;
${{\text{K}}_{{\text{a1}}}}{\text{ = 1}}{\text{.5}} \times {\text{1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 5}}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\text{(I)}}$
${\text{HCN}} \rightleftharpoons {{\text{H}}^{{\text{ + }}}}{\text{ + C}}{{\text{N}}^{{\text{ - }}}}$
${{\text{K}}_{{\text{a2}}}}{\text{ = 4}}{\text{.5}} \times {\text{1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 10}}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{(II)}}$
$\mathrm {\mathrm{CN}^{-}+\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{HCN}+\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-} \mathrm{K}=?}$
On subtracting Eq. (II) from Eq. (I), we get
$\mathrm {\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{COOH}+\mathrm{CN}^{-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{HCN}+\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}}$
$\mathrm {K=\dfrac{K_{a 1}}{K_{a 2}}=\dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{-5}}{4.5 \times 10^{-10}}=\dfrac{10^{5}}{3}=3.33 \times 10^{4}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Stoichiometric Representation of Equilibrium Constant

question:
In the reaction ${\text{Fe(OH}}{{\text{)}}_{\text{2}}}_{{\text{(S)}}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{Fe}}_{{\text{(aq)}}}^{{\text{3 + }}}{\text{ + 3OH}}_{{\text{(aq)}}}^{{\text{ - }}}$
If the concentration of $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ions is decreased by $1 / 4$ times, then the equilibrium concentration of $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ will increase by
explanation:
${{\text{K}}_{\text{c}}}{\text{ = }}[{\text{x}}]{[{\text{3x}}]^{\text{3}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,({\text{I}})$
When concentration of $\mathrm {\mathrm{OH}^{-}}$ions is decresed
by $\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{4}}}{\text{times, }}{{\text{K}}_{\text{c}}}{\text{ = }}\left[ {{{\text{x}}^{\text{'}}}} \right]{\left[ {\frac{{{\text{3x}}}}{{\text{4}}}} \right]^{\text{3}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\text{(II)}}$
Equation eq. (I) and (II)
$\rm { x \times(3 x)^{3}=x^{\prime}\left(\dfrac{3 x}{4}\right)^{3} \\ 64 x=x^{\prime}}$
So, $\mathrm {\mathrm{x}^{\prime}=64 \mathrm{x}}$ or 64 times

question: For the reaction, ${\text{2H}}{{\text{I}}_{\left( {\text{g}} \right)}} \rightleftharpoons {{\text{H}}_{{\text{2}}\left( {\text{g}} \right)}}{\text{ + }}{{\text{I}}_{{\text{2}}\left( {\text{g}} \right)}}{\text{ - QkJ}}$, the equilibrium constant depends upon
explanation:
In general, the temperature dependence of the equilibrium constant depends on the nature of the reaction whether it is endothermic or exothermic.
The equilibrium constant for an exothermic reaction (negative $\mathrm {\Delta \mathrm{Q}}$) decreases as temperature increases and for an endothermic reaction ${\text{(positive }}\Delta {\text{Q)}}$ equilibrium constant increases as temperature increases.

explanation: $\mathrm {K_{c}}$ decreases with temperature, i.e., reaction is exothermic or energy of $\mathrm {\mathrm{HI}}$ is more than $\mathrm {\mathrm{H}_{2}}$ and $\mathrm {\mathrm{I}_{2}}$ or $\mathrm {\mathrm{HI}}$ is less stable than $\mathrm {\mathrm{H}_{2}, \mathrm{I}_{2}}$

$\mathrm{HI}$ is relatively less stable than $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{I}_{2}$

question:
For the equilibrium
${\text{CaC}}{{\text{O}}_{{\text{3}}\left( {\text{s}} \right)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{Ca}}{{\text{O}}_{\left( {\text{s}} \right)}}{\text{ + C}}{{\text{O}}_{{\text{2}}\left( {\text{g}} \right)}}{\text{;}}$
${{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}}{\text{ = }}$ ${\text{1}}{\text{.64}}$ $\,{\text{atm}}\,\,$ ${\text{at}}$ ${\text{1000K.}}$ ${\mkern 1mu} {\text{50}}\,$ ${\text{g}}$ ${\text{of}}$ ${\text{CaC}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}$ in a
10 litre closed vessel is heated to $1000 \mathrm{~K}$.
Percentage of $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$ that remains unreacted at equilibrium is (Given : ${\text{R = 0}}{\text{.082}}$ ${\text{L}}$ $\,{\text{atm}}$ ${{\text{K}}^{{\text{ - 1}}}}$ ${\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{{\text{ - 1}}}}{\text{)}}$
explanation:
For the reaction
$\mathrm {\mathrm{CaCO}_{3(\mathrm{~s})} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CaO}_{(\mathrm{s})}+\mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}}$
$\mathrm {\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}=\left[\mathrm{CO}_{2}\right]}$ or $\mathrm {\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}}$
No. of moles of
$\mathrm {\mathrm{CaCO}_{3(\mathrm{~s})}=\dfrac{\text { Mass }}{\text { Mol.mass }}=\dfrac{50}{100}=0.5}$
Applying ideal gas equation, $\mathrm {\mathrm{PV}=\mathrm{nRT}}$
$\mathrm {1.64 \times 10=\mathrm{n} \times 0.082 \times 1000}$
No. of moles of $\mathrm {\mathrm{CO}_{2}, \mathrm{n}=0.2}$
No. of moles of unreacted
$\mathrm {\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}=0.5-0.2=0.3}$
$\mathrm {\%}$ of unreacted $\mathrm {\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}=\dfrac{0.3}{0.5} \times 100=60 \%}$

question: 5 moles of $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$ and 5 moles of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ are allowed to react. At equilibrium, it was found that $60 \%$ of $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$ is used up. If the partial pressure of the equilibrium mixture is one atmosphere, the partial pressure of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ is
explanation:
$\mathrm {2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{SO}_{3}}$
at $\mathrm {\mathrm{t}=0 \quad 5}$ mole 5 mole
at teq $\mathrm {5-2 \mathrm{x} \quad 5-\mathrm{x} \quad 2 \mathrm{x}}$
Given $\mathrm {2 x=\dfrac{60}{100} \times 5}$
$\mathrm {\therefore \quad x=1 \cdot 5}$
Now $\mathrm {\mathrm{Po}_{2}=\mathrm{Xo}_{2} \times P_{\text {Total }}}$
$\rm { =\dfrac{5-x}{10-x} \times 1=\dfrac{3.5}{8.5} \times 1 \\ =0.41 \mathrm{~atm}}$

question:
The reaction,
${\text{2A(g) + B(g)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{3C(g) + D(g)}}$
is begun with concentration of ${\text{A}}$ and ${\text{B}}$ both at an initial value of $1.00\,\,{\text{M}}$. When equilibrium is reached, the concentration of ${\text{D}}$ is measured and found to be $0.25\,\,{\text{M}}$. The value for the equilibrium constant for this reaction is given by the expression:
explanation:
$\mathrm {2 A+B \rightleftharpoons 3 C+D}$
Initial conc. $\mathrm {\quad 1 \quad 1}$
Reacted $\mathrm {\quad .5 \quad .25}$
Produced $\mathrm {\quad .75 \quad .25}$
$\mathrm {\begin{array}{lllll}\text { At equilibrium } & .5 & .75 & .75 & .25\end{array}}$
$\rm {K_{c}=\dfrac{(0.25) \times(0.75)^{3}}{(0.5)^{2} \times(0.75)}}$

subtopic: Calculation of Equilibrium Constant for Homogeneous Equilibrium

question:
At $700 \mathrm{~K}$, the equilibrium constant $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}$ for the reaction
${\text{2S}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{(g)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{2S}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g) + }}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}}$ is $1.8 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{kPa}$. What is the numerical value of $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}$ for this reaction at the same temperature?
explanation:
We know the relationship
$\mathrm {\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{P}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}(\mathrm{RT})^{\Delta \mathrm{n}} \mathrm{g}}$
Here $\mathrm {\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{P}}=1.80 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{kPa}}$
$\mathrm {\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{P}}=\dfrac{1.8 \times 10^{-3}}{101.3} \mathrm{~atm}=1.78 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~atm}}$
${\text{R = 0}}{\text{.082 1 L atm }}{{\text{K}}^{{\text{ - 1}}}}{\text{ mo}}{{\text{l}}^{{\text{ - 1}}}}$
$\mathrm {\Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}}=3-2=1}$
$\mathrm {\mathrm{T}=700 \mathrm{~K}}$
$\mathrm {\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{P}}}{(\mathrm{RT})^{\Delta \mathrm{n}} \mathrm{g}}=\dfrac{1.78 \times 10^{-5}}{0.0821 \times 700}}$
$\rm {=3.09 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{molL}^{-1}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between Equilibrium Constants, \[\rm{K_p}\]  and \[\rm{K_c}\]

question: The equilibrium constant $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}$ for the decomposition of $\mathrm{PCl}_{5}$ is  ${\text{0}}{\text{.0625}}$  ${\text{mol}}{{\text{L}}^{{\text{ - 1}}}}$  at $300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. What will be the value of $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}$ ?
explanation:
$\mathrm {\mathrm{PCl}_{5}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{PCl}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})}$
$\Delta \mathrm{n} =2-1=1$
$\mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{P}} =\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}(\mathrm{RT})^{\Delta \mathrm{n}} \mathrm{g}$
= $0.0625 \times (0.082 \times 573)^{1}$
= $2.936 \mathrm{~atm}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between Equilibrium Constants, \[\rm{K_p}\]  and \[\rm{K_c}\]

question:
Given that the equilibrium constant for the reaction
${\text{2S}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g) + }}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{2S}}{{\text{O}}_3}{\text{(g)}}$
has a value of ${\text{278}}$ at a particular temperature. What is the value of the equilibrium constant for the following reaction at the same temperature?
${\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_3}{\text{(g)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g) + }}\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_2}{\text{(g)}}$
explanation:
$\rm {{\text{2S}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ + }}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ + }}\dfrac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{2S}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{;}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{K = 278}}}$
${\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ + }}\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{; }}{{\text{K}}^{\text{1}}}{\text{ = }}\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\sqrt {\text{K}} }}$
${{\text{K}}^{\text{1}}}{\text{ = }}\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\sqrt {\text{K}} }}{\text{ = }}\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\sqrt {{\text{278}}} }}{\text{ = 6}} \times {\text{1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 2}}}}$
If we reverse the equation,then equilibrium constant is reciprocal of the previous. if equation is divided by n new equilibrium constant is $\rm {{{\text{K}}^{{\text{1/n}}}}}$

subtopic: Stoichiometric Representation of Equilibrium Constant

question: The value of equilibrium constant of the reaction,${\text{HI(g)}} \rightleftharpoons \frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{2}}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g) + }}\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{2}}}{{\text{I}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}}$  is${\text{ 8}}{\text{.0}}$. The equilibrium constant of the reaction  ${{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g) + }}{{\text{I}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{2HI(g)}}$  will be
explanation:
${\text{For HI}} \rightleftharpoons \frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{2}}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ + }}\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{2}}}{{\text{I}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{; }}{{\text{K}}_{{{\text{C}}_{\text{1}}}}}{\text{ = 8}}$
Reverse given eqn. and multiply by 2 .
$\therefore \,{\text{For}}\,\,{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ + }}{{\text{I}}_{\text{2}}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{2HI; }}{{\text{K}}_{{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}}}{\text{ = }}{\left( {\frac{{\text{1}}}{{{{\text{K}}_{{{\text{c}}_{\text{1}}}}}}}} \right)^{\text{2}}}$
$\mathrm {=\left(\dfrac{1}{8}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{1}{64}}$

question:
For the reaction ${{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g) + }}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{2NO(g)}}$ the equilibrium constant is ${{\text{K}}_{\text{1}}}$. For the reaction ${\text{2NO(g) + }}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{2N}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}}$, the equilibrium constant is $\mathrm{K}_{2}$.
What is ${{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}}$ for the reaction
${\text{N}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{1/2}}{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g) + }}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)?}}$
explanation:
$\mathrm {N_{2}+O_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 N O ; K_{1}=\dfrac{[N O]^{2}}{\left[N_{2}\right]\left[O_{2}\right]}}$
$\mathrm {2 \mathrm{NO}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2} ; \mathrm{K}_{2}=\dfrac{\left[N \mathrm{O}_{2}\right]^{2}}{[\mathrm{NO}]^{2}\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]}}$
$\mathrm {\operatorname{Aim} N O_{2} \rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2} N_{2}+O_{2} ; K_{c}=\dfrac{\left[N O_{2}\right]^{1 / 2}\left[O_{2}\right]}{[N O]^{2}}}$
To get aim reverse eqn. (2) $\mathrm {\times \dfrac{1}{2}+e q n .(1) \times \dfrac{1}{2}}$
By observation $\mathrm {K_{c}=\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{K_{1} K_{2}}}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Stoichiometric Representation of Equilibrium Constant

question: In which of the following case, does the reaction go farthest to completion?
explanation: The higher is the value of  $\rm {{{\text{K}}_P}}$ or $\rm {{\text{  }}{{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}}}$, the more is the concentration of product formed.

question:
${\text{3}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{2}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{(g)}}$
for the above reaction at $298 \mathrm{~K}, \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{C}}$ is found to be $3.0 \times 10^{-59}$. If the concentration of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ at equilibrium is $0.040\,\,{\text{M}}$ then concentration of $\mathrm{O}_{3}$ in $\mathrm{M}$ is
explanation:
$\rm {{{\text{3O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{2}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{(g)}}}$
$\mathrm {\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}=\dfrac{\left[\mathrm{O}_{3}\right]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]^{3}}}$
$\mathrm {3 \times 10^{-59}=\dfrac{\left[\mathrm{O}_{3}\right]^{2}}{\left(4 \times 10^{-2}\right)^{3}}}$
$\mathrm {\left[\mathrm{O}_{3}\right]^{2}=3 \times 10^{-59} \times 64 \times 10^{-6}=192 \times 10^{-65}}$
$\mathrm {=19.2 \times 10^{-64}}$
$\mathrm {\left[\mathrm{O}_{3}\right]=4.38 \times 10^{-32} \mathrm{M}}$

subtopic: Calculation of Equilibrium Concentration

question:
Assertion: The equilibrium constant of the exothermic reaction at high temperature decreases.


Reason: Since $\ln \dfrac{\mathrm{K}_{2}}{\mathrm{~K}_{1}}=\dfrac{\Delta \mathrm{H}^{0}}{\mathrm{R}}\left[\dfrac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_{1}}-\dfrac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_{2}}\right]$ and for exothermic reaction, $\Delta H^{0}=-\mathrm{ve}$ and thereby $\dfrac{\mathrm{K}_{2}}{\mathrm{~K}_{1}} < 1$
explanation: Both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of the assertion. So the option (1) is correct

$\mathrm {A_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 A(g)\{20 \%}$ means $\mathrm {\mathrm{x}=0.2\}}$
$\mathrm {1 \quad 0}$

$\mathrm {\begin{array}{ll}0.8 & 0.4\end{array}}$

Given $\mathrm {\Delta H^{\circ}=-29.8 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
$\mathrm {\Delta S^{\circ}=-1.00 \mathrm{kJK}^{-1}}$
From the equation
$\rm { \Delta G=\Delta H^{\circ}-T \Delta S^{\circ}=-29.8-(298 \times 0.100) \\ =-29.8+29.8=0}$
Now, ${\rm{\Delta }}{{\rm{G}}^{\rm{^\circ }}}$ ${\rm{ = - 2}}{\rm{.303 }}$ ${\rm{RT}}$ ${\rm{log}}$ ${{\rm{K}}_{{\rm{eq}}}}$
${\rm{0 = - 2}}{\rm{.303 }}$ ${\rm{RT}}$ ${\rm{log}}$ ${{\rm{K}}_{{\rm{eq}}}}$
$\mathrm {\therefore K_{e q}=1}$

question:
For the decomposition of the compound, represented as
${\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{COON}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{4}}}{\text{(s)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{2N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{(g) + C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g); }}$
${{\text{K}}_{\text{P}}}{\text{ = 2}}.{\text{9}} \times {\text{1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 5}}}}\,{\text{at}}{{\text{m}}^{\text{3}}}$
If the reaction is started with $1 \mathrm{~mol}$ of the compound, the total pressure at equilibrium would be:
explanation:
$\mathrm {\mathrm{NH}_{2} \mathrm{COONH}_{4}(s) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(g)+\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})}$
$\rm {K_{P}=\dfrac{\left(P_{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}\right)^{2} \times\left(P_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}\right)}{P_{\mathrm{NH}_{2}} \mathrm{COONH}_{4}{ }^{(S)}}=\left(P_{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}\right)^{2} \times\left(P_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}\right)}$
As evident by the reaction, $\mathrm {\mathrm{NH}_{3}}$ and $\mathrm {\mathrm{CO}_{2}}$ are formed in molar ratio of $\mathrm {2: 1}$. Thus if $\mathrm {\mathrm{P}}$ is the total pressure of the system at equilibrium, then
$\rm { P_{N H_{3}}=\dfrac{2 \times P}{3} \quad P_{C O_{2}}=\dfrac{1 \times P}{3} \\ K_{P}=\left(\dfrac{2 P}{3}\right)^{2} \times \dfrac{P}{3}=\dfrac{4 P^{3}}{27}}$
Given $\mathrm {K_{P}=2.9 \times 10^{-5}}$
$\rm { \therefore 2.9 \times 10^{-5}=\dfrac{4 P^{3}}{27} \\ P^{3}=\dfrac{2.9 \times 10^{-5} \times 27}{4} \\ P=\left(\dfrac{2.9 \times 10^{-5} \times 27}{4}\right)^{1 / 3}=5.82 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~atm}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between Equilibrium Constant and Total Pressure of Reactions

question: At a certain temperature, only ${\text{50% }}\,\,{\text{HI}}$ is dissociated into $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ and  ${{\text{I}}_{\text{2}}}$ at equilibrium. The equilibrium constant is:

https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764953282/preptracker/questions/q_17400_explanation_1764953281639.png Given, $\alpha = 50\% = 0.5$
${{\text{K}}_{\text{c}}} = \frac{{\left( {\frac{\alpha }{2}} \right)\left( {\frac{\alpha }{2}} \right)}}{{{{\left( {1 - \alpha } \right)}^2}}} = \frac{{0.25 \times 0.25}}{{{{\left( {0.5} \right)}^2}}} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25$

${\text{ }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g) + }}\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{2}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O(g),}}{{\text{K}}_{\text{3}}}$
The equation for the equilibrium constant of the reaction
${\text{2N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{(g) + }}\dfrac{{\text{5}}}{{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{2NO(g) + 3}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O(g),}}\left( {{{\text{K}}_{\text{4}}}} \right)$

question:
The ratio$\dfrac{{{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{p}}}}}{{{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{c}}}}}$ for the reaction
${\text{CO(g) + }}\dfrac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}}$ is:
explanation:
$\mathrm {K_{P}=K_{C}(R T)^{\Delta n_{g}}}$
For the reaction $\mathrm {\mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g})+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})}$
$\mathrm {\Delta n_{g}=1-\left(1+\dfrac{1}{2}\right)=-\dfrac{1}{2}}$
$\mathrm {\therefore \quad K_{P}=\dfrac{K_{C}}{\sqrt{R T}} ; \quad \dfrac{K_{P}}{K_{C}}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{R T}}}$

question: In a reaction  ${\text{A + 2B}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{2C + D}}$, initial concentration of  ${\text{B}}$  was 1.5 times of $[\mathrm{A}]$, but at equilibrium the concentrations of $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ become equal. The equilibrium constant for the reaction is:
explanation:
$\rm {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\text{A}}&{\text{ + }}&{{\text{2B}}}& \rightleftharpoons &{{\text{2C}}}&{\text{ + }}&{\text{D}} \\ {\text{a}}&{}&{{\text{1}}{\text{.5}}}&{}&{\text{0}}&{}&{\text{0}} \\ {{\text{(a - x)}}}&{}&{{\text{(1}}{\text{.5a - 2x)}}}&{}&{{\text{2x}}}&{}&{\text{x}} \end{array}}$
Hence ${{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}}{\text{ = }}\frac{{{\text{(2x)}}{{\text{ }}^{\text{2}}}{\text{ }} \times {\text{ x}}}}{{{\text{(a - x)(1}}{\text{.5a - 2x}}{{\text{)}}^{\text{2}}}}}$
Given, at equilibrium
$\therefore {\text{(a - x) = (1}}{\text{.5a - 2x) }}$
$\therefore {\text{a = 2x}}$
${\text{On solving }}$ ${{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}}{\text{ = 4}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Calculation of Equilibrium Constant for Homogeneous Equilibrium

question: The plot of $\log {{\text{K}}_{{\text{eq}}}}$ Vs $1/{\text{T}}$  shows a slope of 2000. The change in standard enthalpy for the reaction at equilibrium is
explanation:
The plot of $\mathrm {\log \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{eq}}}$ vs $\mathrm {1 / \mathrm{T}}$ gives a slope equal to $\mathrm {-\dfrac{\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}}{2.303 \mathrm{R}}}$, according to the following equation.
$\mathrm {\log \mathrm{K}=-\dfrac{\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}}{2.303 \mathrm{R}}\left(\dfrac{1}{\mathrm{~T}}\right)+\dfrac{\Delta \mathrm{S}^{\circ}}{2.303 \mathrm{R}}}$
$\mathrm {-\dfrac{\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}}{2.303 \mathrm{R}}=-2000}$
$\mathrm {\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=2000 \times 2.303 \mathrm{R}}$
$\mathrm {=2000 \times 2.303 \times 2 \mathrm{cal}=9.2 \mathrm{kcal}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Effect of Temperature on the Equilibrium Constant

question:
\[{{\text{X}}_{{\text{2}}({\text{g}})}}{\mkern 1mu} \] and \[{\mkern 1mu} {{\text{Y}}_{{\text{2}}({\text{g}})}}\] react according to the following equation:
\[{{\text{X}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g) + }}{{\text{Y}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{2XY(g)}}\]
A mixture containing \(4 \mathrm{~mol}\) each of \[{{\text{X}}_{{\text{2}}({\text{g}})}}{\mkern 1mu} \] and \[{\mkern 1mu} {{\text{Y}}_{{\text{2}}({\text{g}})}}\] is heated in a closed container. The system is allowed to reach equilibrium. The graph shows how the number of moles of each gas varies with time.
What is the equilibrium constant \[{\text{ }}{{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}}\] for the reaction?
explanation:
From given graph, At equilibrium, number of moles of $\mathrm {X Y=6 \mathrm{~mol}}$ Number of moles of $\mathrm {X_{2}=1 \mathrm{~mol}}$ and $\mathrm {Y_{2}=1 \mathrm{~mol}}$
Volume is neglected for the reaction as $\mathrm {\Delta \mathrm{n}=0}$
$\rm {\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{\left(\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{XY}}\right)^{2}}{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{X}_{2}} \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{Y}_{2}}}=\dfrac{6^{2}}{(1)(1)}=36}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Calculation of Equilibrium Constant for Homogeneous Equilibrium

question: $0.96 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{HI}$ was heated till the equilibrium  ${\text{2HI(g)}} \rightleftharpoons {{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g) + }}{{\text{I}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}}$ is reached. The reaction mixture was suddenly cooled and the amount of iodine produced required ${\text{15}}{\text{.7 }}{\mkern 1mu} {\text{mL}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \frac{{\text{N}}}{{{\text{10}}}}$  hypo solution. The ${{\text{K}}_{\text{P}}}$ for the above equilibrium reaction is
explanation:
Initial moles of $\rm {{\rm{ HI}}}$=$\dfrac{{{\text{0}}{\text{.96}}}}{{{\text{128}}}}{\text{ = 7}}{\text{.5 1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 3}}}}$
Moles of $\rm {{{\rm{I}}_{\rm{2}}}}$ produced $\mathrm {=\dfrac{15.7 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-3}}{2}}$
$\mathrm {=7.85 \times 10^{-4}}$
Moles of $\mathrm {\mathrm{HI}}$ left undissociated at equilibrium
$\rm {=7.5 \times 10^{-3}-2 \times 7.85 \times 10^{-4}=5.93 \times 10^{-3}}$
For $\mathrm {2 \mathrm{HI} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{I}_{2}}$
$\mathrm {K=\dfrac{\left(7.85 \times 10^{-4}\right)^{2}}{\left(5.93 \times 10^{-3}\right)^{2}}=17.52 \times 10^{-3}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Calculation of Equilibrium Constant for Homogeneous Equilibrium

question:
A sample of ${\text{CaC}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{(s)}}$ introduced into a sealed container of volume $0.654 \mathrm{~L}$ and heated to ${\text{1000}}\,{\text{K}}$ until equilibrium is reached. The equilibrium constant for the reaction,
${\text{CaC}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}\left( {\text{s}} \right) \rightleftharpoons {\text{CaO}}\left( {\text{s}} \right){\text{ + C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right)$ is
$3.9 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~atm}$ at this temperature. The mass
of $\mathrm{CaO}$ present at equilibrium is
explanation:
$\mathrm {K_{P}=P_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}=3.9 \times 10^{-2}}$
$\mathrm {n=\dfrac{P V}{R T}=\dfrac{3.9 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.654}{0.082 \times 1000}=3.11 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mol}}$
no. of moles of $\mathrm {\mathrm{CaO}=3.11 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mol}}$
Mass of $\mathrm {\mathrm{CaO}}$ formed $\mathrm {=3.11 \times 10^{-4} \times 56}$
$\rm {=0.0174 g}$

question:
A 20 litre container at $400 \mathrm{~K}$ contains ${\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_{{\text{2}}\left( {\text{g}} \right)}}$ at pressure $0.4 \mathrm{~atm}$ and an excess of $\mathrm{SrO}$ (neglect the volume of solid ${\text{SrO}}$. The volume of the container is now decreased by moving the movable piston fitted in the container. The maximum volume of the container, when pressure of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ attains its maximum value, will be
(Given that :
${\text{SrC}}{{\text{O}}_{{\text{3(g)}}}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{Sr}}{{\text{O}}_{{\text{(s)}}}}{\text{ + C}}{{\text{O}}_{{\text{2(g)}}}}{\text{,}} {{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}}$=1.6 atm)
explanation:
$\mathrm {\mathrm{SrCO}_{3(s)} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{SrO}_{(s)}+\mathrm{CO}_{2(g)} ; K_{p}=1.6 \mathrm{~atm}}$
$\mathrm {K_{p}=p_{\left(\mathrm{CO}_{2}\right)}\left(\because p_{(\mathrm{SrO})}=p_{\left(\mathrm{SrCO}_{3}\right)}=1\right) ; p_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}=1.6}$
$\mathrm {\therefore}$ Maximum pressure of $\mathrm {\mathrm{CO}_{2}=1.6 \mathrm{~atm}}$
Let the maximum volume of the container when pressure of $\mathrm {\mathrm{CO}_{2}}$ is $\mathrm {1.6 \mathrm{~atm}}$ be VL.
During the process, $\mathrm {\mathrm{PV}=}$ constant
$\mathrm {\therefore \quad P_{1} V_{1}=P_{2} V_{2} \Rightarrow 0.4 \times 20=1.6 \times V}$
$\mathrm {\Rightarrow \quad V=\dfrac{0.4 \times 20}{1.6}=5 L}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between Equilibrium Constant and Total Pressure of Reactions

question:
For the following three reactions equilibrium constants are given
${\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_{{\text{(g)}}}}{\text{ + }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{{\text{(g)}}}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_{{\text{2(g)}}}}{\text{ + }}{{\text{H}}_{{\text{2(g)}}}}{\text{;}}{{\text{K}}_{\text{1}}}$
${\text{ C}}{{\text{H}}_{{\text{4(g)}}}}{\text{ + }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{{\text{(g)}}}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_{{\text{(g)}}}}{\text{ + 3}}{{\text{H}}_{{\text{2(g)}}}}{\text{;}}\,{{\text{K}}_{\text{2}}}$
${\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{{\text{4(g)}}}}{\text{ + 2}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{{\text{(g)}}}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_{{\text{2(g)}}}}{\text{ + 4}}{{\text{H}}_{{\text{2(g)}}}}{\text{;}}{{\text{K}}_{\text{3}}}$
Which of the following relations is correct?
explanation:
${\text{CO(g) + }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O(g)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g) + }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g);}}{{\text{K}}_{\text{1}}}\,\,{\text{(1)}}$
${\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{4}}}{\text{(g) + }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O(g)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{CO(g) + 3}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g);}}{{\text{K}}_{\text{2}}}\,\,{\text{(2)}}$
${\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{4}}}{\text{ + 2}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O(g)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g) + 4}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g);}}{{\text{K}}_{\text{3}}}\,\,{\text{(3)}}$
Eq. (3) $\mathrm {=}$ eq. (1) + eq. (2)
Thus, $\mathrm {K_{3}=K_{1} \cdot K_{2}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Stoichiometric Representation of Equilibrium Constant

question:
The formation of ammonia from nitrogen and hydrogen gases can be written as,
${\text{I}}{\text{. }}\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{2}}}{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g) + }}\frac{{\text{3}}}{{\text{2}}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{(g)}}$
${\text{II}}{\text{. }}\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{3}}}{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g) + }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}} \rightleftharpoons \frac{{\text{2}}}{{\text{3}}}{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{(g)}}$
These two equations have equilibrium constants ${{\text{K}}_{\text{1}}}\,\,{\text{and }}{{\text{K}}_{\text{2}}}$, respectively. The correct relationship between equilibrium constants is
explanation:
For Eq. I; $\mathrm {K_{1}=\dfrac{\left[N H_{3}\right]}{\left[N_{2}\right]^{1 / 2}\left[H_{2}\right]}}$
For Eq. II; $\mathrm {\quad K_{2}=\dfrac{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]^{2 / 3}}{\left[\mathrm{~N}_{2}\right]^{1 / 3}\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Stoichiometric Representation of Equilibrium Constant

question: At $700 \mathrm{~K}$, equilibrium constant for the reaction:${{\text{H}}_{{\text{2(g)}}}}{\text{ + }}{{\text{I}}_{{\text{2(g)}}}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{2H}}{{\text{I}}_{{\text{(g)}}}}$ is ${\text{54}}{\text{.8}}$ . explanation:
Given, $\mathrm {H_{2}+I_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 H I, K_{c}=54.8}$
For, $\mathrm {2 \mathrm{HI} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{I}_{2}, K_{c}^{1}=\dfrac{1}{54.8}}$
$\rm {[H I]=0.5 M}$
Let, $\mathrm {\left[H_{2}\right]=\left[I_{2}\right]=x M}$
$\therefore {\text{K}}_{\text{C}}^{\text{1}}{\text{ = }}\frac{{\left[ {{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}} \right]\left[ {{{\text{I}}_{\text{2}}}} \right]}}{{{{\left[ {{\text{HI}}} \right]}^{\text{2}}}}}$
$\frac{{\text{1}}}{{{\text{54}}{\text{.8}}}}{\text{ = }}\frac{{{\text{(x)(x)}}}}{{{{{\text{(0}}{\text{.5)}}}^{\text{2}}}}}$
on solving, $\mathrm {x=0.0675 M}$
$\rm {\therefore \quad\left[H_{2}\right]=\left[I_{2}\right]=0.0675 M}$

question:
The values of ${\text{K}}{{\text{ }}_{{\text{P1}}}}$ and ${\text{K}}{{\text{ }}_{{\text{P2}}}}$ for the reactions
${\text{X}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{Y + Z }}$
${\mkern 1mu} {\text{A}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{2B}}$
are in the ratio $9: 1$. The total pressure at equilibrium are in the ratio, when the degree of dissociation of ${\rm{X}}$ and ${\rm{A}}$ are equal is
explanation:
${\text{X}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{Y + Z}}$
${\text{A}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{2B}}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764959008/preptracker/questions/q_17071_explanation_1764959007549.png Total number of moles at equilibrium
$\mathrm {=1-\alpha+2 \alpha=1+\alpha}$
$\mathrm {A \rightleftharpoons 2 B}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764959008/preptracker/questions/q_17071_explanation_1764959008496.png Total number of moles at equilibrium
$\mathrm {=1-\alpha+2 \alpha=1+\alpha}$
$\mathrm {\therefore K_{P_{1}}=\dfrac{p_{Y}+p_{Z}}{p_{X}}=\dfrac{\dfrac{\alpha}{1+\alpha} \times P_{1} \times \dfrac{\alpha}{1+\alpha}+P_{1}}{\dfrac{1-\alpha}{1+\alpha} \times P_{1}}}$
${{\text{K}}_{{{\text{P}}_{\text{2}}}}}{\text{ = }}\frac{{{{\left( {{{\text{p}}_{{\text{ B}}}}} \right)}^{\text{2}}}}}{{{{\text{p}}_{{\text{ A}}}}}}{\text{ = }}\frac{{\left( {\frac{{{\text{2}}\alpha }}{{{\text{1 + }}\alpha }} \times {{\text{P}}_{\text{2}}}} \right)}}{{\frac{{{\text{1 - }}\alpha }}{{{\text{1 + }}\alpha }} \times {{\text{P}}_{\text{2}}}}}$
Now, $\mathrm {\dfrac{K_{p_{1}}}{K_{p_{2}}}=\dfrac{P_{1}}{4 P_{2}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{P_{1}}{P_{2}}=\dfrac{36}{1}=36: 1}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between Equilibrium Constant and Total Pressure of Reactions

$\rm {\mathrm{x}=0.0259 \mathrm{~mol}}$
Moles of ${\text{ NO}}$ at equilibrium $\mathrm {=(0.087-2 x)}$
$\rm {=0.087-0.0518=0.0352 \mathrm{~mol}}$
Moles of $\mathrm {\mathrm{Br}_{2}}$ at equilibrium $\mathrm {=(0.0437-\mathrm{x})}$
$\mathrm {=0.0437-0.0259=0.0178 \mathrm{~mol}}$.

question:
The following reaction is performed at $298 \mathrm{~K}$. ${\text{2NO(g) + }}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{2N}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}}$
The standard free energy of formation of${\text{ N}}{{\text{O}}_{{\text{(g)}}}}$ is $86.6 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$ at $298 \mathrm{~K}$. What is the standard free energy of formation of ${\text{N}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}}$ at ${\text{298}}\,{\text{K}}?$ $({{\text{K}}_{\text{P}}}{\text{ = 1}}.{\text{6}} \times {\text{1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{12}}}})$
explanation:
$\Delta {\text{G}}^\circ {\text{NO(g) = 86}}{\text{.6kJ/mol = 86600J/mol}}$
${\text{G}}^\circ {\text{N}}{{\text{O}}_2}({\text{g}}){\text{ = xJ/mol}}$
$\mathrm {T=298 K, K_{P}=1.6 \times 10^{12}}$
$\rm {\Delta G^{\circ}=-R T \ln K_{P}}$
Given equation,
$\rm { 2 \mathrm{NO}(g)+\mathrm{O}_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}(g) \\ \therefore 2 \Delta G^{\circ}{ }_{N O_{2}}-2 \Delta G^{\circ}{ }_{N O}=-R(298) \ln \left(1.6 \times 10^{12}\right) \\ 2 \Delta{G^{\circ}}^{\circ} O_{2}-2 \times 86600=-R(298) \ln \left(1.6 \times 10^{12}\right) \\ 2 \Delta G^{\circ}{ }_{N O_{2}}=2 \times 86600-R(298) \ln \left(1.6 \times 10^{12}\right) \\ \Delta G^{\circ}{ }_{N O_{2}}=\dfrac{1}{2}\left[2 \times 86600-R(298) \ln \left(1.6 \times 10^{12}\right)\right] \\ =0.5\left[2 \times 86600-R(298) \ln \left(1.6 \times 10^{12}\right)\right]}$
subject: Chemistry
topic: Relationship between Equilibrium Constant, Reaction Quotient and Gibbs Free Energy
subtopic: Relationship between Equilibrium Constant, Reaction Quotient and Gibbs Free Energy

question:
The equilibrium constant ${{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}}$ for the reaction ${{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}}{\mkern 1mu} {\text{ + }}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}}{\mkern 1mu} \to {\mkern 1mu} {\text{2NO(g)}}$ at temperature $\mathrm{T}$ is $4 \times 10^{-4}$. The value of ${\rm{ }}{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{C}}}$ for the reaction
${\text{NO(g)}} \to \frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{2}}}{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g) + }}\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}}$ at the same temperature is
explanation:
$\mathrm {N_{2}(g)+O_{2}(g) \rightarrow 2 N O(g)}$
$\rm {K_{C}=\dfrac{[N O]^{2}}{\left[N_{2}\right]\left[O_{2}\right]}=4 \times 10^{-4}}$
For the reverse reaction,
$\mathrm {2NO(g) \rightarrow N_{2}(g)+O_{2}(g)}$
${{{\text{K'}}}_{\text{C}}}{\text{ = }}\frac{{\text{1}}}{{{{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}}}}{\text{ = }}\frac{{\left[ {{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}} \right]\left[ {{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}} \right]}}{{{{\left[ {{\text{NO}}} \right]}^{\text{2}}}}} = \frac{1}{{4 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}} = \frac{{{{10}^4}}}{4}$
On dividing the reverse reaction by 2
$\mathrm {NO(g) \rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2} N_{2}(g)+\dfrac{1}{2} O_{2}(g)}$
$\mathrm{K_{C}^{\prime \prime}=\dfrac{\left[N_{2}\right]^{1 / 2}\left[O_{2}\right]^{1 / 2}}{[N O]}=\sqrt{K_{C}^{\prime}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{10^{4}}{4}}=\dfrac{100}{2}=50}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Stoichiometric Representation of Equilibrium Constant

question: For the reaction ${\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g) + }}\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{(g)}}$,  If  ${{\text{K}}_{\text{P}}}{\text{  =  }}{{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}}{{\text{(RT)}}^{\text{x}}},$  where the symbols have usual meanings, then, the value of  ${\rm{ x}}$ is (assuming ideality)
explanation:
By using this formula, equilibrium constant,
$\rm {K_{P}=K_{C}(R T)^{\Delta n_{g}}}$
Where $\mathrm {\Delta n_{g}=}$ (no.of moles of products) - (no. of moles of reactants)
For the given reaction,
$\rm {S O_{2}(g)+\dfrac{1}{2} O_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons {O}_{3}(g)}$
$\rm{\Delta n_{g}}=1-\left[\dfrac{1}{2}+1\right]=1-\left[\dfrac{3}{2}\right]=-\dfrac{1}{2}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between Equilibrium Constants, \[\rm{K_p}\]  and \[\rm{K_c}\]

question: The value of ${\rm{\Delta G^\circ }}$ for the phosphorylation of glucose in glycolysis is $13.8 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$. Find the value of ${{\rm{K}}_{\rm{C}}}$ at $298 \mathrm{~K}$.
explanation:
$\mathrm {\Delta G^{\circ}=13.8 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}=13.8 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}}$
Also, $\mathrm {\Delta G^{\circ}=-2.303 \mathrm{RT} \log K_{c}}$
Hence, $\rm {\log K_{C}}=-\dfrac{13.8 \times 1000}{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298} \\ \rm{\log K_{C}}=-2.4 \\ \mathrm{K_{C}}=4 \times 10^{-3} .$

question: The value of ${{\text{K}}_{\text{P}}}$ for the reaction, $\mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{C}_{(\mathrm{s})} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{CO}_{(\mathrm{g})}$ is 3.0 at $1000 \mathrm{~K}$. If initially ${{\text{p}}_{{\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}}}{\text{ = 0}}{\text{.48}}$ bar and ${{\rm{p}}_{{\rm{CO}}}}{\rm{ = 0}}$ bar and pure graphite is present, calculate the equilibrium partial pressures of $\mathrm{CO}$ and $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$.
explanation:
For the reaction, let ' $\mathrm {x}$ ' be the decrease in pressure of
${\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{,}}$ then
$\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {}&{{\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_{{\text{2(g)}}}}}&{{\text{  +  }}}&{{\text{C(s)}}}& \rightleftharpoons &{{\text{2CO(g)}}}&{}&{} \\ 
  {{\text{Intial}}}&{}&{}&{{\text{0}}{\text{.48 bar}}}&{}&{\text{0}}&{}&{} \\ 
  {{\text{At}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{equi}}{\mkern 1mu} {\text{m:}}}&{}&{}&{{\text{(0}}{\text{.48  -  x)}}}&{}&{{\text{2x}}{\mkern 1mu} {\text{bar}}}&{}&{} 
\end{array}$
$\rm {K_{P}=\dfrac{\left(P_{C O}\right)^{2}}{P_{C O_{2}}} \\ K_{P}=(2 x)^{2} /(0.48-x)=3 \\ 4 x^{2}=3(0.48-x) \\ 4 x^{2}=1.44-3 x \\ 4 x^{2}+3 x-1.44=0} \\ \mathrm{a}=4, \mathrm{~b}=3, \mathrm{c}=-1.44$
${\text{ x  =  }}\frac{{\left( {{\text{  -  b}} \pm \sqrt {{{\text{b}}^{\text{2}}}{\text{  -  4ac}}} } \right)}}{{{\text{2a}}}}\frac{{\left[ {{\text{  -  3}} \pm \sqrt {{{({\text{3}})}^{\text{2}}}{\text{  -  4}}({\text{4}})({\text{  -  1}}.{\text{44}})} } \right]}}{{{\text{2}} \times {\text{4}}}}{\text{ }}$
${\text{ =  }}({\text{  -  3}} \pm {\text{5}}.{\text{66}}){\text{/8  =  }}({\text{  -  3  +  5}}.{\text{66}}){\text{/8}}$ as value of x cannot be
negative hence we neglect that value)
$\rm {x=2.66 / 8=0.33}$
The equilibrium partial pressures are,
${\text{ }}{{\text{p}}_{{\text{CO}}}}{\text{  =  2x  =  2}} \times {\text{0}}{\text{.33  =  0}}{\text{.66  bar}}$
${{\text{p}}_{{\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}}}{\text{ =  0}}{\text{.48  - }} \times {\text{ =  0}}{\text{.48  - 0}}{\text{.33 = 0}}{\text{.15 bar}}{\text{.}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between Equilibrium Constant and Total Pressure of Reactions

question:
Dihydrogen gas used in Haber's process is produced by reacting methane from natural gas with high temperature steam. The first stage of two stage reaction involves the formation of $\mathrm{CO}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2}$. In second stage, $\mathrm{CO}$ formed in first stage is reacted with more steam in water gas shift reaction,
${\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_{\left( {\text{g}} \right)}}{\text{ + }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\left( {\text{g}} \right)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_{{\text{2}}\left( {\text{g}} \right)}}{\text{ + }}{{\text{H}}_{{\text{2}}\left( {\text{g}} \right)}}$
If a reaction vessel at $400^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is charged with an equimolar mixture of $\mathrm{CO}$ and steam such that ${{\text{p}}_{{\text{CO}}}}{\text{ = }}{{\text{p}}_{{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}}}{\text{ = 4}}{\text{.0}}$ bar, what will be the partial pressure of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ at equilibrium? ${{\rm{K}}_{\rm{P}}}$ =10.1 at $400^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958996/preptracker/questions/q_16914_explanation_1764958995985.png ${\text{ }}{{\text{K}}_{\text{P}}}{\text{ = }}\frac{{{{\text{p}}_{{\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}}} \cdot {{\text{p}}_{{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}}}}}{{{{\text{p}}_{{\text{CO}}}} \cdot {{\text{p}}_{{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}}}}}{\text{ = }}\frac{{{\text{p}} \cdot {\text{p}}}}{{({\text{4 - p}}) \cdot ({\text{4 - p}})}}$
${\text{10}}.{\text{1 = }}\frac{{{{\text{p}}^{\text{2}}}}}{{{{({\text{4 - p}})}^{\text{2}}}}} \Rightarrow \sqrt {{\text{10}}.{\text{1}}} {\text{ = }}\frac{{\text{p}}}{{({\text{4 - p}})}}$
${\text{3}}.{\text{17 = }}\frac{{\text{p}}}{{({\text{4 - p}})}}
{\text{p = }}\frac{{{\text{12}}.{\text{71}}}}{{{\text{4}}.{\text{17}}}}{\text{ = 3}}.{\text{04}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{bar}}$
Hence, ${{\text{p}}_{{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}}}{\text{ = 3}}.{\text{04 bar}}.$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between Equilibrium Constant and Total Pressure of Reactions

question: At $1127 \mathrm{~K}$ and 1 atm pressure, a gaseous mixture of $\mathrm{CO}$ and $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ in equilibrium with soild carbon has $90.55\% \,\,{\text{CO}}$  by mass ${{\text{C}}_{{\text{(s)}}}}{\text{ + C}}{{\text{O}}_{{\text{2(g)}}}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{2C}}{{\text{O}}_{{\text{(g)}}}}$. Calculate ${{\rm{K}}_{\rm{C}}}$  for this reaction at the above temperature.
explanation:
$\mathrm {90.55 \%}$ CO by mass means ${\text{90}}.{\text{55}}\,{\text{g CO}}$ and ${\text{9}}{\text{.45 g C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}$ are present in $\mathrm {100 \mathrm{~g}}$ mixture
No. of moles of $\mathrm {\mathrm{CO}, \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{CO}}=\dfrac{90.55}{28}=3.234 \mathrm{~mol}}$
No. of moles of $\mathrm {\mathrm{CO}_{2}, \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}=\dfrac{9.45}{44}=0.215 \mathrm{~mol}}$
partial pressure of $\mathrm {\mathrm{CO}, \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{CO}}=\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{CO}} \cdot \mathrm{P}_{\text {total }}}$
$\rm { \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{CO}}=0.938 \times 1 \mathrm{~atm}=0.938 \mathrm{~atm} \\ \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}=0.062 \times 1 \mathrm{~atm}=0.062 \mathrm{~atm} \\ \mathrm{C}_{(\mathrm{s})}+\mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{CO}_{(\mathrm{g})} \\ \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{P}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{CO}}^{2}}{\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}}=\dfrac{(0.938)^{2}}{0.062}=14.19 \\ \Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}}=2-1=1 \\ \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{P}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}(\mathrm{RT})^{\Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}}} \\ \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{RT}} \quad\left(\because \Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}}=1\right) \\ \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{14.19}{0.0821 \times 1127}=0.153 .}$

question: For the equilibrium, ${\text{2NOC}}{{\text{l}}_{\left( {\text{g}} \right)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{2N}}{{\text{O}}_{\left( {\text{g}} \right)}}{\text{ + C}}{{\text{l}}_{{\text{2}}\left( {\text{g}} \right)}}$  the value of the equilibrium constant, ${\rm{ }}{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{C}}}$  is $3.75 \times 10^{-6}$ at $1069 \mathrm{~K}$. explanation:
We know that,
$\mathrm {K_{P}=K_{C}(\mathrm{R} T)^{\Delta n}}$
For the above reaction,
$\mathrm {\Delta n=(2+1)-2=1}$

question: For the gaseous reaction $3 \mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{N}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}$, the equilibrium pressures of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ are $0.4 \mathrm{~atm}$  and $0.8 \mathrm{~atm}$ respectively. The total pressure of the equilibrium system is $2.8 \mathrm{~atm}$. The value of $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{P}}$ is
explanation:
At equilibrium,
Total pressure $\mathrm {=p_{N_{2}}+p_{H_{2}}+p_{N H_{3}}}$
$\mathrm {2.8=0.8+0.4+P_{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}}$
$\mathrm {p_{N H_{3}}=1.6 \mathrm{~atm}}$
Now $\mathrm {K_{P}=\dfrac{p_{N H_{3}}^{2}}{p_{N_{2}} \times p_{H_{2}}^{3}}}$.
$\rm {=\dfrac{(1.6)^{2}}{(0.8)(0.4)^{2}}=50 \mathrm{~atm}^{2}}$

question: A mixture of 2 moles of $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ and 8 moles of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ are heated in a 2 lit vessel, till equilibrium is established. At equilibrium, 0.4 moles of $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ was present. The equilibrium concentration of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ will be
explanation:
$\mathrm {\begin{array}{lllc} & \mathrm{N}_{2} & +3 \mathrm{H}_{2} & \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3} \\ \text { Initial (mol/lit) } & 1 & 4 & 0 \\ \text { At Equilibrium } & \text {1-x} & \text {4-3 x} & \text {2 x}\end{array}}$
But, at equilibrium $\left[ {{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}} \right]$=0.2${\text{mol/lit}}$
$\mathrm {\text {1-x}=0.2 \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{lit}}$
${\left[ {{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}} \right]_{{\text{Equilibrium }}}}$=${\text{43}}\,\,{\text{x = 1}}{\text{.6 mol/lit}}$
$\mathrm {\mathrm{x}=0.8 \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{L}}$.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Calculation of Equilibrium Concentration

question:
One mole of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ and one mole of $\mathrm{CO}$ are taken in $10 \mathrm{~L}$ vessel and heated to $725 \mathrm{~K}$. At equilibrium $40 \%$ of water (by mole) reacts with CO according to the equation,
${{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{O}}_{{\rm{(g)}}}}$+${\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_{{\text{(g)}}}} \rightleftharpoons {{\text{H}}_{{\text{2(g)}}}}{\text{ + C}}{{\text{O}}_{{\text{2(g)}}}}$
Calculate the equilibrium constant $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}$ for the reaction.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Calculation of Equilibrium Constant for Homogeneous Equilibrium

question:
An equilibrium mixture for the reaction,
$2{{\text{H}}_2}{{\text{S}}_{({\text{g}})}} \rightleftharpoons 2{{\text{H}}_{2(\,{\text{g}})}} + {{\text{S}}_{2(\,{\text{g}})}}$ has 1 mole of ${{\text{H}}_2}{\text{S}}$, 0.2 mole of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ and 0.8 mole of $\mathrm{S}_{2}$ in $2 \mathrm{~L}$ flask. The value of $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}$ in ${\rm{mol}}$ ${{\rm{L}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$ is
explanation:
${\text{ }}\left[ {{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{S}}} \right]{\text{ = 0}}{\text{.5 M,}}\left[ {{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}} \right]{\text{ = 0}}{\text{.1 M,}}\left[ {{{\text{S}}_{\text{2}}}} \right]{\text{ = 0}}{\text{.4 M}}$
${{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}}{\text{ = }}\frac{{\left[ {{{\text{S}}_{\text{2}}}} \right]\left[ {{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}} \right]}}{{{{\left[ {{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{S}}} \right]}^{\text{2}}}}}{\text{ }}$
${\text{ = }}\frac{{{\text{(0}}{\text{.4)(0}}{\text{.1}}{{\text{)}}^{\text{2}}}}}{{{{{\text{(0}}{\text{.5)}}}^{\text{2}}}}}{\text{ = 0}}{\text{.016}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Calculation of Equilibrium Constant for Homogeneous Equilibrium

question: At equilibrium, the concentrations of $\mathrm{N}_{2}=3.0 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M}, \mathrm{O}_{2}=4.2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M}$ and $\mathrm{NO}=2.8 \times$ $10^{-3} \mathrm{M}$ in a sealed vessel at $800 \mathrm{~K}$. What will be  ${{\rm{K}}_{\rm{C}}}$ for the reaction${{\text{N}}_{{\text{2(g)}}}}{\text{  +  }}{{\text{O}}_{{\text{2(g)}}}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{2N}}{{\text{O}}_{{\text{(g)}}}}?$
explanation:
For the reaction equilibrium constant, $\mathrm {K_{C}}$ can be written as
$\rm{K_{C}=\dfrac{[N O]^{2}}{\left[N_{2}\right]\left[O_{2}\right]}}$
= $\dfrac{\left(2.8 \times 10^{-3} \rm{M}\right)^{2}} {\left(3.0 \times 10^{-3} \rm{M}\right)\left(4.2 \times 10^{-3} \rm{M}\right)}$ =0.622.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Calculation of Equilibrium Constant for Homogeneous Equilibrium

question:
Assertion: Reaction between iron and steam is irreversible if carried out in the open tube.


Reason: An irreversible reaction cannot take place in the reverse direction.
explanation:
Iron reacts with steam to form iron oxide as follows: $3 \mathrm{Fe}+4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}+4 \mathrm{H}_{2}$
In an open tube, continuous exposure to oxygen from the air drives the iron oxidation reaction towards iron oxide formation. But in a controlled environment (isolating iron from the air) the reaction becomes potentially reversible by preventing continuous oxidation. So option (2) is correct.

question:
Assertion: $\mathrm{pH}$ of ammonium chloride solution is less than 7.


Reason: Solution of a salt of weak base and strong acid is acidic.
explanation: Ammonium chloride is formed by the reaction of ammonia $\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)$, a weak base, with hydrochloric acid $(\mathrm{HCl})$, a strong acid. The presence of the strong acid $(\mathrm{HCl})$ in this salt means it can readily furnish more $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ions to the solution. Consequently, this leads to an increase in the concentration of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$. Therefore, the solution is acidic and its $\mathrm{pH}$ is less than 7. So option (1) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Solution of salts formed from strong bases and weak acids are alkaline.


Reason: $\mathrm{pH}$ of such solution is greater than 7 .
explanation:
Salts formed from strong bases and weak acids typically consist of a cation from the strong base and an anion from the weak acid.
Anions from weak acids, such as acetate
$\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}\right)$, may have the ability to accept
protons $\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right.$ions) from water, acting as weak bases. This can result in the formation of hydroxide ions $\left(\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right)$or other species that contribute to the alkalinity of the solution.
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \underset{\text { (anionic hydrolysis) }}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}}+\mathrm{OH}^{-}$
So option (2) is correct.

question: A weak acid ${\text{HX}}$ has the dissociation constant $1 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{M}$. It forms a salt $\mathrm{NaX}$ on reaction with alkali. The degree of hydrolysis of $0.1 \mathrm{M}$ solution of $\mathrm{NaX}$ is
explanation:
$\mathrm{X}^{-}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{HX}+\mathrm{OH}^{-}$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{h}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{w}}}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}}=\dfrac{10^{-14}}{10^{-5}}=10^{-9}$
So, degree of hydrolysis
$\mathrm{h}=\sqrt{\dfrac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{h}}}{\mathrm{C}}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{10^{-9}}{10^{-1}}}=10^{-4}$
Percentage of degree of hydrolysis
$=10^{-4} \times 100=10^{-2} \%=0.01 \%$

question: $\mathrm{pH}$ of a salt solution of weak acid $\left(\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}=4\right)$ and weak base $\left(\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{b}}=5\right)$ at $25^{\circ}$ is
explanation:
$\mathrm{pH}$ of buffer solution of weak acid and weak base is calculated, using the following formula.
$\mathrm{pH}=\dfrac{1}{2}\left[\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{w}}+\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}-\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{b}}\right]$
Given: $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}=4, \mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{b}}=5, \mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{w}}=14$
$\therefore \mathrm{pH}=\dfrac{1}{2}[14+4-5]=6.5$

explanation:

$\begin{aligned}& \quad \mathrm{pH}=7+\dfrac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{pk}_{\mathrm{a}}-\mathrm{pk}_{\mathrm{b}}\right) \\& =7+\dfrac{1}{2}(5.9-5.8) \\& =7.05\end{aligned}$

question: The $\mathrm{pH}$ of  ${\text{0}}{\text{.1}}\,\,{\text{M}}{\mkern 1mu} \,\,{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{4}}}{\text{Cl}}$ solution is 5.13. What will be the dissociation constant of $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}$ ?
explanation:
$\mathrm {\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}}$ is salt of weak base and strong acid, formula for $\mathrm {\mathrm{pH}}$ of its hydrolysis may be calculated as,
$\mathrm {\mathrm{pH}=\dfrac{1}{2}\left[\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{w}}-\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{b}}-\log \mathrm{C}\right]}$
where $\mathrm {\mathrm{pH}=5.13}$
$\mathrm {\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{W}}=-\log \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{W}}=-\log 10^{-14}=14}$
$\mathrm {\log \mathrm{C}=\log 0.1=-1}$
Substituting the values in eq. (1) we get
$\rm { 5.13=\dfrac{1}{2}\left[14-\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{b}}+1\right] \\ 10.26=15-\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{b}} \\ \mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}=4.74 \\ \therefore-\log \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}=\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{b}}=4.74 \\ \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{b}}=\operatorname{anti} \log (-4.74) \\ \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}=1.8 \times 10^{-5}}$

subtopic: Nature and pH of Salt Solutions

question: What is the $\mathrm{pH}$ of ${\text{0}}{\text{.10 M}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{COONa}}$ solution? Hydrolysis constant of sodium acetate is $5.6 \times 10^{-10}$
explanation:
Hydrolysis of the salt may be represented as https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764953306/preptracker/questions/q_17811_explanation_1764953305566.png ${{\text{K}}_{\text{h}}} = \frac{{{{\text{C}}^2}{\alpha ^2}}}{{{\text{C}}(1 - \alpha )}} = {\text{C}}{\alpha ^2}{\text{ when }}\alpha < < 1$
$\alpha = \sqrt {\left( {\frac{{{{\text{K}}_{\text{h}}}}}{{\text{C}}}} \right)} = \sqrt {\left( {\frac{{5.6 \times {{10}^{ - 10}}}}{{0.10}}} \right)} = 7.5 \times {10^{ - 5}}{\text{ }}$
$\left[ {{\text{O}}{{\text{H}}^ - }} \right] = {\text{C}}\alpha = 0.10 \times 7.5 \times {10^{ - 5}}$
${\text{pOH}} = - \log \left( {7.5 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}} \right) = 5.126$
$\mathrm {\mathrm{pH}=14-\mathrm{pOH}=14-5.126=8.874}$

question: Equimolar solutions of the following compounds are prepared separately in water. Which will have the lowest $\mathrm{pH}$ value?
explanation:
Metal halide on hydrolysis with water form corresponding hydroxides.
The basic strength of hydroxide increases as we move down in a group. This is because of the increase in size which results in decrease of ionisation energy which weakens the strength of $\mathrm {M-O}$ bonds in $\mathrm {\mathrm{MOH}}$ and thus increases the basic strength.
$\rm { \underset{\text { Amphoteric }}{\mathrm{Be}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}} \underset{\text { Weak base }}{\mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}} \\ \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2} \underset{\text { Strong base }}{\mathrm{Sr}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}} \mathrm{Ba}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}}$
Hence, $\mathrm {\mathrm{Be}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}}$ will have lowest $\mathrm {\mathrm{pH}}$.

question: The extent of hydrolysis of  ${\text{0}}{\text{.0050 M}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\text{K}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{Cr}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}$ is (The ionisation constants of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}$ are ${\text{K}}{{\text{a}}_{\text{1}}}{\text{ = 1}}{\text{.6, K}}{{\text{a}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ =  3}}{\text{.1 }} \times {\text{ 1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{  - 7}}}}{\text{)}}$
explanation:
Just as in the ionisation of polyprotic acids, so in the hydrolysis of their salts, the reaction proceeds in successive stages. The extent of the second stage is generally very small compared with the first. This is particularly true in this case, where $\mathrm {\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}}$ is essentially a strong acid with respect to its first ionisation. The equation of interest is
$\mathrm {\mathrm{CrO}_{4}^{2-}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{HCrO}_{4}^{-}+\mathrm{OH}^{-}}$
Which indicates that the conjugate acid of hydrolysing $\mathrm {\mathrm{CrO}_{4}^{2-}}$ is $\mathrm {\mathrm{HCrO}_{4}^{-}}$. As the ionisation constant for $\mathrm {\mathrm{HCrO}_{4}^{-}}$is $\mathrm {\mathrm{Ka}_{2}}$, the hydrolysis constant for the reaction is $\mathrm {K_{W} / \mathrm{Ka}_{2}}$.
$\rm { \dfrac{\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]\left[\mathrm{HCrO}_{4}^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{CrO}_{4}^{2-}\right]}=K_{h} \\ =\dfrac{K_{w}}{\mathrm{Ka}}=\dfrac{10^{-14}}{3.1 \times 10^{-7}}=3.2 \times 10^{-8}}$
Let $\mathrm {x=\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=\left[\mathrm{HCrO}_{4}^{-}\right]}$.
Then $\mathrm {\left[\mathrm{CrO}_{4}^{2-}\right]=0.005-x \approx 0.005}$ and
$\rm { \dfrac{x^{2}}{0.005}=3.2 \times 10^{-8} \\ x=1.3 \times 10^{-5}}$
$\mathrm {\%}$ hydrolysis $\mathrm {=\left(1.3 \times 10^{-5} \times 100\right) / 0.005=0.26 \%}$

question: The dissociation constants of aniline, acetic acid and water at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ are respectively, $3.83 \times 10^{-10}$, $1.75 \times 10^{-15}$ and $1.008 \times 10^{-14}$. What is the percentage hydrolysis of aniline acetate in a decimolar solution?
explanation:
The degree of hydrolysis ' $\mathrm {h}$ ' of a salt of a weak base is given by $\rm {{\rm{h = }}\sqrt {\dfrac{{{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{w}}}}}{{{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{a}}}{\rm{ \times }}{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{b}}}}}} }$
$\rm {{\rm{ = }}\sqrt {\dfrac{{{\rm{1}}{\rm{.008 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{ - 4}}}}}}{{{\rm{1}}{\rm{.75 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{ - 5}}}}{\rm{ \times 3}}{\rm{.83 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{ - 10}}}}}}} {\rm{ = 1}}{\rm{.219}}}$
But, ' $\mathrm {h}$ ' cannot be greater than 1. This indicates that the degree of hydrolysis in this case is very large and therefore, the above formula, which is based on the assumption that ' $\mathrm {h}$ ' is small, is not applicable.
In the present case, therefore, we have to calculate ' $\mathrm {h}$ ' as follows:
${{\text{K}}_{\text{h}}}{\text{ = }}\frac{{{{\text{K}}_{\text{w}}}}}{{{{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}} \times {{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}}}{{\text{K}}_{\text{h}}}{\text{ = }}\frac{{{{\text{h}}^{\text{2}}}}}{{{{{\text{(1 - h)}}}^{\text{2}}}}}\frac{{\text{h}}}{{{\text{1 - h}}}}{\text{ = }}\sqrt {{{\text{K}}_{\text{h}}}} $
${\text{ = }}\sqrt {\frac{{{{\text{K}}_{\text{w}}}}}{{{{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}} \times {{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}}}} {\text{ = }}\sqrt {{\text{1}}{\text{.219}}} {\text{ = 0}}{\text{.5495}}$
Percentage hydrolysis $\mathrm {=55}$

question: The ionisation constant of nitrous acid is $4.5 \times$ $10^{-4}$. The $\mathrm{pH}$ and degree of hydrolysis of 0.04 $\mathrm{M}$ sodium nitrite solution are, respectively.
explanation:
$Given,{\text{ }}\left[ {{\text{NaN}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}} \right]{\text{ = 0}}.{\text{04 M}}$
$\mathrm {\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}}$ of $\mathrm {\mathrm{HNO}_{2}=4.5 \times 10^{-4}}$
$\mathrm {p K_{a}=-\log K_{a}=-\log \left(4.5 \times 10^{-4}\right)}$
$\mathrm {p K_{a}=-4-0.6532=3.3468 \approx 3.35}$
$\mathrm {\mathrm{pH}=7+\dfrac{p K_{a}+\log \mathrm{C}}{2}}$
$\mathrm {\mathrm{pH}=7+\dfrac{3.35+\log 0.04}{2}=7.98}$
$\mathrm {\mathrm{pH}=7.98}$.
Degree of hydrolysis,
$\mathrm {h=\sqrt{\dfrac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{w}}}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{C}}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{10^{-14}}{4.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.04}}}$

NaCl is not hydrolysed in aqueous solutions. Its $\mathrm {\mathrm{pH}=7}$. $\mathrm {\mathrm{NaCN}}$ undergoes hydrolysis in solution to give alkaline solution. question: For a concentrated solution of a weak electrolyte$\mathrm{A}_{2} \mathrm{~B}_{3}$ of concentration ' c ', the degree of dissociation ' $\alpha$ ' is : ( $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{eq}}=$ equilibrium constant)
explanation:
$\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {}&{{{\text{A}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{B}}_{\text{3}}}}& \rightleftharpoons &{2\,{{\text{A}}^{ + 3}}}&{ + 3\,{{\text{B}}^{ - 2}}} \\ \begin{gathered} {\text{Initial}}\,\, \hfill \\ {\text{conc}}{\text{.}} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} &{\text{c}}&{}&0&0 \\ {{\text{At}}\,\,{\text{eqm}}{\text{.}}}&{{\text{c}}(1 - \alpha )}&{}&{2\,{\text{c}}\alpha }&{3\,{\text{c}}\alpha } \end{array}$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{eq}}=\dfrac{(2 \mathrm{c} \alpha)^{2}(3 \mathrm{c} \alpha)^{3}}{\mathrm{c}(1-\alpha)}=\dfrac{108 \mathrm{c}^{5} \alpha^{5}}{\mathrm{c}}$
$(\because 1-\alpha \approx 1$ as $\alpha$ is very small)
or $\mathrm{K}_{\text {eq }}=108 \mathrm{c}^{4} \alpha^{5}$
or $\alpha=\left(\dfrac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{eq}}}{108 \mathrm{c}^{4}}\right)^{1 / 5}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Degree of Dissociation and Factors Influencing It

question:
If ${\text{A}}{{\text{g}}^ + } + 2{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_3} \rightleftharpoons {\left[ {{\text{Ag}}{{\left( {{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_3}} \right)}_2}} \right]^ + };{{\text{K}}_{\text{1}}}{\text{ = 1}}{\text{.7}} \times {\text{1}}{{\text{0}}^{\text{7}}}$
${\text{A}}{{\text{g}}^ + } + {\text{C}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{AgCl}};{{\text{K}}_2} = 5.4 \times {10^9}$
${\text{Then for AgCl}} + 2{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_3} \rightleftharpoons {\left[ {{\text{Ag}}{{\left( {{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_3}} \right)}_2}} \right]^ + } + {\text{C}}{{\text{l}}^ - }$ equilibrium constants will be
explanation:
For reaction
$\mathrm {\mathrm{Ag}^{+}+2 \mathrm{NH}_{3} \rightleftharpoons\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right]^{+} ; K_{1}=1.7 \times 10^{7}}$
For $\mathrm {\mathrm{Ag}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{AgCl} ; K_{2}=5.4 \times 10^{9}}$
Thus for
${\text{AgCl}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{A}}{{\text{g}}^{\text{ + }}}{\text{ + C}}{{\text{l}}^{\text{ - }}}$
${{{\text{K'}}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ = }}\frac{{\text{1}}}{{{{\text{K}}^{\text{2}}}}}{\text{ = }}\frac{{\text{1}}}{{{\text{5}}{\text{.4}} \times {\text{1}}{{\text{0}}^{\text{9}}}}}$
Now for the reaction
$\rm {\mathrm{AgCl}+2 \mathrm{NH}_{3} \rightleftharpoons\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right]^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}}$
$\mathrm {K=K_{1} \times K_{2}^{\prime}=1.7 \times 10^{7} \times \dfrac{1}{5.4 \times 10^{9}}=0.31 \times 10^{-2}}$

question: If $0.1 \mathrm{M}$ of a weak acid is taken and its percentage degree of ionisation is $1.34 \%$, then its ionisation constant will be
explanation:
According to Ostwald's dilution law, $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{C} \alpha^{2}}{1-\alpha}$For very weak acid
$\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {\alpha < < < < 1{\mkern 1mu} {\text{or}}{\mkern 1mu} 1 - \alpha \approx 1} \\ {\,{{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}} = {\text{C}}{\alpha ^2}\,\,} \\ {{\text{C}} = 0.1{\text{M}},\alpha = 1.34\% = 0.0134} \\ {\therefore {{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}} = 0.1 \times {{\left( {1.34 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}} \right)}^2}\,} \\ { = 1.79 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}} \end{array}$
subject: Chemistry

question:
BOH is a weak base. Molar concentration of
BOH that provides a $\left[ {{\text{O}}{{\text{H}}^ - }} \right]\,\,{\text{of}}\,1.5 \times {10^{ - 3}}{\text{M}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\text{is}}$
${{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}} = ({\text{BOH}}) = 1.5 \times {10^{ - 5}}$
explanation:
$\mathrm{BOH} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{B}^{+}+\mathrm{OH}^{-}$
$\begin{array}{l} {{\rm{K}}_{\rm{b}}} = \frac{{\left[ {{{\rm{B}}^ + }} \right]\left[ {{\rm{O}}{{\rm{H}}^ - }} \right]}}{{{\rm{[BOH]}}}}\\ {\rm{As}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \left[ {{{\rm{B}}^ + }} \right] = \left[ {{\rm{O}}{{\rm{H}}^{\rm{ - }}}} \right]\\ {{\rm{K}}_{\rm{b}}} = \frac{{{{\left[ {{\rm{O}}{{\rm{H}}^ - }} \right]}^2}}}{{[{\rm{BOH}}]}}\\ \therefore \,[{\rm{BOH}}] = \frac{{{{[{\rm{O}}{{\rm{H}}^{\rm{ - }}}]}^2}}}{{{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{b}}}}}\\ [{\rm{BOH}}] = \frac{{{{\left( {1.5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}} \right)}^2}\,{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{b}}}}}{{1.5 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}}}\\ = 1.5 \times {10^{ - 1}} = 0.15{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{M}}\\ \end{array}$

question: The degree of ionization of the two monobasic acids are in the ratio of $1: 10$ and their ionization constants are in the ratio of $1: 50$, then what is the ratio of their concentrations?
explanation:
Degree of dissociation, $\alpha=\sqrt{\dfrac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}}{\mathrm{C}}}$
(or) $\mathrm{C}=\dfrac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}}{\alpha^{2}}$
so, $\mathrm{C}=\dfrac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{1}}}{\mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{a}_{2}}} \times \dfrac{\alpha_{2}^{2}}{\alpha_{1}^{2}}$
$=\dfrac{1}{50} \times \dfrac{10^{2}}{1}$
$=2$

question: The $\mathrm{pH}$ of monoacidic weak base is 10.9 . Calculate the percent dissociation in $0.02 \mathrm{M}$ solution.
explanation:
$\mathrm{pOH}=14-\mathrm{pH}=14-10.9=3.1$
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{pOH}=-\log _{10}\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right] \\& \begin{aligned}\therefore \log _{10}\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right] & =-\mathrm{pOH} \\& =-3.1 \\& =-3-0.1-1+1 \\& =\overline{4}+0.9=\overline{4} .9\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$
$\therefore\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=\operatorname{antilog}(\overline{4} .9)=7.943 \times 10^{-4}$
For monoacidic base,
$\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=\propto \mathrm{c}$
$\therefore \propto=\dfrac{\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]}{\mathrm{c}}$
$=\dfrac{7.943 \times 10^{-4}}{0.02}=3.97 \times 10^{-2}$
Percent dissociation $=\propto \times 100$
$=3.97 \times 10^{-2} \times 100$
$=3.97$

question: At $298 \mathrm{~K}, 0.1 \mathrm{M}$ solutin of acetic acid is $1.34 \%$ ionized. What is the dissociation constant of acetic acid?
explanation:
$\mathrm{c}=0.1 \mathrm{M}, \propto \mathrm{c}=1.34 \%=\dfrac{1.34}{100}=1.34 \times 10^{-2}$
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}=\propto^{2} \mathrm{c} \\& =\left(1.34 \times 10^{-2}\right)^{2} \times 0.1 \\& =1.8 \times 10^{-5}\end{aligned}$

question: A weak monobasic acid is $10 \%$ dissociated in $0.05 \mathrm{M}$ solution. What is its percentage dissociation in $0.10 \mathrm{M}$ solution?
explanation:
$\propto_{1}=10 \%=\dfrac{10}{100}=0.1, \mathrm{c}_{1}=0.05 \mathrm{M}$
$\mathrm{c}_{2}=0.10 \mathrm{M}, \propto_{2}=?$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}=\mathrm{C} \alpha^{2}$ So, $\mathrm{C}_{1} \alpha_{1}^{2}=\mathrm{C}_{2} \alpha_{2}^{2}$
$\therefore(0.1)^{2} \times 0.05=\propto_{2}^{2} \times 0.10$
$\therefore \propto_{2}^{2}=\dfrac{10^{-2} \times 5 \times 10^{-2}}{10^{-1}}=50 \times 10^{-4}$
$\therefore \propto_{2}=\sqrt{50 \times 10^{-4}}=7.17 \times 10^{-2}=7.17 \%$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Ionization Constant of Weak Acid, \[\rm{K_a}\]

question: The dissociation constant of weak monobasic acid is $2.7 \times 10^{-5}$. If degree of dissociation of acid is $3 \times 10^{-2}$, what is the concentration of acid?

$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}=\mathrm{c} \propto^{2}$
$\therefore \mathrm{c}=\dfrac{\mathrm{K}}{\propto^{2}}=\dfrac{2.7 \times 10^{-5}}{\left(3 \times 10^{-2}\right)^{2}}=\dfrac{2.7 \times 10^{-5}}{9 \times 10^{-4}}=0.03 \mathrm{M}$

question: What is the molar concentration of weak monobasic acid if dissociation constant is $5 \times 10^{-8}$ and undergoes $0.5 \%$ dissociation?

$\therefore \mathrm{c}=\dfrac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}}{\propto^{2}}=\dfrac{5 \times 10^{-8}}{\left(5 \times 10^{-3}\right)^{2}}=\dfrac{5 \times 10^{-8}}{25 \times 10^{-6}}$

question: The $\mathrm{pH}$ of $0.1 \mathrm{M}$ solution of monobasic acid is 2.34. Calculate the degree of dissociation of the acid.

(i) $\mathrm{pH}=-\log _{10}\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]$
$\therefore\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=4.571 \mathrm{M}$
(ii) $\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=\propto \mathrm{c}$
$\therefore \propto=\dfrac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]}{\mathrm{c}}=\dfrac{4.571 \times 10^{-3}}{0.1}=4.571 \times 10^{-2}$

question: At $-50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the self-ionisation constant (ionic  product) of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ is ${{\text{K}}_{{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}}}$=$\left[ {{\text{NH}}_{\text{4}}^{\text{ + }}} \right]\left[ {{\text{NH}}_{\text{2}}^{\text{ - }}} \right]{\text{ = 1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 30}}}}{{\text{M}}^{\text{2}}}$ . How many amide ions are present per $\mathrm{mm}^{3}$ of pure liquid ammonia?
explanation:
$\mathrm {2 \mathrm{NH}_{3(l)} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}+\mathrm{NH}_{2}^{-}}$
$\rm {K_{N H_{3}}=x^{2}=10^{-30}}$
Thus, ${\text{x = 1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 15}}}}{\text{ M = }}\left[ {{\text{NH}}_{\text{2}}^{\text{ - }}} \right]$
$\mathrm {\left[N H_{2}^{-}\right]=10^{-15} \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{L}=\dfrac{10^{-15}}{10^{6}} \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{mm}^{3}}$
$\mathrm {\left[\mathrm{NH}_{2}^{-}\right]=10^{-21} \times 6 \times 10^{23}}$ ions $\mathrm {/ \mathrm{mm}^{3}=600}$ ions $\mathrm {/ \mathrm{mm}^{3}}$

question: The ionisation constant of dimethylamine is $5.4 \times 10^{-4}$. Calculate percentage ionisation of ${\text{0}}{\text{.02}}\,\,{\text{M}}$ solution in ${\text{0}}{\text{.1}}\,\,{\text{M NaOH}}$?
explanation:
In the presence of ${\text{0}}.{\text{1 M NaOH}}$, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764953236/preptracker/questions/q_17155_explanation_1764953235819.png [dimethylamine is a weak base and $\mathrm {\mathrm{NaOH}}$ is a strong base so we can assume that $\mathrm {(\mathrm{C} \alpha+0.1) \approx 0.1]}$
$\mathrm {\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}=\dfrac{\left[\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \stackrel{+}{\mathrm{N}} \mathrm{H}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]}{\left[\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{NH}\right]}}$
or $\mathrm {5.4 \times 10^{-4}=\dfrac{\mathrm{C} \alpha \times 0.1}{0.02}}$
$\mathrm {\mathrm{C} \alpha=\dfrac{5.4 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.02}{0.1}=108 \times 10^{-6}}$
$\mathrm {\alpha=\dfrac{108 \times 10^{-6}}{0.02}=54 \times 10^{-4}=5.4 \times 10^{-3}}$.
$\mathrm {\alpha=0.54 \%}$.
In the presence of strong base, dissociation of weak base i.e., dimethylamine decreases due to common ion effect.

question:

${{\text{K}}_{\text{1}}}{\text{ = 4}}{\text{.2}} \times {\text{1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 7}}}}{\text{ and }}{{\text{K}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ = 4}}{\text{.8}} \times {\text{1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 11}}}}$

$\mathrm {\mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-} ; \mathrm{K}_{2}=4.8 \times 10^{-11}}$
$\mathrm {\because \quad K_{1} \gg K_{2}}$, so, $\mathrm {\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}}$ ionises more than $\mathrm {\mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-}}$ and hence, contribution of $\mathrm {\mathrm{H}^{+}}$is mostly due to ionisation of carbonic acid, thus, the concentrations of $\mathrm {\mathrm{H}^{+}}$and $\mathrm {\mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-}}$are approximately equal.

question: A 0.1 molar solution of weak base $\mathrm{BOH}$ is $1 \%$ dissociated. If 0.2 mole of $\mathrm{BCl}$ is added in 1 litre solution of $\mathrm{BOH}$, the degree of dissociation of $\mathrm{BOH}$ will become
explanation:
$\mathrm {K_{b}=C \alpha^{2}=1 \times 10^{-5}}$
In the presence of 0.2 moles $\mathrm {\mathrm{BCl}}$

$\mathrm {0.1-0.1 \alpha=0.1}$ Since $\mathrm {\alpha}$ is very less.
$\rm {K_{b}=\dfrac{0.2 \times 0.1 \alpha}{0.1} \Rightarrow 10^{-5}=2 \times 10^{-1} \times \alpha \\ \therefore \quad \alpha=5 \times 10^{-5}}$

explanation: Statement A is false because forming a complex with a reactant effectively reduces its concentration, shifting the equilibrium to produce less product. Le Chatelier's principle provides a qualitative understanding of the direction of shift in equilibrium when a stress is applied.
explanation: Statement  A  is false as Le Chatelier's principle does not provide quantitative predictions about how much the equilibrium  will   shift. Catalysts alter the equilibrium position by making one of the pathways (forward or reverse) more favorable.
Catalysts decrease the time required to reach equilibrium without affecting the position of equilibrium.
explanation: Statement  A  is false because catalysts do not favor any specific direction; they accelerate both forward and reverse reactions equally. Statement B is true as catalysts only decrease the time to reach equilibrium.   Correct option is (3).

A decrease in temperature will shift the equilibrium of an exothermic reaction to the left.
Exothermic reactions release heat, so increasing temperature favors the forward reaction to produce more heat.
explanation: Statement  A  is true as decreasing temperature shifts the equilibrium to the left for an exothermic reaction, favoring the reactants to absorb heat. Statement B is false because it incorrectly states that the forward reaction is favored; actually, the reverse reaction is favored to absorb the lost heat.   Correct option is (2).

question:

(ii) $\mathrm{X}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{Y}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{XY}(\mathrm{g})$
(iii) $\mathrm{X}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{Z}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{XZ}(\mathrm{g})$
(iv) $\mathrm{X}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{Y}_{4}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{XY}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
explanation: Increase in pressure favours lesser volume side (less moles side). So, option 3 is the correct answer.

question:
Aqueous solution of ferric nitrate when mixed with aqueous solution of potassium thiocyanate gives red colour solution. The intensity of red colour becomes constant on attaining equilibrium. Choose the correct statement when the following chemical is added to the above solution at equilibrium.
I. Oxalic acid
II. Mercuric chloride
explanation:
$\because$ When ferric nitrate is mixed with aqueous solution of potassium thiocyanate, i.e. (KSCN), the intensity of red colour becomes constant on attaining equilibrium.
When, (I) oxalic acid, i.e. $\left(\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}\right)$ or (II)mercuric chloride, i.e.. $\left(\mathrm{HgCl}_{2}\right)$ is added at equilibrium. Both (I) and (II) will decrease the intensity of red colour, due to formation of complex.
Hence, option (1) is the correct answer.

options.text: Both (I) and (II) will decrease the intensity of red colour.
Both (I) and (II) will increase the intensity of red colour.
(I) will increase but (II) will decrease the intensity of red colour.
(I) will decrease but (II) will increase the intensity of red colour.

For the reaction

question: Match the columns.
explanation:
$({\rm{A}} - {\rm{P}},{\rm{Q}},{\rm{R}})$ it is an endothermic reaction, hence on increasing temperature, reaction proceeds in forward direction. Addition of inert gas, at constant pressure favours more volumes side. Decrease in pressure also favours more volumes side
$({\rm{B}} - {\rm{Q}},{\rm{R}},{\rm{S}})$ It is exothermic reaction and no. of gaseous moles are increasing, So, Low, T, Low P and inert gas addition (at constant P ) favours forward reaction.
$({\rm{C}} - {\rm{P}})$ It is an endothermic reaction therefore on increasing temperature reaction proceed in product side. $\Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}}=0$, so, no effect of $\mathrm{P} \&$ inert gas addition. So, low T forward reaction. side.
$({\rm{D}} - {\rm{S}})$ exothermic reaction $\Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}}=0$, so, no effect of P & inert gas addition.

At constant volume, addition of He will not affect the equilibrium. At constant pressure, the addition of inert gas (helium) will favour more number of moles side.
$\mathrm{PCl}_{5(\mathrm{~g})} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{PCl}_{3(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$
Hence, more amount of $\mathrm{PCl}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ will be produced.

options.text: addition of helium will not affect the equilibrium.
the equilibrium will shift in the forward direction and more of $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{PCl}_{3}$ gases will be produced.
the equilibrium will go backward due to suppression of dissociation of $\mathrm{PCl}_{5}$
helium will deactivate $\mathrm{PCl}_{5}$ and reaction will stop.
