question:
Assertion: Aqueous solution of $\mathrm{CoCl}_{2}$ is pink in colour. It turns blue in presence of conc $\mathrm{HCl}$.

Reason: It is due to the formation of $\left[\mathrm{CoCl}_{4}\right]^{2-}$.
explanation:
${\left[ {\mathop {{\text{Co}}{{\left( {{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}} \right)}_{\text{6}}}}\limits_{{\text{ Pink }}} } \right]^{{\text{2 + }}}}{\text{ + 4C}}{{\text{l}}^{\text{ - }}} \rightleftharpoons \left[ {\mathop {{\text{CoC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{4}}}}\limits_{{\text{ Blue }}} } \right]{\text{ + 6}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}$
According to Le Chatelier's principle, on adding conc. $\mathrm{HCl}$ the equilibrium shifts in forward direction giving blue colour and when this blue colour is diluted, equilibrium shifts in backward direction leading to pink colour. So option (1) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

explanation: When a solid is heated, it undergoes a phase transition into a liquid. As a result, the forward reaction is favored, and the amount of solid will decrease. So option (1) is correct.

Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

Reason: According to Le Chatelier principles, reaction shifts in a direction that tends to undo the effect of the stress.
explanation: There is no change in number of gaseous moles. Therefore, the expression for $\mathrm{K}$ is independent of volume. Hence, $\mathrm{K}$ will remain same. So option (2) is correct.

question: In which of the equilibria, the position of the equilibrium shifts towards products, if the total pressure is increased
explanation:
Increase in pressure favours less moles side.
${\rm{i}}{\rm{.e}}{\rm{.,}}\,\,\Delta {\rm{n}} = - {\rm{ve.}}$
$(1)\,\,{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ + 3}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{2N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}\,;\,\,\Delta {\text{n = }} - 2$
${\text{(2)}}\,\,{{\text{I}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ + }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{2HI }}\,{\text{; }}\Delta {\text{n = 0}}$
${\text{(3)}}\,\,{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ + }}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{2NO}}\,{\text{;}}\Delta {\text{n = 0}}$
${\text{(4)}}\,\,{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{2N}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}\,{\text{;}}\,\Delta {\text{n = + 1.}}$
Hence, option (1) is correct.

As a result,
explanation:
$\mathrm {\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{\left[\mathrm{SO}_{3}\right]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{SO}_{2}\right]^{2}\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]}}$
If volume is reduced to half, $\mathrm {\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}}$ will decrease to half. Thus, to maintain the equilibrium, the reaciton should shift in the forward direction, i.e. towards right. Also, to attain equilibrium back, the rate of forward direction will become double the rate of backward direction.

The equilibrium will shfit to the right

explanation: $\mathrm {\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{6}^{2+}}$ is pink while $\mathrm {\mathrm{CoCl}_{4}^{2-}}$ is blue. So, on cooling the reaction tries to overcome the effect of temperature and goes in backward direction (Le - Chatelier's principle). Both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of the assertion. So the option (1) is correct

$\mathrm {\mathrm{PCl}_{5} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{PCl}_{3}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2}}$
At constant pressure, when $\mathrm {\mathrm{He}}$ is added to the equilibrium, volume increases. Thus, in order to maintain the $\mathrm {\mathrm{K}}$ constant, degree of dissociation of $\mathrm {\mathrm{PCl}_{5}}$ increases. Moreover, He (helium) is unreactive towards chlorine gas.
So, the option (1) is correct.

Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

question:

The addition of an inert gas at constant volume does not change the partial pressure or the molar concentrations of the substance involved in the reaction.
explanation: If the volume is kept constant and an inert gas such as argon is added which does not take part in the reaction, the equilibrium remains undisturbed. It is because the addition of an inert gas at constant volume does not change the partial pressure or the molar concentration of reaction quotient changes only if the added gas is reactant or product involved in the reaction. So, the option (3) is correct.

At equilibrium, the Gibbs free energy change ($\Delta \mathrm{G}$) of a system is zero.

explanation: Statement A is true because $\Delta \mathrm{G}$ at equilibrium is zero; indicating no further net change in the state of the system. question: Find the value of the equilibrium constant (K) of a reaction at 300 K, when standard Gibbs free energy change is $-25 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ ?  (Consider $\mathrm{R}=8.33 \mathrm{Jmol}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$ )
explanation:
$\Delta {\text{G}}^\circ = - {\text{RT}}\ln {\text{K}} - 25\,{\text{kJ}}\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}$ $ = \left( { - 8.314\,{\text{J}}\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}\,{{\text{K}}^{ - 1}} \times 300\,{\text{K}}} \right)\ln {\text{K}}$
$\ln \mathrm{K}=\left(-\dfrac{25 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}{8.314 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \times 300 \mathrm{~K}}\right)$
$=\dfrac{25 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}{8.314 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \times 300 \mathrm{~K}}$
$\ln \mathrm{K}=-10.02, \mathrm{~K}=\mathrm{e}^{10.02}$
Hence, option (3) is correct.

question: The standard Gibb's free energy change, $\Delta {\text{G}}^\circ $ is related to equilibrium constant, ${{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}}$ as
explanation:
Standard free energy change, $\Delta {\text{G}}^\circ $ and equilibrium constant ${{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}}$ are related as
$\Delta {\text{G}}^\circ = - {\text{RT}}\,\,{\text{ln}}\,\,{{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}}$
${\text{ln}}\,\,{{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}} = - \frac{{\Delta {\text{G}}^\circ }}{{{\text{RT}}}} \Rightarrow {{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}} = {{\text{e}}^{ - \Delta {{\text{G}}^{\text{^\circ }}}{\text{/RT}}}}$

question:
Match Column I with Column II and choose the correct combination from the options given
Match Column I (Equations) with Column II (Types of process) and select the correct option.
explanation:
(1) When ${{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}} > {\text{Q}},$ the reaction goes in forward direction, i.e., the reaction is spontaneous.
(2)$\Delta {\text{G}} = \Delta {\text{G}}^\circ + {\text{RT}}\,\,{\text{ln}}Q,\Delta {\text{G}}^\circ < {\text{RT}}\,\,{\text{ln}}\,{\text{Q}}$, thus, $\Delta {\text{G}} = + {\text{ve}}$ and hence, the reaction is non-spontaneous.
(3) At equilibrium, ${{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}} = {\text{Q}}$
(4) ${\text{T}} > \frac{{\Delta {\text{H}}}}{{\Delta {\text{S}}}}$ or ${\text{T}}\Delta {\text{S}} > \Delta {\text{H}}$
This condition is true for spontaneous, endothermic reactions $({\text{as}}\,\,\Delta {\text{G}} = \Delta {\text{H}} - {\text{T}}\Delta {\text{S}})$.

question:
The standard Gibbs energy change at 300 K for the reaction, $2 \mathrm{~A} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{B}+\mathrm{C}$ is 2494.2 J . At a given time, the composition of the reaction mixture is $[\mathrm{A}]=\dfrac{1}{2},[\mathrm{~B}]=2$ and $[\mathrm{C}]=\dfrac{1}{2}$. The reaction proceeds in the
$[\mathrm{R}=8.314 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{K} / \mathrm{mol}, \mathrm{e}=2.718]$
explanation:
${\rm{\Delta G}} = - {\rm{RT}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{ln}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{K}}_{\rm{c}}}$
$2494.2=8.314 \times 300 \ln _{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}=\mathrm{e}^{-1}=\dfrac{1}{2.718}=0.36$
$\mathrm{Q}=\dfrac{[\mathrm{B}][\mathrm{C}]}{[\mathrm{A}]^{2}}=\dfrac{2 \times \dfrac{1}{2}}{\left[\dfrac{1}{2}\right]^{2}}=4$
$\mathrm{Q}>\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}$, i.e., backward reaction is favoured.

question: The equilibrium concentrations of the species in the reaction $\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{D}$ are $2,3,10$ and $6 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$, respectively at $300 \mathrm{~K}$. $\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}$ for the reaction is $(\mathrm{R}=2 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{K})$
explanation:
$\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{D}$
Given
$[\mathrm{A}]=2 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$
$[\mathrm{B}]=3 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$
$[\mathrm{C}]=10 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$
$[\mathrm{D}]=6 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}, \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{[\mathrm{C}][\mathrm{D}]}{[\mathrm{A}][\mathrm{B}]}=\dfrac{10 \times 6}{2 \times 3}=10$
$\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=-2.303 \mathrm{RT} \log \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{eq}}$
$=-2.303 \times 2 \times 300 \times \log 10$
$\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=-1381.80 \mathrm{cal}$

$\mathrm {1 \quad 0}$
$\mathrm {1-\mathrm{x} \quad \mathrm{x}}$

question:
For the reaction,
${\text{A(g) + B(g) }} \rightleftharpoons {\text{ C(g) + D(g)}}$, $\Delta {\text{H}}^\circ $ and $\Delta {\text{S}}^\circ $ and $\Delta {\text{S}}^\circ $ are, respectively, $ - 29.8{\text{kJ}}{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}$ and $-0.100 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ at $298 \mathrm{~K}$. The equilibrium constant for the reaction at $298 \mathrm{~K}$ is:

$\mathrm {\Delta S^{\circ}=-1.00 \mathrm{kJK}^{-1}}$
From the equation
$\rm { \Delta G=\Delta H^{\circ}-T \Delta S^{\circ}=-29.8-(298 \times 0.100) \\ =-29.8+29.8=0}$

${\rm{0 = - 2}}{\rm{.303 }}$ ${\rm{RT}}$ ${\rm{log}}$ ${{\rm{K}}_{{\rm{eq}}}}$
$\mathrm {\therefore K_{e q}=1}$

question:
The following reaction is performed at $298 \mathrm{~K}$. ${\text{2NO(g) + }}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{2N}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}}$
The standard free energy of formation of${\text{ N}}{{\text{O}}_{{\text{(g)}}}}$ is $86.6 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$ at $298 \mathrm{~K}$. What is the standard free energy of formation of ${\text{N}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}}$ at ${\text{298}}\,{\text{K}}?$ $({{\text{K}}_{\text{P}}}{\text{ = 1}}.{\text{6}} \times {\text{1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{12}}}})$
explanation:
$\Delta {\text{G}}^\circ {\text{NO(g) = 86}}{\text{.6kJ/mol = 86600J/mol}}$
${\text{G}}^\circ {\text{N}}{{\text{O}}_2}({\text{g}}){\text{ = xJ/mol}}$
$\mathrm {T=298 K, K_{P}=1.6 \times 10^{12}}$
$\rm {\Delta G^{\circ}=-R T \ln K_{P}}$
Given equation,
$\rm { 2 \mathrm{NO}(g)+\mathrm{O}_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}(g) \\ \therefore 2 \Delta G^{\circ}{ }_{N O_{2}}-2 \Delta G^{\circ}{ }_{N O}=-R(298) \ln \left(1.6 \times 10^{12}\right) \\ 2 \Delta{G^{\circ}}^{\circ} O_{2}-2 \times 86600=-R(298) \ln \left(1.6 \times 10^{12}\right) \\ 2 \Delta G^{\circ}{ }_{N O_{2}}=2 \times 86600-R(298) \ln \left(1.6 \times 10^{12}\right) \\ \Delta G^{\circ}{ }_{N O_{2}}=\dfrac{1}{2}\left[2 \times 86600-R(298) \ln \left(1.6 \times 10^{12}\right)\right] \\ =0.5\left[2 \times 86600-R(298) \ln \left(1.6 \times 10^{12}\right)\right]}$

question:
If for the heterogeneous equilibrium
${\text{CaC}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}\left( {\text{s}} \right) \rightleftharpoons {\text{CaO}}\left( {\text{s}} \right){\text{ + C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right)$, ${\text{K = 1}}$ $\,at$ ${\text{1atm}}$ pressure, the temperature is given by
explanation:
Condition for equilibrium obtained from the relation,
$\mathrm{\Delta G^{\circ}}$=$\mathrm{-R T \ln K=-R T \ln 1=0}$
$\mathrm{\Delta H^{\circ}-T \Delta S^{\circ}=0}$
$\mathrm{T=\dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{\Delta S^{\circ}}}$

Exothermic dissolution processes are characterized by a decrease in solubility with increasing temperature.
explanation: Statement  A  is false because dissolution can be either endothermic or exothermic, depending on the salt. Statement B is true, as exothermic dissolution processes typically exhibit decreased solubility with an increase in temperature. Correct option is (3).

The pH of a solution does not influence the solubility of sparingly soluble salts.

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
A salt's $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}$ value is directly proportional to its molar solubility.
Salts that produce more ions upon dissociation have higher $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}$ values.
explanation: Statement  A  is false as ${{\text{K}}_{{\text{sp}}}}$ depends on the product of the concentrations of the ions to the power of their stoichiometry, not directly proportional to molar solubility. Statement B is true because salts producing more ions upon dissociation typically have higher $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}$ values. Correct option is (3).

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
The presence of a common ion decreases the solubility of a salt.
This effect is explained by Le Chatelier's principle, which states that if a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it will shift to counteract the disturbance.
explanation: Both statements are true. The common ion effect reduces the solubility of a salt because adding more of an ion that is a product of the dissociation shifts the equilibrium toward the reactants, thus decreasing solubility, in accordance with Le Chatelier's principle. Correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

question: Calculate the molar solubility of calcium hydroxide $\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$ in 0.10 M NaOH solution. The solubility product of calcium hydroxide is $5.5 \times 10^{-6}$.
explanation:
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}=\left[\mathrm{Ca}^{+2}\right]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]^{2}$
$5.5 \times 10^{-6}=\left[\mathrm{Ca}^{+2}\right](0.1)^{2}$
$\left[\mathrm{Ca}^{+2}\right]=5.5 \times 10^{-4}$

question: At 298 K the molar solubility of $\mathrm{Cd}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$ in 0.1 M KOH solution is $\mathrm{x} \times 10^{-\mathrm{y}}$. The values of  ${\text{x}}$  and  ${\text{y}}$  are respectively. (At $298 \mathrm{~K}, \mathrm{~K}_{\text {sp }}$ of $\left.\mathrm{Cd}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}=2.5 \times 10^{-14}\right)$
explanation:
$\mathrm{Cd}(\mathrm{OH})_{2} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Cd}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-}$
$\mathrm{KOH}=0.1 \mathrm{M}=\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]$
Let solubility of $\mathrm{Cd}^{2+}$ ions be S .
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}=\left[\mathrm{Cd}^{2+}\right]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]^{2}$
$2.5 \times 10^{-14}=\mathrm{S} \times[0.1]^{2}$
$2.5 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{M}=\mathrm{S}$ or $25 \times 10^{-13} \mathrm{M}=\mathrm{S}$
On comparing the answer with $\mathrm{x} \times 10^{-\mathrm{y}}$, $\mathrm{x}=25$ and $\mathrm{y}=13$.

question: What is the solubility product $\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}\right)$ of calcium phosphate in pure water? [ $\mathrm{S}=$ molar solubility]
explanation:
$\because$ Solubility product $\left( {{{\text{K}}_{{\text{sp}}}}} \right) = {{\text{x}}^{\text{x}}} \cdot {{\text{y}}^{\text{y}}} \cdot {{\text{S}}^{{\text{x}} + {\text{y}}}}$ where, x and y are number of $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{PO}_{4}^{3-}$ ions in 1 L of pure water respectively.
For $\ln \mathrm{Ca}_{3}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{2}, \mathrm{x}=3$ and $\mathrm{y}=2$
Therefore, $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}=3^{3} \cdot 2^{2} \mathrm{~S}^{2+3}=108 \mathrm{~S}^{5}$
So, option (1) is the correct answer.

question: The molar solubility of  Zirconium phosphate with molecular formula $\left(\mathrm{Zr}^{+4}\right)_{3}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}^{-3}\right)_{4}$ is given relation
explanation:
$\mathrm{Zr}_{3}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{4}(\mathrm{~s}) \rightleftharpoons 3 \mathrm{Zr}^{4+}(\mathrm{aq})+4 \mathrm{PO}_{4}^{3-}(\mathrm{aq})$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}=(3 \mathrm{~s})^{3}(4 \mathrm{~s})^{4}=(27 \times 256) \mathrm{s}^{7}$
${{\text{K}}_{{\text{sp}}}} = 6912\,{{\text{s}}^7}$
$\mathrm{s}=\left[\dfrac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}}{6912}\right]^{\frac{1}{7}}$

question: Solubility of calcium phosphate (molecular mass, ${\mathrm{\mathrm{\mathrm{M})}}}$ in water is Wg per 100 mL at ${\mathrm{\mathrm{25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}}}$. Its solubility product at ${\mathrm{\mathrm{25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}}}$ will be approximately
explanation:
${\rm{C}}{{\rm{a}}_{\rm{3}}}{\left( {{\rm{P}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}} \right)_{\rm{2}}} \to {\rm{3}}{\mathop {{\rm{Ca}}}\limits_{{\rm{3s}}} ^{{\rm{2 + }}}}{\rm{ + 2}}\mathop {{\rm{PO}}_{\rm{4}}^{{\rm{3 - }}}}\limits_{{\rm{2s}}} $
It is ${\mathrm{\mathrm{\mathrm{A}_{3} \mathrm{~B}_{2}}}}$ type salt.
${\rm{ So, }}{{\rm{K}}_{{\rm{sp }}}}{\rm{ = 108\,}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{5}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( {{\rm{s = }}\frac{{\rm{W}}}{{\rm{M}}}{\rm{ \times }}\frac{{{\rm{1000}}}}{{{\rm{100}}}}} \right)$
${\rm{ = 108}}{\left( {\frac{{\rm{W}}}{{\rm{M}}}{\rm{ \times 10}}} \right)^{\rm{5}}}$
$=108 \times 10^{5} \times\left(\dfrac{\mathrm{W}}{\mathrm{M}}\right)^{5}$
${\mathrm{\mathrm{K_{\text {sp }}=10^{7} \times\left(\dfrac{\mathrm{W}}{\mathrm{M}}\right)^{5}}}}$
So, the correct option is (3).

question: For a sparingly soluble salt ${\mathrm{\mathrm{\mathrm{AB}_{2}}}}$, the equilibrium concentrations of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{\mathrm{A}^{2+}}}}$ ions and ${\mathrm{\mathrm{\mathrm{B}^{-}}}}$ions are ${\mathrm{\mathrm{1.2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{M}}}}$ and ${\mathrm{\mathrm{0.24 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M}}}}$, respectively. The solubility product of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{\mathrm{AB}_{2}}}}$ is
explanation:
${\mathrm{\mathrm{\mathrm{AB}_{2} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{A}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{~B}^{-}}}}$
Solubility product ${\mathrm{\mathrm{\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}\right)=\left[\mathrm{A}^{2+}\right]\left[2 \mathrm{~B}^{-}\right]^{2}}}}$
$\begin{aligned}& =1.2 \times 10^{-4} \times\left(0.24 \times 10^{-3}\right)^{2} \\& \mathrm{~K}_{\text {sp }}=6.91 \times 10^{-12}\end{aligned}$
So, the correct option is (2).

explanation:
${\mathrm{K_{\text {sp }} \mathrm{MS}=\left[\mathrm{M}^{2+}\right]\left[\mathrm{S}^{2-}\right]}}$
${\mathrm{5 \times 10^{-21}=(0.05)\left[\mathrm{S}^{2-}\right]}}$
For precipitation ${\mathrm{Q_{\mathrm{sp}}=K_{\mathrm{sp}}}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore\left[\mathrm{S}^{2-}\right]=\dfrac{5 \times 10^{-21}}{5 \times 10^{-2}}=10^{-19}}}$
For ${\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}}}$ :
${\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{\oplus}+\mathrm{HS}^{\ominus}\left(K_{1}\right)}}$
${\mathrm{\mathrm{HS}^{\ominus} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{\oplus}+\mathrm{S}^{2-}\left(K_{2}\right)}}$
${\mathrm{K_{1} \times K_{2}=\dfrac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{\oplus}\right]^{2}\left[\mathrm{~S}^{2-}\right]}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}}}}$
${\mathrm{10^{-7} \times 10^{-14}=\dfrac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{\oplus}\right]^{2}\left[10^{-19}\right]}{0.1}}}$
${\mathrm{\left[\mathrm{H}^{\oplus}\right]^{2}=10^{-3}}}$,
${\mathrm{\Rightarrow}}$[Taking negative logarithm both sides]
${\mathrm{-2 \log \left[H^{\oplus}\right]=3}}$
${\mathrm{\mathrm{pH}=\dfrac{3}{2}=1.5}}$
So, the correct option is (3).

question: The solubility of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{PbCl}_{2}}}$ in water is ${\mathrm{\mathrm{S} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}{ }^{-1}}}$ and its solubility product is ${\mathrm{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}}}$. The relation between ${\mathrm{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}}}$ and S is represented as ${\mathrm{\mathrm{S}=\sqrt[3]{\dfrac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}}{x}}}}$. The value of ${\mathrm{x}}$ is ____ .
explanation:
$\mathop {{\text{PbC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}}\limits_{{\text{(S)}}} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {{\text{Pb}}_{}^{2 + }}\limits_{{\text{(S)}}} + \mathop {{\text{2Cl}}_{}^ - }\limits_{{\text{(S)}}} $
${\mathrm{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}=\left[\mathrm{Pb}^{2+}\right]\left[\mathrm{Cl}^{-}\right]^{2}}}$
${\mathrm{=S \times(2 S)^{2}=4 S^{3}}}$
${\mathrm{S=\sqrt[3]{\dfrac{K_{p}}{4}}}}$
$\therefore \,\,\,$Value of ${\mathrm{x=4}}$

question: The ${{\rm{K}}_{{\rm{sp}}}}$ of $\mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$ is $1 \times 10^{-12} .0 .01 \mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$ will precipitate at the limited $\mathrm{pH}$
explanation:
Given, $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}$ of $\mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}=1 \times 10^{-12}$Concentration of $\mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}=0.01 \mathrm{M}$$\mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{OH})_{2} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Mg}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-}$
$\begin{aligned}& \therefore \quad \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}=\left[\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}\right]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]^{2} \\& \text { or } \quad 1 \times 10^{-12}=0.01 \times\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]^{2} \\& \text { or } \quad\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]^{2}=1 \times 10^{-10} \\& \text { or } \quad\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=1 \times 10^{-5}\end{aligned}$
Again, from $\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=10^{-14}$
or$\begin{gathered}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[1 \times 10^{-5}\right]=10^{-14}} \\{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=\dfrac{10^{-14}}{10^{-5}}=10^{-9}}\end{gathered}$$\begin{aligned}& \because \quad \mathrm{pH}=-\log \left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right] \\& \mathrm{pH}=-\log 10^{-9}=9\end{aligned}$
$\therefore \mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$ will precipitate at limit $\mathrm{pH}$ of 9 .

question: How many grams of $\mathrm{CaC}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ will dissolve in distilled water to make one litre of saturated solution $\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}=2.5 \times 10^{-9}\right.$ and its molecular mass is 128 )
explanation:
$\mathrm{CaC}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ is AB type salt. So,
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}=\mathrm{S}^{2}$
$\mathrm{S}=\sqrt{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}}=\sqrt{2.5 \times 10^{-9}}$
$\mathrm{S}=\sqrt{25 \times 10^{-10}}=5 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~mol} /$ litre
$\mathrm{S}=5 \times 10^{-5} \times 128 \mathrm{~g} /$ litre
So, to make a saturated solution of 1 litre,$640 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~g}$ i.e., 0.0064 g is needed.

question: Match Column I with Column II and choose the correct combination from the options given
explanation:
(A) $\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\mathop {{\text{HCl}}}\limits_{0.1{\mkern 1mu} {\text{M}}} \to {\mathop {\text{H}}\limits_{0.1{\mkern 1mu} {\text{M}}} ^{\text{ + }}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} + {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{Cl}}$
${\text{AgCl}}({\text{s}}) \rightleftharpoons \mathop {{\text{A}}{{\text{g}}^ + }({\text{aq}})}\limits_{{\text{s}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{M}}} + \mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{l}}^ - }}\limits_{({\text{s}}{\mkern 1mu} + {\mkern 1mu} 0.1){\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{M}}} ({\text{aq}})$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}=\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{Cl}^{-}\right]$
$=\mathrm{s} \times(\mathrm{s}+0.1)$
$\mathrm{K}_{\text {sp }}=\mathrm{s} \times 0.1\,\,\,\,\,(\because 0.1>>\mathrm{s})$
$\mathrm{s}=\dfrac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}}{0.1}$
(B) Similarly for $\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}=\left[\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}\right]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]^{2}$
$\mathrm{s}=\dfrac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}}{(0.1)^{2}}$
(C) $\mathrm{Zr}_{3}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{4} \rightleftharpoons 3 \mathrm{Zr}^{4+}+4 \mathrm{PO}_{4}^{3-}$
Let solubility be s, then
$\mathrm{K}_{\text {sp }}=(3 \mathrm{~s})^{3}(4 \mathrm{~s})^{4}$
$\mathrm{s}=\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}} / 6912\right)^{1 / 7}$
(D) For $\mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{OH})_{3}$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}=\left[\mathrm{Al}^{3+}\right]\left[3 \mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=(\mathrm{s})(0.1)^{3}$
$\mathrm{s}=\dfrac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}}{(0.1)^{3}}$

question: The solubility product of a salt AB is $1 \times 10^{-8}$. In a solution in which concentration of  A  is $10^{-3} \mathrm{M}, \mathrm{AB}$ will precipitate when the concentration of B will be
explanation:
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}=[\mathrm{A}][\mathrm{B}]$
Precipitation occurs when $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}} < [\mathrm{A}][\mathrm{B}]$
Given, $[A]=10^{-3}$
$\Rightarrow 10^{-8} < 10^{-3}[\mathrm{~B}]$
$\Rightarrow 10^{-5} < [\mathrm{B}]$
So, if $[B]=10^{-4}$, then precipitation will occur.

question:

Reason: ${{\text{H}}_2}{\text{S}}$ is a weak acid.
explanation:
${{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{S}}$ is a weak acid and also a weak electrolyte, thus its ionisation is suppressed when small amount of strong electrolyte (like $\mathrm{HCl}$ ) is added due to common ion effect.
https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2023_10_03_56e84c1e517c487f569dg-3.jpg?height=185&width=373&top_left_y=1441&top_left_x=329

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question: The solubility product of sparingly soluble salt $\mathrm{AX}_{2}$ is $3.2 \times 10^{-11}$. Its solubility (in $\mathrm{mol} / \mathrm{L}$ ) is
explanation:
$\mathrm{AX}_{2}$ is $\mathrm{AB}_{2}$ type of salt
So, ${{\text{K}}_{{\text{SP}}}}{\text{ = 4}}{{\text{S}}^{\text{3}}}$
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{SP}}=3.2 \times 10^{-11}=32 \times 10^{-12} \\& \mathrm{~S}=\left(\dfrac{32 \times 10^{-12}}{4}\right)^{1 / 3}=2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{L}\end{aligned}$

question: The solubility product of a salt having formula $\mathrm{M}_{2} \mathrm{X}_{3}$ is $2.2 \times 10^{-20}$. If the solubility of another salt having formula $\mathrm{M}_{2} \mathrm{X}$ is twice the molar solubility of $\mathrm{M}_{2} \mathrm{X}_{3}$, the solubility product of $\mathrm{M}_{2} \mathrm{X}$ is
explanation:
$\mathrm{M}_{2} \mathrm{X}_{3} \rightleftharpoons \underset{2 \mathrm{~S}}{2 \mathrm{M}^{3+}}+\underset{3 \mathrm{~S}}{3 \mathrm{X}^{2-}}$
(where, $\mathrm{S}=$ solubility)
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}=(2 \mathrm{~S})^{2} \cdot(3 \mathrm{~S})^{3}=108 \mathrm{~S}^{5}$
Given $2.2 \times 10^{-20}=108 \mathrm{~S}^{5}$
$\therefore \mathrm{S}=4.59 \times 10^{-5}$ For $\mathrm{M}_{2} \mathrm{X}$,
${{\text{M}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{X}} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {{\text{2}}{{\text{M}}^{\text{ + }}}}\limits_{{\text{2S}}} {\text{ + X}}_{\text{S}}^{{\text{2 - }}}$
${{\text{K}}_{{\text{sp}}}} = {(2\,{\text{S}})^2} \cdot ({\text{S}}) = 4\,{{\text{S}}^3}$
$\mathrm{M}_{2} \mathrm{X}$ solubility is twice the molar solubility of $\mathrm{M}_{2} \mathrm{X}_{3}$. So, $\mathrm{S}=2 \times 4.59 \times 10^{-5}$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}=4\left(2 \times 4.59 \times 10^{-5}\right)^{3}=3 \times 10^{-12}$

question: On adding $0.1 \mathrm{M}$ solution each of $\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right],\left[\mathrm{Ba}^{2+}\right],\left[\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}\right]$ in a $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ solution, species first precipitated is $\left[\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}\right.$ of $\mathrm{BaSO}_{4}=10^{-11}, \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{sp}}$ of $\mathrm{CaSO}_{4}=10^{-6}, \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{sp}}$ of $\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}=10^{-5}$ ]
explanation:
Solubility of $\mathrm{BaSO}_{4}(\mathrm{x})=\sqrt{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}}=\sqrt{10^{-11}}$ $=3.6 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{L}$
Solubility of
$\mathrm{CaSO}_{4}(\mathrm{x})=\sqrt{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}}=\sqrt{10^{-6}}$
$=1.0 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{L}$
Solubility of $\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}=\sqrt[3]{\dfrac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}}{4}}$
$\left(\because\right.$ for $\left.\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}, 4 \mathrm{x}^{3}=\mathrm{K}_{\text {sp }}\right)$ $=\sqrt[3]{\dfrac{10^{-5}}{4}}=1.3 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{L}$
Least solubility is of $\mathrm{BaSO}_{4}$, hence it will precipitate first.

question:
Assertion: On mixing $500 \mathrm{~mL}$ of ${10^{ - 6}}{\text{M C}}{{\text{a}}^{2 + }}$ ion and $500 \mathrm{~mL}$ of ${10^{ - 6}}{\text{ M }}{{\text{F}}^ - }$ ion, a precipitate of $\mathrm{CaF}_{2}$ will be obtained. $\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}\right.$ of $\mathrm{CaF}_{2}=10^{-18}$ ).

Reason: If $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}$ is greater than ionic product, a precipitate will develop.
explanation:
$\mathrm{CaF}_{2} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ca}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{~F}^{-}$
$\mathrm{K}=\left(10^{-6}\right)\left(\dfrac{10^{-6}}{2}\right)^{2}=2.5 \times 10^{-19}$ which is less than $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}\left(10^{-18}\right)$.
Hence, no precipitate will develop. Precipitate will develop only when ionic product is more than solubility product.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

parent_topic: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts

question:
Assertion: ${\text{NaCl}}$ is precipitated when $\mathrm{HCl}$ gas is passed in a saturated solution of $\mathrm{NaCl}$.

Reason: $\mathrm{HCl}$ is a strong acid.
explanation:
Pure ${\text{NaCl}}$ is precipitated when $\mathrm{HCl}$ gas is passed in a saturated solution of $\mathrm{NaCl}$.
This is due to the common ion effect. The concentration of $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$increases in the solution, due to which the reaction of dissociation of $\mathrm{NaCl}$ moves in the backward direction leading to precipitation of $\mathrm{NaCl}$.
$\mathrm{HCl}$ is strong acid in aq.solution but the strength of it has no relation with assertion.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

subtopic: Common Ion Effect on Solubility of Ionic Salts

question:
Match the column I with column II and mark the appropriate choice.
A. ${\rm{Fe}}{\left( {{\rm{OH}}} \right)_{\rm{3}}}$
B.${\rm{A}}{{\rm{g}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{Cr}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}$
C.${\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{COOAg}}$
D.${\rm{C}}{{\rm{a}}_{\rm{3}}}{\left( {{\rm{P}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}} \right)_{\rm{2}}}$
P ${{\text{K}}_{{\text{sp}}}}{\text{ = }}{{\text{S}}^{\text{2}}}$
Q ${{\text{K}}_{{\text{sp}}}} = 27\,{{\text{S}}^4}$
R ${{\text{K}}_{{\text{sp}}}}{\text{ = 108}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\text{S}}^{\text{5}}}$
S $\,{{\text{K}}_{{\text{sp}}}}{\text{ = 4}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\text{S}}^{\text{3}}}$
explanation:
(A) ${\text{Fe}}{({\text{OH}})_{\text{3}}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{F}}{{\text{e}}^{{\text{3 + }}}}{\text{ + 3O}}{{\text{H}}^{\text{ - }}};{{\text{K}}_{{\text{sp}}}}{\text{ = 27}}\,{{\text{S}}^{\text{4}}}$
(B) ${\text{A}}{{\text{g}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{Cr}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{2A}}{{\text{g}}^{\text{ + }}}{\text{ + CrO}}_{\text{4}}^{{\text{2 - }}};{{\text{K}}_{{\text{sp }}}}{\text{ = 4}}{{\text{S}}^{\text{3}}}$
(C) ${\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{COOAg}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{CO}}{{\text{O}}^{\text{ - }}}{\text{ + A}}{{\text{g}}^{\text{ + }}};{{\text{K}}_{{\text{sp}}}}{\text{ = }}{{\text{S}}^{\text{2}}}$
(D) ${\text{C}}{{\text{a}}_{\text{3}}}{\left( {{\text{P}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}} \right)_{\text{2}}} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {{\text{3C}}{{\text{a}}^{{\text{2 + }}}}}\limits_{\left( {{\text{3}}\,{{\text{s}}^{\text{2}}}} \right)} {\text{ + }}\mathop {{\text{2PO}}_{\text{4}}^{{\text{3 - }}}}\limits_{\left( {{\text{2}}\,{{\text{s}}^{\text{2}}}} \right)} ;{{\text{K}}_{{\text{sp}}}}{\text{ = 108}}\,{{\text{S}}^{\text{5}}}$
So, the option (2) is correct.

options.text: ${\text{A - R,    B - Q,    C - S,    D - P}}$
${\text{A - Q,    B - S,    C - P,    D - R}}$
${\text{A - P,    B - R,    C - Q,    D - S}}$
${\text{A - S,    B - P,    C - R,    D - Q}}$

question:
One $\mathrm{dm}^{3}$ solution containing $10^{-5}$ moles each of $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ions and $\mathrm{CrO}_{4}^{2-}$ ions is treated with $10^{-4}$ moles of silver nitrate. Which one of the following observations is made?
$\begin{aligned}& {\left[\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}} \mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}=4 \times 10^{-12}\right],}
{\left[\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}} \mathrm{AgCl}=1 \times 10^{-10}\right]}\end{aligned}$
explanation:
Ionic product of $\mathrm {\mathrm{AgCl}=\left[10^{-4}\right]\left[10^{-5}\right]}$
$\rm {=10^{-9}}$
Ionic product of $\mathrm {\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}=\left[10^{-8}\right]\left[10^{-5}\right]=10^{-13}}$
Ionic product of $\mathrm {\mathrm{AgCl}>\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}}$ of $\mathrm {\mathrm{AgCl}}$
So, precipitation will take place.
Ionic product of $\mathrm {\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4} < \mathrm{K}_{\text {sp }}}$ of $\mathrm {\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}}$ So, precipitation will not take place.

question: Solubility product of a salt ${\text{AB}}$  is ${\text{1}} \times {\text{1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 8}}}}{\text{ }}{{\text{M}}^{\text{2}}}$ in a solution in which the concentration of $\mathrm{A}^{+}$ions is ${10^{ - 3}}{\text{ M}}$. The salt will precipitate when the concentration of $\mathrm{B}^{-}$ ions is kept
explanation:
$\mathrm {\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}=1 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{M}^{2}}$
$\rm {\begin{gathered}{\left[\mathrm{A}^{+}\right]=10^{-3} \mathrm{M}} \\\therefore\left[\mathrm{B}^{-}\right]=\dfrac{1 \times 10^{-8}}{10^{-3}} \mathrm{M}=10^{-5} \mathrm{M}\end{gathered}}$
$\mathrm {\mathrm{AB}}$ will be precipitated if ionic product will be greater than solubility product. That means when the concentration of $\mathrm {\mathrm{B}^{-}}$ions will be more than $\mathrm {10^{-5} \mathrm{M}}$, then $\mathrm {\mathrm{AB}}$ will be precipitated.

question: $\mathrm{M}(\mathrm{OH})_{\mathrm{x}}$ has $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}=4 \times 10^{-12}$ and solubility $10^{-4} \mathrm{M}$. Hence, $\mathrm{x}$ is
explanation:
${\text{M(OH}}{{\text{)}}_{\text{x}}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{M}}_{\text{s}}^{{{\text{x}}^{\text{ + }}}}{\text{ + x}}\mathop {{\text{OH}}}\limits_{{\text{xS}}} $

question: Given the solubility product of ${{\text{A}}_{\text{3}}}{{\text{B}}_{\text{2}}}$  is $2 \times 10^{-30}$. What will will be the solubility in moles/litre?
explanation:
$\mathrm {\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}}$ of $\mathrm {\mathrm{A}_{3} \mathrm{~B}_{2}=2 \times 10^{-30}}$
${{\text{A}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{}}{{\text{B}}_{\text{2}}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{3}}{{\text{A}}^{{\text{2 + }}}}{\text{ + 2}}{{\text{B}}^{{\text{3 - }}}}$
Assuming ' $\mathrm {\mathrm{s}}$ ' is the solubility of ${{\text{A}}_{\text{3}}}{{\text{B}}_{\text{2}}}$ then
${{\text{K}}_{{\text{sp}}}}{\text{ = (3s}}{{\text{)}}^{\text{3}}}{{\text{(2s)}}^{\text{2}}}{\text{ = 108}}{{\text{s}}^{\text{5}}}$
$\mathrm {\mathrm{s}=5 \sqrt{\dfrac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}}{108}} \quad \therefore \mathrm{s}=\left(\dfrac{2 \times 10^{-30}}{108}\right)^{1 / 5}}$

question: The solubility product of chalk is $9.3 \times 10^{-8}$. Calculate its solubility in gram per litre
explanation:
$\mathrm {\mathrm{CaCO}_{3} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ca}^{2+}+\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}}$
Let the solubility of $\mathrm {\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}}$ be ' $\mathrm {\mathrm{s}}$ ' mole per litre
$\mathrm {\therefore \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}=\left[\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}\right]\left[\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}\right]=\mathrm{s} . \mathrm{s}}$
$\mathrm {\therefore \mathrm{s}=\sqrt{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}}=\sqrt{9.3 \times 10^{-8}}=0.000304 \mathrm{~mole} / \mathrm{litre}}$
Solubility in $\mathrm {\mathrm{g} / \mathrm{l}=}$ mole/litre $\mathrm {\times}$ Molecular wt.
${\text{ = 0}}.{\text{000304}} \times {\text{100 = 0}}{\text{.0304 gram/litre}}$

question: Find out the solubility of $\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$ in ${\text{0}}{\text{.1}}\,\,{\text{M}}$ ${\text{NaOH}}$. Given that the ionic product of $\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$ is $2 \times 10^{-15}$
explanation:
$\mathrm {\alpha=1}$ for $\mathrm {\mathrm{NaOH}}$
$\mathrm {N a O H(a q) \rightarrow_{0.1 M}^{N a^{+2}}(a q)+_{0.1 M}^{O H^{-}(a q)}}$
$\mathrm {\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}(s) \rightleftharpoons_{S^{\prime}}^{N^{+2}}(a q)+_{0.1+2 S^{\prime}}^{2 O H^{-}}(a q)}$
Ionic product $\mathrm {=\left(S^{\prime}\right)\left(0.1+2 S^{\prime}\right)^{2}}$
$\mathrm {2 \times 10^{-15}=S^{\prime}(0.1)^{2}}$
${{\text{S}}^\prime }{\text{ = 2}} \times {\text{1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 13}}}}{\text{ M}}$

question: The solubility of $\mathrm{BaSO}_{4}$ in water is $2.42 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{gL}^{-1}$ at $298 \mathrm{~K}$. The value of its solubility product $\left( {{{\text{K}}_{{\text{sp}}}}} \right)$ will be
explanation:
Solubility in mol/
$\mathrm {\mathrm{L}=\dfrac{2.42 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{L}}{233(\text { mol.wt })} \therefore S=1.04 \times 10^{-5}}$
$\mathrm {\mathrm{BaSO}_{4} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ba}_{(a q)}^{2+}+\mathrm{SO}_{4(a q)}^{2-}}$,
$\mathrm {K_{s p}=\left[\mathrm{Ba}^{2+}\right]\left[\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}\right]=S \times S}$
$\mathrm {\therefore K_{s p}=S^{2}=\left(1.04 \times 10^{-5}\right)^{2}=1.08 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~mol}^{2} \mathrm{~L}^{-2}}$

${\text{MY}}{\mkern 1mu} {{\text{K}}_{{\text{SP}}}}{\text{ = S}}_{\text{1}}^{\text{2}}{\text{ = 6}}{\text{.2}} \times {\text{1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 13}}}}{\text{ = 62}} \times {\text{1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 14}}}}$

Solubility of $\mathrm {\mathrm{NY}_{3}>}$ solubility of ${\text{MY}}$ so $\rm {{{\text{3}}^{{\text{rd}}}}}$ statement is true.

question: How many grams of $\mathrm{CaC}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ will dissolve in $1 \mathrm{~L}$ of saturated solution?  ${{\rm{K}}_{{\rm{SP}}}}$  of $\mathrm{CaC}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ is ${\text{2}}{\text{.5}} \times {\text{1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 9}}}}{\text{ }}{{\text{M}}^{\text{2}}}$ and its molecular weight is ${\text{128}}$.
explanation:
$\mathrm {\mathrm{CaC}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ca}_{s}^{2+}+\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}^{2-}}$
$\rm {{{\rm{K}}_{{\rm{SP}}}}{\rm{ = S \times S = }}{{\rm{S}}^{\rm{2}}}}$
$\rm {{\rm{S = }}\sqrt {{{\rm{K}}_{{\rm{SP}}}}} {\rm{ = }}{\left( {{\rm{2}}{\rm{.5 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{ - 9}}}}} \right)^{{\rm{1/2}}}}}$
$\mathrm {=5 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}}$
$\mathrm {\therefore \dfrac{w}{128}=5 \times 10^{-5}}$

question: The solubility product constant of $\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{AgBr}$ are $1.1 \times 10^{-12}$ and $5.0 \times 10^{-13}$ respectively. Calculate the ratio of the solubilities of their saturated solutions.
explanation:
${\text{A}}{{\text{g}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{Cr}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{2A}}{{\text{g}}^{\text{ + }}}{\text{ + CrO}}_{\text{4}}^{{\text{2 - }}}{\text{;}}{{\text{K}}_{{\text{sp}}}}{\text{ = 1}}{\text{.1 1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 12}}}}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\text{S}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\text{2S}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\text{S}}$
(solubility of $\mathrm {\mathrm{AgBr}}$ is $\mathrm {\mathrm{S} \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{L}^{-1}}$ )
$\mathrm {\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}=\left[A g^{+}\right]\left[B r^{-}\right]=S . S=S^{2}}$
or $\mathrm {S=\sqrt{K_{s p}}=\sqrt{5.0 \times 10^{-13}}=\sqrt{50 \times 10^{-14}}}$
$\mathrm {S=7.07 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{M}}$
Ratio of their solubilities
$\mathrm {\dfrac{S\left(\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}\right)}{S(\mathrm{AgBr})}=\dfrac{6.50 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{M}}{7.07 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{M}}=91.93 \approx 92}$
$\mathrm {\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}}$ is 92 times more soluble than $\mathrm {\mathrm{AgBr}}$.

question:
Solid $\mathrm{Ba}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}$ is gradually disolved in a ${\text{1}}.{\text{0}} \times {\text{1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 4}}}}{\text{ M}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{N}}{{\text{a}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}$ solution. At what concentrations of $\mathrm{Ba}^{2+}$, will a precipitate begin to form?
$\left( {{{\text{K}}_{{\text{sp}}}}{\text{ of BaC}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{ = 5}}{\text{.1}} \times {\text{1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 9}}}}} \right)$
explanation:
$\mathrm {\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{Na}^{+}+\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}}$
$\left[ {{\text{N}}{{\text{a}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}} \right]{\text{ = }}\left[ {{\text{CO}}_{\text{3}}^{{\text{2 - }}}} \right]{\text{ = 1}} \times {\text{1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 4}}}}{\text{ M}}$
${\text{BaC}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{B}}{{\text{a}}^{{\text{2 + }}}}{\text{ + CO}}_{\text{3}}^{{\text{2 - }}}$
${{\text{K}}_{{\text{sp}}\left( {{\text{BaC}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}} \right)}}{\text{ = }}\left[ {{\text{B}}{{\text{a}}^{{\text{2 + }}}}} \right]\left[ {{\text{CO}}_{\text{3}}^{{\text{2 - }}}} \right]$
$\left[ {{\text{B}}{{\text{a}}^{{\text{2 + }}}}} \right]{\text{ = }}\frac{{{{\text{K}}_{{\text{sp}}}}\left( {{\text{BaC}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}} \right)}}{{\left[ {{\text{CO}}_{\text{3}}^{{\text{2 - }}}} \right]}}{\text{ }}$
$\left[ {{\text{B}}{{\text{a}}^{{\text{2 + }}}}} \right] = \frac{{5.1 \times {{10}^{ - 9}}}}{{1 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}}\left[ {{\text{B}}{{\text{a}}^{2 + }}} \right] = 5.1 \times {10^{ - 5}}{\text{ M}}$
Hence, at $\mathrm {5.1 \times 10^{-5}}$ concentration of $\mathrm {\mathrm{Ba}^{2+} \mathrm{a}}$ precipitate will begin to form.

for calculation of $\mathrm {\left[B a^{+2}\right]}$ in original solution (450ml)

$\mathrm {\left[M_{1}=\right.}$ molarity of $\mathrm {\mathrm{Ba}^{+2}}$ in original solution (450 $\mathrm {\mathrm{ml})}$ ]

$\mathrm {\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]^{2}=\dfrac{K_{s p}}{\left[\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}\right]}}$
${\text{ = }}\frac{{{\text{9}} \times {\text{1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 12}}}}}}{{{\text{0}}{\text{.01}}}}$
${\text{ = 9}} \times {\text{1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 10}}}}{\text{ }}{{\text{M}}^{\text{2}}}$
$\therefore {\text{ }}{\left[ {{\text{OH}}} \right]^{{\text{ - }}}}{\text{ = }}\sqrt {{\text{9}} \times {\text{1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 10}}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\text{M}}^{\text{2}}}} $
${\text{ = 3}}\, \times {\text{1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 5}}}}\,{\text{M}}$

question: What is the minimum volume of water required to dissolve $1 \mathrm{~g}$ of calcium sulphate at $298 \mathrm{~K}$ ? (For calcium sulphate, ${{\rm{K}}_{{\rm{sp}}}}$ is $9.1 \times 10^{-6}$ )?
explanation:
$\begin{gathered} {\text{CaS}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{C}}{{\text{a}}^{{\text{2 + }}}}{\text{ + SO}}_{\text{4}}^{{\text{2 - }}}{\text{; }}{{\text{K}}_{{\text{SP}}}}{\text{ = 9}}{\text{.1}} \times {\text{1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 6}}}} \hfill \\ {\text{S}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\text{S}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\text{S}} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} $
where $\mathrm {\mathrm{S}}$ is the solubility of $\mathrm {\mathrm{CaSO}_{4}}$.
$\mathrm {K_{s p}=\left[\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}\right]\left[\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}\right]=S . S=S^{2}}$
$\mathrm {S=\sqrt{K_{s p}}=\sqrt{9.1 \times 10^{-6}}}$
$\mathrm {S=3.017 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M}}$
$\mathrm {\mathrm{CaSO}_{4}}$ solubility $\mathrm {=3.017 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}}$
The solubility in $\mathrm {g / L=3 \times 10^{-3} \times 136}$
$\mathrm {=0.410 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{L}}$
Hence, $\mathrm {0.41 \mathrm{~g}}$ dissolve in $\mathrm {1 \mathrm{~L}}$
$\mathrm {1 \mathrm{~g}}$ will dissolve in $\mathrm {\dfrac{1}{0.41}=2.43 \mathrm{~L}}$.
subject: Chemistry

question: The values of $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}$  of two sparingly soluble salts $\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$ and $\mathrm{AgCN}$ are $2.0 \times 10^{-15}$ and $6 \times 0^{-17}$ respectively. Which salt is more soluble?
explanation:
$\mathrm {\mathrm{AgCN} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ag}^{+}+\mathrm{CN}^{-}}$
$\rm {\mathrm{K}_{S P}=\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{CN}^{-}\right]=6 \times 10^{-17} \\ \operatorname{Let}\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]=\mathrm{S}_{1} \text {, then }\left[\mathrm{CN}^{-}\right]=\mathrm{S}_{1}}$
$\mathrm {S_{1}^{2}=6 \times 10^{-17}, S_{1}=7.8 \times 10^{-9}}$
$\mathrm {\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{OH})_{2} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ni}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{OH}}$
$\mathrm {K_{S P}=\left[\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}\right]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]^{2}=2 \times 10^{-15}}$
${\text{Let}}\left[ {{\text{N}}{{\text{i}}^{{\text{2 + }}}}} \right]{\text{ = }}{{\text{S}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{,then }}\left[ {{\text{O}}{{\text{H}}^{{\text{ - }}}}} \right]{\text{ = 2}}{{\text{S}}_{\text{2}}}$
$\mathrm {\left(S_{2}\right)\left(2 S_{2}\right)^{2}=2 \times 10^{-15}, S_{2}=0.58 \times 10^{-4}}$
$\mathrm {\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}}$ is more soluble than $\mathrm {\mathrm{AgCN}}$.

${\text{For }}{{\text{A}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{B,}}{{\text{K}}_{{\text{SP}}}}{\text{ = }}\left( {{{\text{2}}^{\text{2}}}} \right)\left( {{{\text{1}}^{\text{1}}}} \right){{\text{S}}^{{\text{2 + 1}}}}{\text{ = 4}}{{\text{S}}^{\text{3}}}$

question: The $\mathrm{pH}$ of pure water at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $35^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ are 7 and 6 respectively. The heat of formation ${\rm{(in \,kJ}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{ - 1}})$ of water from $\mathrm{H}^{+}$and $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$is _____.
explanation:
At $25^\circ {\text{C}}\,;\,[{{\text{H}}^ + }] = {10^{ - 7}}$
$\therefore \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{w}}=10^{-14}$
At $35^\circ {\text{C}}\,;\,[{{\text{H}}^ + }] = {10^{ - 6}}$
$\therefore \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{w}}=10^{-12}$
Now using
$2.303\,{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{log}}\frac{{{{\text{K}}_{{{\text{w}}_{\text{2}}}}}}}{{\,{{\text{K}}_{{{\text{w}}_{\text{1}}}}}}} = \frac{{\Delta {\text{H}}}}{{\text{R}}}\left[ {\frac{{{{\text{T}}_{\text{2}}} - {{\text{T}}_{\text{1}}}}}{{\,{{\text{T}}_{\text{1}}} \times {{\text{T}}_{\text{2}}}}}} \right]$
$2.303\,\,{\text{log}}\frac{{{{10}^{ - 12}}}}{{{{10}^{ - 14}}}} = \frac{{\Delta {\text{H}}}}{2}\left[ {\frac{{10}}{{298 \times 308}}} \right]$
$\therefore \Delta {\text{H}} = 84551.4\,\,{\text{cal}}/{\text{mol}}$
$ = 84.551\,\,{\text{kcal}}/{\text{mol}}$
Thus, $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{OH}^{-}$
$\Delta {\text{H}} = - 84.551\,\,{\text{kcal}}/{\text{mol}}$

question:
Assertion: The degree of ionization of water is small at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, only about one of every $10^{7}$ molecules in pure water is ionised at any instant.


Reason: In pure water at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the molar concentration of water is essentially constant.
explanation: At $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, autoionization in pure water is a dynamic equilibrium with a low degree of ionization. The strong hydrogen-oxygen bond limits dissociation, resulting in approximately only one in every $10^{7}$ water molecules forming ions, as indicated by the $\mathrm{Kw}$ value. So option (2) is correct.

question: Ionic product of water at $310 \mathrm{~K}$ is $2.7 \times 10^{-14}$. What is the $\mathrm{pH}$ of neutral water at this temperature?
explanation:
$\mathrm {\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{w}}=\left[\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}\right] \cdot\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=2.7 \times 10^{-14}}$ at $\mathrm {310 \mathrm{~K}}$
$\mathrm {\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons\left[\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]}$
$\mathrm {\left[\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}\right]=\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]}$
Therefore,
$\rm { {\left[\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}\right]=\sqrt{2.7 \times 10^{-14}}=1.643 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{M}} \\ \mathrm{pH}=-\log \left[\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}\right]=-\log 1.643 \times 10^{-7} \\ \mathrm{pH}=7+(-0.2156)=6.7844 .}$

question: Which among the following has highest pH ?
explanation: Strong bases have higher pH than that of acids. So, NaOH has more pH than $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ and HCl. More is the concentration of NaOH more is the pH of the solution. Hence, 1 M NaOH has highest pH value.

question: At $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 100 \mathrm{~mL}$ of 0.5 M HCl is mixed with 100 mL of 0.4 M NaOH solution. To this resultant solution, 800 mL of distilled water is added. What is the pH of final solution?
explanation:
$\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]_{\text {mix }}=\dfrac{(\mathrm{MV})_{\mathrm{HCl}}-(\mathrm{MV})_{\mathrm{NaOH}}}{\mathrm{V}_{\text {Total }}}$
$=\dfrac{(0.5 \times 100)-(0.4 \times 100)}{1000}=0.01 \mathrm{M}$.
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{pH}=-\log \left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=-\log \left[10^{-2}\right]=2$.

explanation: The value of  ${{\text{K}}_{\text{w}}}$  increases with rise in temperature because dissociation of water is endothermic process. question: What is the pH of a $10^{-8}$ solution of HCl ?
explanation:
Total $\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]$ obtained from $\mathrm{HCl}+$ [ $\mathrm{H}^{+}$] obtained by $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
$\because \mathrm{HCl}$ is strong acid.
$\therefore\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=1.0 \times 10^{-8}$ from HCl
The concentration of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$from
water $=10^{-7}$ or $0.1 \times 10^{-18}$
Total $\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=1.1 \times 10^{-7}$
$\because \mathrm{pH}=-\log \left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=-\log \left[1.1 \times 10^{-7}\right]$
$\mathrm{pH}=6.9$
Hence, option (4) is the correct answer.

question: pH of 0.05 M aqueous solution of diethyl amine is 10 . The value of equilibrium constant is
explanation:
For weak bases, $\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=\mathrm{C} \alpha$ & ${{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}} = {\text{C}}{\alpha ^2}$
$\therefore \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}=\dfrac{\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]^{2}}{\mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{\left(10^{-4}\right)^{2}}{0.05}=2 \times 10^{-7}$
( $\mathrm{pH}=10$ so $\mathrm{pOH}=4$ and $\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right] 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mol} /$ lit $)$

question: The concentration in   ${\text{M}}$  of   $\left[ {{\text{O}}{{\text{H}}^ - }} \right]$    in  $0.001{\text{M}}\,\,{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_4}$  is
explanation:
$\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=0.001 \mathrm{M} \times 2=0.002 \mathrm{~N}=2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~N}$
$\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=10^{-14}$
$\therefore\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=\dfrac{10^{-14}}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]}=\dfrac{10^{-14}}{2 \times 10^{-3}}=5 \times 10^{-12}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between pH and pOH and Calculations Based on pH and pOH

question: What is the pH of acetic acid at equilibrium, given that acetic acid concentration is 0.1 M and it is $30 \%$ dissociated at equilibrium? ( $\log 3=0.47$ )
explanation:
For weak acids, $\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=\mathrm{C} \alpha$
$=0.1 \times \dfrac{30}{100}=0.03=3 \times 10^{-2}$
$\mathrm{pH}=-\log \left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=-\log \left(3 \times 10^{-2}\right)$
$=2-\log 3=2-0.47=1.53$.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: pH of Weak Acid and Weak Base

question: The pH of water is 7 at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ if water is heated to $80^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, its pH will?
explanation: When water is heated, degree of ionisation increases, $\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]$  ion concentration increases and pH decreases.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Salient Features of pH Concept

question: A solution having ${\mathrm{\mathrm{pOH}=4}}$ is diluted 1000 times. The pH of the resulting solution will be ____ .
explanation:
${\mathrm{\mathrm{pOH}=4 \quad \therefore[\mathrm{OH}]=10^{-4} \mathrm{M}}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]}}$of diluted solution ${\mathrm{=10^{-4} / 1000}}$
$=10^{-7} \mathrm{M}$
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad \mathrm{pOH}}}$ of diluted solution ${\mathrm{=7}}$
${\mathrm{\mathrm{pH}+\mathrm{pOH}=14}}$,
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad \mathrm{pH}=14-7=7}}$
subject: Chemistry

The pH of 0.01 M HCOOH will be ____ .

${\mathrm{\left.\log _{10} 1.18=0.0719\right]}}$

question:
What is the pH of the resulting solution when equal volumes of 0.1 M NaOH and 0.01 M HCl are mixed?
[Given: ${\mathrm{\log _{10}(45)=1.6532}}$ ]
explanation:
Assuming that 1000 mL of 0.1 M NaOH is mixed with 1000 mL of 0.01 M HCl .
$\mathop {{\text{NaOH}}}\limits_{{\text{0}}{\text{.1}}\,{\text{mol}}} {\text{ + }}\mathop {{\text{HCl}}}\limits_{{\text{0}}{\text{.01}}\,{\text{mol}}} \to \mathop {{\text{NaCl}}}\limits_{{\text{0}}{\text{.01}}\,{\text{mol}}} {\text{ + }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}$
Here, NaOH is the excess reagent. Hence, the pH of the solution will be greater than 7.0 .
Excess amount of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{NaOH}=0.09 \mathrm{~mol}}}$
The volume of the resulting solution
${\mathrm{\approx 2000 \mathrm{~mL} \approx 2 \mathrm{~L}}}$
${\mathrm{\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=\dfrac{0.09}{2}=0.045 \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{L}}}$
${\text{pOH }} = - {\log _{10}}\left[ {{\text{O}}{{\text{H}}^ - }} \right]$
$ = - {\log _{10}}\left( {45 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}} \right)$
$ = - [1.6532 - 3] = 1.3468$
${\text{pH }} + {\text{pOH}} = 14$
$\therefore \quad {\text{pH }} = 14 - {\text{pOH}} = 14 - 1.3468$
$ = 12.6532 \approx 12.65$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between pH and pOH and Calculations Based on pH and pOH

question: One litre of an aqueous solution has 3.65 g of HCl . If it is desired to increase the pH of the solution to 2 then $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}$ion concentration present initially should be
explanation:
Molar mass of $\mathrm{HCl}=1+35.5=36.5$
${\text{Molarity}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\text{of}}\,{\text{HCl}}\,{\text{ = }}\frac{{{\text{3}}.{\text{65}}}}{{{\text{36}}.{\text{5}} \times {\text{1}}}}{\text{ = }}\,{\text{0}}.{\text{1M}}$
${\text{pH}}\,{\text{ = }} - {\text{log}}\left[ {{{\text{H}}^{\text{ + }}}} \right]{\text{ = }} - {\text{log[0}}.{\text{1}}]{\text{ = 1}}$
${\text{pH}}\,{\text{ = }}\,{\text{2}}$
$ - {\text{log}}\left[ {{{\text{H}}^{\text{ + }}}} \right] = 2 \Rightarrow \left[ {{{\text{H}}^{\text{ + }}}} \right] = {10^{ - 2}}{\text{M}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: What is $\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]$ of a solution having ${\rm{0}}{\rm{.1}}\,{\rm{M}}\,{\rm{HCN}}$ and ${\rm{0}}{\rm{.2}}\,{\rm{M}}\,{\rm{NaCN?}}$ ${\rm{(}}{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{a}}}\,{\rm{for}}\,\left. {{\rm{HCN}} = {\rm{6}}{\rm{.2 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{ - 10}}}}} \right)$

question: A solution has $\mathrm{pH}=5$, it is diluted 100 times, then it will become
explanation:
$\mathrm{pH}=5$
$\left[ {{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}{{\rm{O}}^{\rm{ + }}}} \right] = {10^{ - {\rm{pH}}}} = {10^{ - 5}}{\rm{M}}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{M}_{1}=10^{-5}$
Let $\mathrm{V}_{1}=\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{V}_{2}=100 \mathrm{x}$
According to dilution law
$\mathrm{M}_{1} \mathrm{~V}=\mathrm{M}_{2} \mathrm{~V}_{2}$
$\begin{array}{l} {10^{ - 5}}{\rm{x}} = {{\rm{M}}_2} \times 100{\rm{x}}\,\\ \,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} \frac{{{{10}^{ - 5}}{\rm{x}}}}{{100{\rm{x}}}} = {{\rm{M}}_2}\\ \Rightarrow \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} {{\rm{M}}_2} = {10^{ - 7}}\\ \left[ {{{\rm{H}}_3}{{\rm{O}}^ + }} \right] = {10^{ - 7}} + {10^{ - 7}}{\rm{E}}\\ \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{(from}}\,{\rm{water)}}\\ = 2 \times {10^{ - 7}}{\rm{M}}\\ {\rm{pH}} = - {\rm{log}}\left[ {{{\rm{H}}_3}{{\rm{O}}^ + }} \right]\\ = - {\rm{log}}\left( {2 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}} \right) = 6.69 \end{array}$
Hence, the solution becomes almost neutral in nature.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between pH and pOH and Calculations Based on pH and pOH

question: Which of the following has highest $\mathrm{pH}$ ?
explanation:
$\mathrm{pH}$ of solution $\propto\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]$
$\dfrac{\mathrm{M}}{4} \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$ solution will give highest concentration of ${\text{O}}{{\text{H}}^ - }$
Hence, it has highest $\mathrm{pH}$.

question: pH of  ${\rm{1}}\,{\rm{L}}\,{\rm{0}}{\rm{.1M}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{HClwhen}}\,{\rm{5}}\,{\rm{g}}\,{\rm{of}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{HF}}$  is added
explanation:
$\mathrm{pH}$ of mixture of strong and weak acid depends only upon strong acid concentration.
${\text{0}}.{\text{1M}}\,{\text{HCl = 0}}.{\text{1N}}\,{\text{HCl}}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} \left[ {{{\rm{H}}^{\rm{ + }}}} \right] = 0.1\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} $
${\rm{pH}} = - {\rm{log}}\left[ {{{\rm{H}}^ + }} \right] = {\rm{log}}\frac{1}{{\left[ {{{\rm{H}}^{\rm{ + }}}} \right]}}$
$\therefore {\rm{pH}} = {\rm{log}}\frac{1}{{[0.1]}} = {\rm{log}}10 = 1$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: pH of Mixture of Acids and/or Bases

question: Determine the $\mathrm{pH}$ of the solution that results from the addition of $20.00 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $0.01 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$ to $30.00 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $0.01 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{HCl}$.
explanation:
Millimoles of $\mathrm{H}^{+}=30 \times 0.01=0.3$
Millimole of $\mathrm{OH}^{-}=20 \times 0.01 \times 2=0.4$
Remaining millimoles of $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$
$\begin{aligned}= & 0.4-0.3=0.1 \\\Rightarrow\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right] & =\dfrac{0.1}{50} \text { or } 2 \times 10^{-3}\end{aligned}$
So, $\mathrm{pOH}=2.7$
$\mathrm{pH}=14-2.7=11.30$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: pH of Mixture of Acids and/or Bases

question: What is the $\mathrm{pH}$ value of $10^{-2}$ molar $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ solution?}
explanation: $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ completely ionises as,$\mathrm{HNO}_{3}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}+\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}$$\begin{aligned}& \begin{aligned}\therefore\left[\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}\right]=\mathrm{HNO}_{3}=10^{-2} \mathrm{M} \text { (given) } \\\mathrm{pH}=-\log \left[\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}\right]=-\log 10^{-2} \\\quad=-(-2 \log 10)=2\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: pH of Strong Acid and Strong Base

question: The $\mathrm{pH}$ of a solution prepared by mixing $2.0 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $\mathrm{HCl}$ solution of $\mathrm{pH} 3.0$ and $3.0 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $\mathrm{NaOH}$ of $\mathrm{pH}   10.0$ is
explanation:
$\because \mathrm{pH}$ of $\mathrm{HCl}$ solution $=3.0$
$\therefore\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]$in $\mathrm{HCl}$ solution $=1 \times 10^{-3}$
$\because \mathrm{pH}$ of $\mathrm{NaOH}$ solution $=10.0$
$\therefore\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]$in $\mathrm{NaOH}$ solution $=1 \times 10^{-10}$
$\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]$in $\mathrm{NaOH}$ solution$=\dfrac{1 \times 10^{-14}}{1 \times 10^{-10}}=10^{-4}$
Milliequivalents of $\mathrm{HCl}$
$=\mathrm{N}_{1} \mathrm{~V}_{1}=2.0 \times 1 \times 10^{-3}=2.0 \times 10^{-3}$
Milliequivalents of $\mathrm{NaOH}$
$=3.0 \times 1 \times 10^{-4}=3.0 \times 10^{-4}$
Since, milliequivalents of $\mathrm{HCl}$ are in excess, the milliequivalents of $\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]$in mixture
$\begin{aligned}& =\left(2.0 \times 10^{-3}\right)-\left(3.0 \times 10^{-4}\right) \\& =1.7 \times 10^{-3}\end{aligned}$
Concentration of in $\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]$i mixture
$\begin{aligned}&=\dfrac{1.7 \times 10^{-3}}{3+2}=3.4 \times 10^{-4} \\& \mathrm{pH} \text { of mixture }=-\log \left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right] \\&=-\log \left(3.4 \times 10^{-4}\right) \\&=3.46 \sim 3.5\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: pH of Mixture of Acids and/or Bases

question: The $\mathrm{pH}$ of a solution obtained be mixing equal volume of  $\frac{{\text{N}}}{{{\text{10}}}}{\text{ NaOH}}\,\,{\text{and }}\frac{{\text{N}}}{{20}}{\text{HCl}}$ is
explanation:
Let the volume of $\mathrm{NaOH}$ used
V= Volume of $\mathrm{HCl}$ used
Concentration in the mixture $\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]$
$ = \frac{{0.1{\rm{V}} - 0.05{\rm{V}}}}{{{\rm{(V}}\,{\rm{ + }}\,{\rm{V)}}}}$
[ $\therefore$ milliequivalent of is in excess]
$=\dfrac{0.05}{2}=0.025$
$\mathrm{pOH}=-\log \left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]$
$\begin{gathered}=-\log (0.025)=1.602 \\\mathrm{pH}=14-1.602=12.4\end{gathered}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: pH of Mixture of Acids and/or Bases

question: pH of solution of a strong acid is 5.0. What will be the 1 $\mathrm{pH}$ of solution obtained after diluting the given solution to 100 times?
explanation:
Given, $\mathrm{pH}=5$
i.e. $\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=10^{-5} \mathrm{mol\,L}^{-1}$
On diluting the solution to 100 times.$\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=\dfrac{10^{-5}}{100}=10^{-7} \mathrm{~mol} \,\mathrm{L}^{-1}$
Total $\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]$concentration $+\mathrm{H}^{+}$water ions from acid$+\mathrm{H}^{+}$ions from water.
$\begin{aligned}& {\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=10^{-7}+10^{-7}=2 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{M}} \\& \therefore \mathrm{pH}=-\log \left[2 \times 10^{-7}\right] \\& \mathrm{pH}=7-0.3010=6.699 \approx 6.7\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between pH and pOH and Calculations Based on pH and pOH

question: How many moles of NaOH must be removed from one litre of aqueous solution to change its pH from 12 to 11 ?
explanation:
Given $\mathrm{pH}=12$, so $\mathrm{pOH}_{1}=2$; $\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]_{1}=10^{-2}$
Next $\mathrm{pH}=11$; so $\mathrm{pOH}_{2}=3 ;\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]_{2}=10^{-3}$
Volume of solution is 1 litre
So, ${\left( {{{\text{n}}_{{\text{O}}{{\text{H}}^ - }}}} \right)_1} = {10^{ - 2}}{\text{and }}{\left( {{{\text{n}}_{{\text{O}}{{\text{H}}^ - }}}} \right)_2} = {10^{ - 3}}$
Difference of moles $=10^{-2}-10^{-3}$$=0.01-0.001=0.009$
so, 0.009 moles of NaOH should be removed.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between pH and pOH and Calculations Based on pH and pOH

question: How many litres of water must be added to 1 litre on an aqueous solution of HCl with a pH of 1 to create an aqueous solution with pH of 2 ?
explanation:
$(\mathrm{pH})_{1}=1 \Rightarrow\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]_{1}=10^{-1}$
$(\mathrm{pH})_{2}=2 \Rightarrow\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]_{2}=10^{-2}$
Dilution law,
${{\rm{M}}_1}\,{{\rm{V}}_{\rm{1}}} = {{\rm{M}}_{\rm{2}}}\,{{\rm{V}}_{\rm{2}}}$
${10^{ - 1}} \times 1 = {10^{ - 2}} \times {{\rm{V}}_{\rm{2}}}\,$
${{\rm{V}}_2} = 10\,{\rm{L}}$
Volume of water to be added
$=\mathrm{V}_{2}-\mathrm{V}_{1}=10^{-1}=9 \mathrm{~L}$
(on for every ten times dilution pH of strong acid increases by 1 unit).
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between pH and pOH and Calculations Based on pH and pOH

question: HCOOH and $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$ solutions have equal pH . If $\mathrm{K}_{1} / \mathrm{K}_{2}$ is 4 , their molar concentration ratio will be
explanation:
$\mathrm{pH}=-\log \left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]$
and $\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=\mathrm{C} \alpha=\sqrt{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{C}}$
Given pH of two acids as same, so
$\sqrt{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{1}} \mathrm{C}_{1}}=\sqrt{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{2}} \mathrm{C}_{2}}$
$\dfrac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{1}}}{\mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{a}_{2}}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{C}_{2}}{\mathrm{C}_{1}}$
$4=\dfrac{\mathrm{C}_{2}}{\mathrm{C}_{1}}$
or $\dfrac{\mathrm{C}_{1}}{\mathrm{C}_{2}}=\dfrac{1}{4}=0.25$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: pH of Mixture of Acids and/or Bases

question:
Which of the following statement(s) is/are correct?
(A) The pH of $1 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{MHCl}$ solution is 8 .
(B) The conjugate base of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{PO}_{4}^{-}$ is $\mathrm{HPO}_{4}^{-2}$.
(C) $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{w}}$ increases with increase in temperature.
(D) When solution of a weak monoprotic acid is titrated against a strong base at half neutralisation point, $\mathrm{pH}=\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}$.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
explanation:
(A) pH of $1 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{MHCl}$ solution is 6.96 .
Acid can never have pH more than 7 at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
(B) Conjugate base of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{PO}_{4}^{-}$ is $\mathrm{HPO}_{4}{ }^{2-}$.
(C) Degree of ionisation increases with temperature, thus ionic product of water $\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{w}}\right)$ also increases.
(D) At half neutralisation point, $\mathrm{pH}=\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: pH of Weak Acid and Weak Base

question:
Assertion: $\mathrm{pH}$ of $10\,{\text{M HCl}}$ aqueous solution is less than 1 .

Reason: $\mathrm{pH}$ is equal to negative logarithm of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$concentration.
explanation:
Theoretically, the $\mathrm{pH}$ should can be negative.
$\mathrm{pH}=-\log \left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=-\log 10^{1}=-1$
So option (1) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: $\operatorname{At pH}=7,\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=10^{-7}$.

Reason: $\mathrm{pH}=7$ signifies pure water.
explanation: $\mathrm{pH}=7$ signifies neutral solution and pure water has neutral $\mathrm{pH}$ of 7. So option (3) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question: $\mathrm{pH}$ of a sample of $\mathrm{KOH}$ and another sample of $\mathrm{NaOH}$ are 10 and 12 respectively. Their normalities are related as $\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{NaOH}}=\mathrm{xN}_{\mathrm{KOH}}$. What is the value of ${\text{x}}$ ?
explanation:
$\mathrm{pH}$ of $\mathrm{KOH}=10 ; \mathrm{pH}$ of $\mathrm{NaOH}=12$
So, $\mathrm{pOH}$ of $\mathrm{KOH}=14-10=4$;
$\mathrm{pOH}$ of $\mathrm{NaOH}=14-12=2$.
$\mathrm{N}_{\text {кон }}=\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=10^{-4}$;
$\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{NaOH}}=10^{-2}$ (Because, for strong base,
$\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=$normality $\left.=\mathrm{pOH}\right)$
As per the question,
$10^{-2}=\mathrm{x} \times 10^{-4}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\dfrac{10^{-2}}{10^{-4}}=10^{2}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: pH of Strong Acid and Strong Base

question: How many $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ions are present in $10 \mathrm{~mL}$ of a solution having $\mathrm{pH}=10$ ?
explanation:
$\mathrm{pH}=10 \Rightarrow\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=10^{-10} \mathrm{molL}^{-1}$
No. of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$in $10 \mathrm{~mL}$
$=\dfrac{10}{1000} \times 6.02 \times 10^{23} \times 10^{-10}=6.02 \times 10^{11}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between pH and pOH and Calculations Based on pH and pOH

question: If $\mathrm{pH}$ of $\mathrm{NaOH}$ solution is 12.0 . The $\mathrm{pH}$ of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ solution of same molarity will be
explanation:
${\text{NaOH pH}} = 12,\,\,\therefore {\text{pOH}} = 2$
$\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=10^{-2}$
Molarity of $\mathrm{NaOH}=10^{-2}$
For $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$, molarity $=10^{-2}$ and
Normality $=2 \times 10^{-2}$
$\therefore\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=2 \times 10^{-2}$
$\mathrm{pH}=2-\log 2=1.7$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: pH of Strong Acid and Strong Base

question: A weak mono-basic acid solution has a molarity of $0.005 \mathrm{M}$ and $\mathrm{pH}$ of 5 . What is the percentage ionisation of acid in this solution?
explanation:
$\mathrm{C} \alpha=\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right] ; \mathrm{pH}=5$ means $\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=10^{-5}$
$\alpha=\dfrac{10^{-5}}{5 \times 10^{-3}}=\dfrac{1}{5} \times 10^{-2}$
$\%$ ionisation $=\dfrac{1}{5} \times 10^{-2} \times 100=0.2 \%$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: pH of Weak Acid and Weak Base

question: What is the $\mathrm{pH}$ of millimolar solution of $\mathrm{NaOH}$ ?
explanation:
One millimolar solution of $\mathrm{NaOH}=1 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{L}$ of $\mathrm{NaOH}$
$\therefore\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=1 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mole} / \mathrm{L}$
$\therefore \mathrm{pOH}=-\log \left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=-\log \left(1 \times 10^{-3}\right)=3$
$\therefore \mathrm{pH}=14-\mathrm{pOH}=14-3=11$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between pH and pOH and Calculations Based on pH and pOH

question: What is the $\mathrm{pH}$ of  $0.005\,\,{\text{M}}\,\,{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_4}$  solution?
explanation:
$\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ is dibasic acid, so $0.005 \mathrm{M}=0.01 \mathrm{~N}$
$\begin{gathered}=10^{-2} \mathrm{~N} \\\mathrm{pH}=-\log _{10}\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right] \\=-\log _{10}\left[10^{-2}\right]=2.0\end{gathered}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between pH and pOH and Calculations Based on pH and pOH

question: $\mathrm{pH}$ of soft drink is 3.6. Calculate the concentration of hydrogen ions in it.
explanation:
$\mathrm{pH}=3.6,\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=$?
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{pH}=-\log _{10}\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right] \\& \therefore \log _{10}\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=-\mathrm{pH} \\& =-3.6 \\& =-3-0.6-1+1 \\& =-4+0.4=\overline{4} .4\end{aligned}$
$\therefore\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=\operatorname{Antilog}(\overline{4} .4)=2.512 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{M}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between pH and pOH and Calculations Based on pH and pOH

question: The $\mathrm{pH}$ of $0.005\,\,{\text{M}}\,\,{\text{KOH}}$ is 9.95 . Calculate the $\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]$?
explanation:
$\mathrm{pH}=9.95, \mathrm{c}=0.05 \mathrm{M}$
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{pOH}=14-\mathrm{pH}=14-9.95=4.05 \\& \mathrm{pOH}=-\log _{10}\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right] \\& \log _{10}\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=-4.05 \\& =-4-0.05-1+1 \\& =-5+0.95=\overline{5} .95 \\& {\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=\operatorname{antilog} \overline{5} .95} \\& =8.913 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{M}\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: pH of Strong Acid and Strong Base

question: What is the  ${\rm{pH}}\,\,{\rm{of}}\,\,{\rm{0}}{\rm{.02}}\,{\rm{M}}\,\,{\rm{NaOH}}$  solution?
explanation:
$\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=2 \times 10^{-2}$
$\mathrm{P}^{\mathrm{OH}}=-\log _{10}\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]$
$=-\log 2 \times 10^{-2}$
$=2-\log 2$
$=2-0.3$
$\mathrm{P}^{\mathrm{OH}}=1.7$
and $\mathrm{P}^{\mathrm{H}}=14-\mathrm{P}^{\mathrm{OH}}=14-1.7$
$\mathrm{P}^{\mathrm{H}}=12.3$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: pH of Strong Acid and Strong Base

question: The $\mathrm{pH}$ value of $\dfrac{\mathrm{N}}{10} \mathrm{NaOH}$ is
explanation:
$0.1 {\text{N NaOH}}$ is given,
So, $\mathrm{pOH}=-\log \left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]$
$=-\log 0.1=1$
$\therefore \mathrm{pH}=14-\mathrm{pOH}=14-1=13$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between pH and pOH and Calculations Based on pH and pOH

question: The number of hydroxyl ions in $100 \mathrm{~mL}$ of a solution having ${\text{pH  =  }}10$ is
explanation:
Given, $\mathrm{pH}=10$
$\therefore\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=1 \times 10^{-\mathrm{pH}}=1 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{L}$
$\therefore\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=\dfrac{1 \times 10^{-14}}{1 \times 10^{-10}}=1 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{L}$
$\because$ The number of hydroxyl ions present in 1 mole $=6.023 \times 10^{23}$
$\therefore$ The number of hydroxyl ion in $1 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{L}$
$=6.023 \times 10^{23} \times 10^{-4} / \mathrm{L}$
$=6.023 \times 10^{19} \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{L}$
$\because$ In $1000 \mathrm{~mL}$, number of hydroxyl ion
$=6.023 \times 10^{19}$
$\therefore$ In $100 \mathrm{~mL}$, number of hydroxyl ions
$=\dfrac{6.023 \times 10^{19} \times 100}{1000}=6.02 \times 10^{18}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between pH and pOH and Calculations Based on pH and pOH

question: If the dissociation constant of $5 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{M}$ aqueous solution of diethylamine is $2.5 \times 10^{-5}$,  its $\mathrm{pH}$ value is
explanation:
Diethylamine is a weak base and from Ostwald's dilution law, for weak base,
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}=\mathrm{C} \alpha^{2}$
$\therefore \alpha=\sqrt{\dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{-5}}{5 \times 10^{-4}}}=0.22$$\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=\mathrm{C} \alpha=5 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.22$
$=1.1 \times 10^{-4}$
$\therefore \mathrm{pOH}=-\log \left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]$
$=-\log \left(1.1 \times 10^{-4}\right)=3.96$
$\therefore \mathrm{pH}=14-3.96=10.04$

subtopic: pH of Weak Acid and Weak Base

question: When $10 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $0.1 \mathrm{M}$ acetic acid $\left(\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}=5.0\right)$ is titrated against $10 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $0.1 \mathrm{M}$ ammonia solution $\left(\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{b}}=5.0\right)$, the equivalence point occurs at $\mathrm{pH}$.
explanation:
$\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}=-\log \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}$
and $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{b}}=-\log \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}$
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{pH}=-\dfrac{1}{2}\left[\log \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}+\log \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{w}}-\log \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}\right] \\& =-\dfrac{1}{2}\left[-5+\log 10^{-14}-(-5)\right] \\& =-\dfrac{1}{2}[-5-14+5]=7\end{aligned}$

explanation:
${\text{pH = 3 means 1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 3}}}}{\text{ M}}$ and ${\text{pH = 4 means 1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 4}}}}{\text{ M}}$.
${\text{100}}\,{\text{mL }}of{\text{ 1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 3}}}}{\text{ M}}$ solution contain
$\mathrm {=\dfrac{10^{-3} \times 100}{1000}=10 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~mol}}$

question:
The ionisation of hydrogen sulphide in water is low in the presence of hydrochloric acid.
Hydrogen sulphide is a weak acid.
explanation:
${{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{S}}$ is a weak acid and also a weak electrolyte and its ionisation is suppressed, when small amount of strong electrolyte like $\mathrm {\mathrm{HCl}}$ is added due to common ion effect.
$\rm {\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{S}^{2-}}$
$\rm {\mathrm{HCl} \rightarrow \underset{\substack{\text { Common } \\ \text { ion }}}{\mathrm{H}^{+}}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}}$
So, the option (3) is correct.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

question: What is the ${\text{pH}}{\mkern 1mu} $ of a ${\text{1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 4}}}}{\text{ M}}$  ${\text{O}}{{\text{H}}^{\text{ - }}}$ solution at $330 \mathrm{~K}$, if ${{\text{K}}_{\text{w}}}$  at $330 \mathrm{~K}$ is $10^{-13.6}$ ?
explanation:
Given at $\mathrm {330 \mathrm{~K}}$,
${{\text{K}}_{\text{w}}}{\text{ = 1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 13}}{\text{.6}}}}$
${\text{i}}{\text{.e}}{\text{.,}}\quad {\text{p}}{{\text{K}}_{\text{w}}}{\text{ = pH}}\,{\text{ + }}\,{\text{pOH}}$
$\because {\text{pOH}}\,{\text{ = }}\,{\text{ - log}}\left[ {{\text{O}}{{\text{H}}^{\text{ - }}}} \right]$
$13.6 = {\text{pH}}\,{\text{ + }}\,{\text{pOH}}$
$ = - \log {10^{ - 4}}$
$ = {\text{pH}} + 4$
$ = 4$
$\therefore {\text{pH}} = 13.6 - 4 = 9.6$

question: Calculate the $\mathrm{pH}$ of a $0.10\,\,{\text{M}}$ ammonia solution. The dissociation constant of ammonia, ${{\rm{K}}_{\rm{b}}}{\rm{ = 1}}{\rm{.77 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{ - 5}}}}$
explanation:
$\mathrm {\mathrm{NH}_{3}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}+\mathrm{OH}^{-}}$
$\rm {K_{b}=\dfrac{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]}=1.77 \times 10^{-5}}$
Before neutralisation, $\mathrm {\left[\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}\right]=\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=x}$
$\mathrm {\left[N H_{3}\right]=0.10-x \simeq 0.10}$
$\mathrm {x^{2} / 0.10=1.77 \times 10^{-5}}$
Thus, $\mathrm {x=1.33 \times 10^{-3}=\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]}$
Therefore, $\mathrm {\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]}$
$\mathrm {\left[H^{+}\right]=\dfrac{K_{w}}{\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]}}$
$\rm {=\dfrac{10^{-14}}{\left(1.33 \times 10^{-3}\right)}=7.5 \times 10^{-12}}$
$\mathrm {p H=-\log \left(7.5 \times 10^{-12}\right)=11.12}$
Alternately,
$\rm { {\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=\sqrt{\mathrm{K}_{b} \times \mathrm{C}}} \\ {\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=1.33 \times 10^{-3}} \\ \mathrm{pOH}=2.88 \\ \mathrm{pH}=11.12 .}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: pH of Weak Acid and Weak Base

question: Calculate the percent dissociation of $\mathrm{HOCl}$ of $0.08 \mathrm{M}$ solution of hypochlorous acid. The ionisation constant of the acid is $2.5 \times 10^{-5}$.
explanation:
$\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {}&{{\text{HOC}}{{\text{l}}_{{\text{(aq)}}}}{\text{ + }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}{\mkern 1mu} \rightleftharpoons {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{O}}_{\left( {{\text{aq}}} \right)}^{\text{ + }}{\text{ + ClO}}_{\left( {{\text{aq}}} \right)}^{\text{ - }}} \\ {{\text{Initial}}}&{{\text{0}}{\text{.08}}{\mkern 1mu} {\text{M}}\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\text{0}}\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\text{0}}} \\ {{\text{At }}}&{} \\ {{\text{Equilibrium }}}&{{\text{0}}{\text{.08 - x}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\text{x}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\text{x}}} \\ {\text{ }}&{} \end{array}$
$\rm {K_{a} =\dfrac{\left[\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{ClO}^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{HOCl}]}}$
$=\dfrac{\rm{x}^{2}}{(0.08-\rm{x})}$
As $\mathrm {x < < 0.08}$, therefore $\mathrm {0.08-x \simeq 0.08}$
$\rm {\dfrac{x^{2}}{0.08}=2.5 \times 10^{-5}}$
$\mathrm {\mathrm{x}^{2}=2.0 \times 10^{-6}}$, thus, $\mathrm {\mathrm{x}=1.41 \times 10^{-3}}$
$\mathrm {\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=1.41 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M}}$.
Therefore, Percent dissociation
$\mathrm {=\left[[\mathrm{HOCl}]_{\text {dissociated }} /[\mathrm{HOCl}]_{\text {initial }}\right] \times 100}$
$\rm {=\dfrac{1.41 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{2}}{0.08}=1.76 \%}$
Alternately,
$\rm {K_{a}=C \alpha^{2} \Rightarrow \alpha=\sqrt{\dfrac{K_{a}}{C}} .}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: pH of Weak Acid and Weak Base

question: Assuming complete dissociation, calculate the $\mathrm{pH}$ of  ${\text{0}}{\text{.003}}$ ${\text{M}}$ ${\text{HCl}}$ solution

$\rm { \mathrm{pH}=-\log \left[\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}\right] \\ \mathrm{pH}=-\log [0.003]=-\log \left[3.0 \times 10^{-3}\right] \\ \mathrm{pH}=3+(-0.4771)=2.52 .}$
